title: Braised Chicken With Rosemary and Crispy Artichokes Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I'm off to the grocery store to get fixins for this but I don't expect to find baby artichokes. Halved  or quartered brussel sprouts will do nicely. This recipe seems like the sort that gets better the next day. I would crisp the brussel sprouts just before serving because I find brussel sprouts can get a little stinky if reheated.

Am I being dense in wondering why both a skillet and a roasting pan are needed? Could I do this in a dutch oven by making the sauce, then adding the chicken in and then roasting? I guess less exposed chicken to get browned if slightly more submerged?
@Carley that’s what I did, used an oven safe deep skillet for the sauce and laid the chicken on top.
I searched high and low in the produce capital of California, Berkeley, for baby artichokes to no avail so I used mushrooms and they were a very good alternative.
@Vickie. I got some from Monterey Market
You will not notice a difference between jarred artichokes and the baby artichokes listed in the recipe after braising for an hour and a half.
@NycGal You don't braise the artichokes for an hour and a half. You saute until crisp separately.
I’ll make these with both thigh and leg attached using four rather than six. I’ll also stuff the baby artichokes with mint, parley, breadcrumbs, lemon juice and a touch of EVOO to moisten.

I halved this recipe, using chicken thighs cooked in a cast-iron Dutch oven. In place of fresh baby artichokes, I used frozen artichoke hearts from Trader Joe's. Otherwise I followed the recipe as written, and it came out perfectly. It was delicious and smelled divine! I think the crispy, chewy texture of the fried artichokes definitely added to the dish; next time I might try cooking them in my air fryer. The herb/capers/lemon zest topping was also great!

I made this with chicken thighs rather than quarters, which I find awkward to eat. The pan juices did NOT "reduce and thicken to a dark gravy-like glaze", as mentioned int the accompanying article which disappointed me. I mean, the sauce was nice but not noteworthy. Don't skip the gremolata because it added  lot of flavour. Did not prepare the artichokes. Instead, I served the chicken on a bed of roasted green beans and carrots and served with Persian jeweled rice. Lovely meal.

But baby artichokes are wonderful braised … why would you prepare them separately? Brown them if you want, with or after the chicken, but then braise them in the same pot. I truly don't understand the reason for preparing them on their own.

I'm in rural Central NY with limited grocery options within 50 miles. Would canned artichokes work? I'm pretty sure frozen baby artichokes aren't available locally either. (If only I had a greenhouse where I could grow artichokes!)
@E., Coop.buy them on Goldbelly fresh
I would use the frozen (no way am I turning artichokes) but try the can if you can't get them. I would not used marinated. A previous commenter mentioned subbing brussels

We've grown artichokes in Arizona, and they are plentiful fresh out West.   However, I took one look at this recipe and made a note to head to Trader Joe's for a bag of frozen artichokes.   They are just easy to deal with, and I've had luck with that brand.

Couldn’t you just add the artichokes to the pan with the chicken, maybe half way through cooking, and just let them oven roast?  Especially if you’re using canned or frozen, which both profit from drying out anyway.
I add Costco marinated artichoke hearts to the roasting pan midway through.  Not true to the recipe but delightful and easy!

Delicious, but made some modifications. I cooked it all in a large, round Dutch oven, with somewhat lower sides.  Only one leg/thigh didn't brown much.  Total cooking time about 1.5 hrs. Reduced gravy on top of stove.  Was too astringent, so added a touch of butter, small amound of (nondairy) creamer, also olives. Used packets of marinated olves from TJ marinated but not too briney and mushroom slices sauces. Not too hard and worth it.

A lot of work for an ordinary dish. My guests gave two stars each. It’s an unusual combination. 
My preference: serve the artichokes separately with pasta. Delicious!
Serve the chicken thighs and sauce au vin blanc with rice at an other occasion. Also very delicious. Polenta I’d prefer with any preparation of tomatoes etc.
This was a fantastic dish. Used frozen artichoke bottoms and added them to the braise. Sauce was a bit thin, so added a beurre manie at the end. Delish!
Won’t the roasted chicken be cool before adding the artichokes.perhaps the finished dish should go back into the oven.
The artichokes are made as the chicken is roasting - the word "Meanwhile" in Step 5 is the key.
Sacrilegious question: Can canned or frozen artichoke hearts be substituted for the baby artichokes?  Specifically, will they hold up to sauteing?
@Roald canned are terrible because they’re in a citric acid brine plus they’re too wet to fry
If preparing one day in advance, do you stop at Step 5 - prepping the artichokes?  Seems like the chokes are supposed to be crisp. Not sure that would be true the next day?
In NC all my life. I have never seen baby artichokes

I'm new to this so please bear with me. I'm looking for help along my cooking journey.  I dont see how 6 large legs will fit.  I counted the legs in the picture and came up with 5. what's the solution
We liked it. Next time I’d add more rosemary for cooking and more capers to finish
Great recipe but way too much liquid. You calm halve it. I had to do a major reduction.

I used frozen artichoke bottoms from Trader Joe's.  Despite including many of my favorite things as ingredients, i thought this recipe was just OK.  I really wanted it to be great, but I think it might be too much work for too little enjoyment.

I used sunchokes bc that’s what we had which maybe required cooking for longer, added mushrooms (also had ‘em lying about) and it was truly delicious. Served over wild rice. Worth the effort. Would definitely make again.
If I make this again, I would reduce the number of pans to wash by making the braising sauce in my Dutch oven, then nestle the chicken into the sauce.

I didn't have white wine on hand, but had an open bottle of pinot noir and used that instead.  Excellent sauce. Substituted marinated in oil artichoke hearts and the whole dish was delicious, especially over polenta.

I got baby artichokes through Gold Belly. Very expensive but the minimum order is enough to make this dish several times, but be aware that cleaning up the artichokes requires some more time than the recipe calls for.

This recipe is made for the likes of Julia Child. Half the instructions are missing.  I'm very disappointed.  I am a beginner cook and this takes alot for granted that you expect everyone just to know that is not stated in the directions. Needless to say it came out badly. I was so looking forward to it as it really sounded delicious.

title: Ceci Bean Soup With Red Pepper Pesto Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody:
I haven’t made this soup yet so can’t comment on it, but wanted to clarify for readers unfamiliar with chickpeas that they’re commonly known by various names in different cultures and stores. 

For example, in Spanish-speaking cultures they’re called “garbanzo beans”, and in Italian and Sicilian-speaking cultures they’re known as “ceci beans” (as in this recipe).  Other cultures have their own names, especially depending upon the type of chickpea.
@D. Is anyone really unfamiliar with chickpeas and their many aliases at this point?
@D. Thanks for the explanation! Unlike Cici Pedantson (bless her heart) I was not familiar with the name ceci beans. All my Italian cookbooks use the name garbanzo beans.

Definitely needs more water, at least 2 cups more, so start with 10 cups. Had I cup of broth left when drained ceci before simmering 10 minutes with carrots and onion which absorbed that cup. pureed with immersion blender and it is hummus consistency! don't want to add plain broth now...so add more water at the start!
I had very little liquid remaining after draining chickpeas in step 3.Presume I will need to add more for step 5 or essentially I will have hummus.

This soup was quite delicious! I did make a number of adaptations because it was rather late when I started making it and I wanted to use what I had. To start I only used one can of beans because I am only one person also read through the comments on liquid and went with 2 cups broth, 4 cups water and that yielded a good consistency.  Also used garlic powder and minced onion for the first part. 

For the carrots and onions, I used onions. I pre-chopped and keep in the freezer did not thaw them just threw them right in. Last I did not have a pepper so I didn’t even make that part and it was quite fine without. Considering adding some chili crunch to spice it up or possibly a tomato pesto that I keep on hand at all times.
Can this be made with canned chickpeas? I understand there won't be soaking in that case. Would anything else be different?
You wouldn't have the liquid that it says to reserve from cooking the dried chickpeas.  Could probably just use broth instead, though.
Yes, play it by ear.   Reserve liquid from chickpeas can.   Augment with broth if needed.   Adjust.
Has anyone tried to make this with canned ceci beans?
Yes.  Result is ok but not as flavorful. Tinker more with seasoning and it's fine.  I combine mine with chicken soup, which works for me. But next time will start with dried beans.

I soaked a little over 1 cup of chickpeas for 24 hours and used 4C of water in Step 1 and had the same consistency issue after blending as the earlier commenters. I probably had 2/3C of broth after cooking. It also had the same color as hummus, and is not as bright as the photo above.
I wonder if the soup would be more elegant if the coating of the chickpeas were removed after cooking?
One of my favorite restaurants in SF makes this but adds pasta. Delicious!  Despite the tan on tan look.
What changes if you use canned chickpeas?

The chickpea preparation here is wonderful!!! I've routinely soaked and simmered all of my dry beans since I prefer their texture to canned. The added aromatics, olive oil, and salt gave wonderful flavor to the chickpeas and provided a lovely vegetable broth... more ideas for future dried and soaked beans of all sorts with aromatics adjusted to recipes.

I've made this soup twice now--the first time with "fresh" chickpeas (as per recipe) and the other day with a can.  Next time definitely with "fresh," which are more flavorful.  I used half to make the soup (helped by NYT scaling advice) and the other half to make fresh hummos, which was the best I've ever made. For soup added tsp fish sauce and a little mild curry. Today's lunch, combined this with my chicken soup, leftover couscous and crispy cauliflower. Love this soup.  What a surprise.
Add taro root,beet, sweet potato chips you the top. Serve with add in spicey yummy stuff. It kickcs. It up in color style texture

I really liked this soup and followed the comments about increasing the water. I used about twelve cups and the color came out close to the photo. Using dried beans reminded me how much tastier, if less convenient, they are than canned. 

The beans take quite a while to cook, and I didn’t really understand the point of waiting until they were done and draining them and reserving the liquid so you could cook the onion and carrots in the same pot and then adding the beans and reserved broth back in. So while the beans were cooking I sautéed the veggies in a separate pan. Once the beans were done I fished out the aromatics and just added the veggies to the pot. It seemed quicker, easier and delicious, but maybe I’m missing an important point. 

I used the immersion blender and puréed about half the beans and veggies to be creamy but still retain texture. As I am not a big fan of cayenne pepper, I substituted garlic in the pesto and it came out great. I tripled the peppers and added three garlic cloves so there would be plenty for the leftover soup.
I would be very curious to hear from David Tanis regarding the liquid situation in this recipe. I added about a cup and still had a soup that was a bit unpleasantly thick.

I stumbled onto this recipe while searching what to do with my pot of Ranch Gordo zipper cream peas. I’m going for it! I haven’t looked this up yet but just to muck the water, are cowpeas related to chickpeas?

title: Blood Orange Gelato Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: Is there a work around if you don't have an ice cream maker?  Thanks.

@Jen I lived in Afghanistan for several years working for the UN and once when we were on the road we stopped at an ice cream shop where they made Mango ice cream by hand. They had a wide shallow-ish copper bowl which they set in a big pan of ice and then stirred their mango cream mixture continuously until it set up nicely. It was really cool to watch and fresh to eat! It looked like it took a bit of elbow grease, but I think a thin metal pan set in sink with ice could work!

@Jen I wonder the same thing…also wondering if substituting some yogurt for the cornstarch and some of the half and half would be a good idea. I used to have a ice cream maker, it was noisy and took a long time and I think whisking the heck out of the ingredients and adding yogurt would make something quite tasty, along with the ice bath idea!

Does anyone else find it super frustrating to look at the reviews/comments and find that almost no one has actually made the recipe?  I don’t mean to sound harsh, but maybe folks can find another forum for reminiscing, random comments or speculation about how the recipe might work?  I’d like to read reviews that actually evaluate the recipe, and find it tiresome to sort through all the random notes.

Anyway, I DID make the recipe exactly as written, in an ice cream maker, and it turned out delicious.  It was also quite easy.  I didn’t have any problem with the addition of corn starch (contrary to one commenter’s notes, the amount IS specified as 2 TBSP, at least in the recipe that I am reading).  I used blood orange juice that I squeezed (to my surprise, I actually did need five of the oranges), as well as the zest of the blood oranges/lemon and Cointreau.  The blood orange flavor was pronounced.  I would make this again (I might try it with another citrus, although the blood oranges do have a unique taste and give it a beautiful pink tint).
@RB thank you. I am with you all the way!
Isn't this sorbet? there are no eggs or dairy to make it "gelato"
@Caryn, there are 2 cups of dairy (half and half).
@Caryn Half & half is dairy.
@Caryn it has two cups of 1/2 and 1/2
Of course its gelato.  Its made with half and half.
Half & half is dairy—first ingredient in list.
Replying to Caro, half-and-half is actually a dairy product. It is comprised of milk and cream.

Made it according to directions and DID NOT LIKE IT. The texture was chalky, due to the cornstarch, I'm guessing. Or maybe because I'm used to making ice cream and the creaminess that the eggs lend. But I won't make this again and will find something better to make with blood oranges. Maybe a sorbet?
This reads pretty yummy.  Is there a substitute for an orange alcohol liquor.  Something with no alcohol?  Thanks

@Dave You might try orange oil, which is more potent flavor-wise than orange extract. I would start with 1 tsp. Just be aware it will help amp up the orange flavor but will not keep the mixture from becoming icy, which is what alcohol accomplishes.
@Dave - 2 cups of half and half is dairy.

The alcohol is essential to prevent this from becoming a solid block of (delicious, but unscoopable) ice in the freezer. In a home kitchen, the only other good way to preserve scoopability is to increase the sugar (increasing the fat will also help), which would make this a much sweeter (and richer) dessert. *However*, you could omit the alcohol if you plan to consume this within a few hours of making it, before it freezes solid, though the texture will be a bit icier than it would otherwise.

I don’t recommend this recipe. In retrospect, 4 tsp of zest + 3Tbs of Grand Marnier + 1 cup of pretty tart juice with only 1/2 cup of sugar was way off in terms of hitting the desirable sweet-tart note. 
Also, I mixed the corn starch with about 3 tbs water, amount of water was unspecified, and got tiny lumps when I mixed it into the other liquid. Please specify amount of water and the temperature to cook it to after adding. 

I suggest significantly reducing the amount of zest.
The problem with homemade ice cream or gelato is it gets chalky quickly in a day or two.  There are stabilizers you can easily purchase and add to  the start of churning to maintain creaminess l
What is half and half?
Has anyone made this with coconut cream for non-dairy?

@Karen A, I made it with coconut milk and it’s delicious. Didn’t use coconut cream because I didn’t have that on hand, but I’m sure it would be even richer. I decreased sugar to 1/3 cup since coconut has natural sweetness, and it seems just right to me. Also I used Triple Sec instead of Grand Marnier.
The cornstarch is to keep it creamy not icy. It functions as a stabilizer.
You can buy non dairy half and half.
Holy smokes this is delish!!

@Jen I got a Cuisinart small frozen yogurt maker (I believe it was called) which works great for sherbet /sorbets, although I haven't made this one yet. About $50. You must store the container in the freezer.

Hmmm, I made this recipe and the flavor was great, but the texture was nothing like any gelato I’ve ever eaten. It was not creamy at all; actually a bit crumbly. I make ice cream regularly and never have experienced such an odd texture. But hen I’ve never used cornstarch rather than eggs. I won’t be doing it again.

title: Glazed Pork Tenderloin With Gingery Carrots Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: What can I sub for mirin? Would love to make this tonight without going to the store . . .
@Hollyn, instead of mirin, you can use a bit of mildly sweet sherry, or sweeten some sake or some rice vinegar by adding 1/2 teaspoon of sugar to each tablespoon of either.

In my house 2.5 lb of tenderloin would be 8 to 10 servings, but 1 lb of carrots would barely be enough for 3.

So I would greatly increase the quantities of carrots, maybe 3 lbs for that much meat. And triple all the associated ingredients as well.

Having said that, in addition to the flavors, I also love the color of the meal, with the bright carrots, and the spinach salad.
130 was too low a target for the internal temp; the pork came out bloody even after resting. Next time I will cook it to 145 internal before removing.
Am I removing the pork from the marinade to another baking dish, or put the whole thing from the fridge in the oven?

@Pat
I put the whole thing in the oven after bringing it to room temperature. I also cooked it an extra 10 minutes and only let it sit 5 minutes while I reduced the marinade to sauce in a saucepan. It was delicious.

I share the comment below on the size of the meat and reduced it by half but the carrot serving was fine.  I would also say that this marinade/sauce is a real winner.  I doubled it and put some in the refrigerator with another meal.  I think it would be great brushed on chicken or even just reduced a bit and put on salmon before serving.
I have never successfully simmered away 2 cups of water in 15 minutes.
Assume you need to refrigerate if marinating overnight?
Definitely. raw meat
The tenderloin is too salty. 1/4 soy sauce may be enough. Carrots a bit too tender for us, but very tasty.
This has an over night sous vide written all over it?  Vacuum pack the protein with the marinade and just let it go at 135F?  Finish either a cast iron sear.
The side would work with organic baby carrots -much less prep

@Me yes. But wish “baby” carrots were actually baby carrots.
Way too many of them are trimmed down woody carrots

The cook time for the pork was just fine for me. The sauce was delicious and we spooned it over white rice. The carrots were excellent but 2 cups water seemed like too much. It took a while to cook down. I’d probably try 1.5 cups next time.

This was really delicious.  We loved the flavors, and the pork was perfectly cooked in 30 minutes. The carrots were also great, but overcooked because the liquid did not evaporate in 15 minutes.  I had to cook them for ten more minutes, which left mushy carrots - but GOOD mushy carrots!  Next time I'll use older carrots or reduce the amount of liquid.  Also, six tablespoons of butter are too much.  4 Tbsps.  are plenty, and maybe even less.
Made as written (for once!).  Delicious, easy, and well received.
Would this approach work for beef tenderloin? How would the cooking or marinating (time and temp ) differ?

This was fantastic. It is like the perfect blend of a Chicago deep dish and a 1980s Pizza Hut Supreme Pizza. My favorite homemade pizza recipe by far.

I read a lot of the comments and have to say, this recipe works as is. Although I recommend going with a whole can of tomatoes, not just a cup, and a whole package of mozz as well (although low moisture is essential! There’s a bit of water moisture management)
Make sure you brown the sausage, not cook it through initially, as I suspect the commenter who said their sausage was “carbonized” did. 
The crust does in fact recall a Dutch baby, despite another commenter’s passionate disagreement. This is mostly due to how eggy it is, which frankly was fine with my family and my young kids who are picky. 
This pizza is best eaten with a fork and a knife. Hard for me to say as a New Yorker!

I made this with a 1.5 lb pkg of turkey tenderloins. It was easy and so tasty that a couple people who don't normally eat turkey ate large portions of it. I halved the sauce ingredients to account for a smaller weight of meat. I  skipped the gingered carrots, instead making what I had on hand, which was spaghetti squash and kale, both of which became extra tasty when some of the sauce ran onto them. I also instead baked at 350 for 1hr, because that is what is suggested on the turkey pkg.
The pork and glaze were amazing. The carrots, only meh. I’d rather just roast them—healthier, too.

I use a recipe for marinating pork loin from one of my aunts. But I buy 2 or 3 of the smaller packages of pork tenderloins at Costco, bring them home, do each package in one of 3 different marinades in ziplock type bags, leave them in frig a few hours, turning often, then label and put in another larger ziplock with air squeezed out.  Freeze and use when I want them. Has worked for me for 40+ years...
Is the pork covered or uncovered when it bakes?
@Gerry K - I had same question. i think uncovered since the next step says cover and let sit but not 100%

Delightful / delicious w accompanying salad! I made dinner for two, so halved all ingredients except for carrots, used whole bunch, abt 3/4 lb. Marinated pork in zip-lock back in fridge abt 2h and then at room temp abt 30 min before transferring to roasting pan. Changes for next time: 1) Start checking pork temp at 20 and 25 min - I tested at 30 min and was already over temp. 2) Carrots might be about perfect at 12 min - I cooked 15 min, a bit more tender, but still delicious!

I made the pork exactly as the recipe calls for. I don’t know what others have against mirin. I’ve never heard of it until now so I was happy to follow the recipe. The only changes I made was using reduced sodium tamari for soy sauce. And I used baby carrots, added honey & roasted them in the oven. It was all delicious!

Great recipe! It made too much marinade though. I doubled the carrots, halved the butter and water and it was still too much water—took too long to evaporate but delicious in the end. Served with basmati rice and it made a lovely meal.

This is for TWO tenderloins so you will have extra juice, which isn’t a bad thing, even if you’re only making one.

I used tj’s rice vinegar sans addtl sugar as there’s already sugar in the recipe and tj’s gyoza sauce for the mirin. Ran out of sesame oil and omitted the cayenne as the gyoza sauce has kick. Don’t come for me that it’s not the same recipe :D I have no business being in the kitchen anyway. And thanks to the commenters for all suggestions.

Happy cooking-so says Jacques Pépin
I haven’t cooked the pork (yet) but the gingery carrots are an instant fave and I will make them again and again.

I marinated the pork for an hour and let it come to room temperature for 30 minutes. The pork cooked perfectly and came out juicy and tender. The carrots were a perfect accompaniment to the pork and I served it along side some jasmine rice. Simple to prepare and makes for a lovely week eight meal with low pressure preparation.

The pork was still too pink after resting, so I pan seared the slices one minute per side and stirred in the sauce.  Wasn’t a big fan of the carrots. But the sauce—very teriyaki ish— was a hit and I will try it with other meats—chicken, beef, ground turkey.  It’s very sweet, so I might also try cutting the brown sugar in half.

Very disappointing recipe. Lidey Hueck recipe is vastly superior.  Flavor too sweet and cook time wildly and dangerously inaccurate.  You should bring pork to 140 degrees.  I only tried it because it didn’t require searing.
Not delicious.  Way too sweet and cook time inaccurate if you are bringing out of a cold refrigerator.  And pork needs to be cooked  to 140.  Lidey Huecks recipe is infinitely superior to this one.
I don’t understand why the instructions say that the cook time is 1 hour 50 minutes. Even if you cook the pork 30-35 minutes and THEN make the carrots, the times still don’t add up.
Marinating for the minimum time.
Will the internal temperature of the pork loin continue to rise? Everything I've read says pork tenderloin should be cooked to an internal temp of 140 to 145 for safety.
This is delicious and worthy of company. I would also increase the amount of carrots for four servings.
@me Yes.  David Tanis agrees with you.  In his article accompanying the recipe he writes: “Avoid so-called baby carrots, the pre-peeled kind, which are machine-whittled from giant specimens.”

Where can I find the answer to a question already asked?  I'm not seeing any answers or responses to comments.

Question is: Do I cook the meat in the marinade or remove it first?

My intuition guided me. I cooked all the yummy marinade and pork together. All that liquid assures it will not get dried out in the oven. And it packs a punch with the leftovers for days in a seal tight container. I cook for one, but I always make extra because I'm a leftover queen of my own cooking.
What can you sub for butter? Or is the butter essential?
Cooked this sous vide. No issue with drying out with that technique. Great dish.

Pretty good, maybe a little sweet for me (just reviewing the pork).  I was using only 2/3 of a single tenderloin, because that's what I had and I was only cooking for two.  I temped it at 20 minutes (mindful of its reduced bulk), and it was 135-145 depending on where.  I rested it 20 minutes and it was, if anything, a little more done than I'd have liked.  I did pull it out of the sauce and reduced the sauce some more.  Easy.

Also, I saw a comment somewhere (maybe a different pork tenderloin recipe?) in which the commenter noted the importance of trimming the silverskin off the tenderloin before marinating and cooking.  Hat tip to her, that's an important note.  I think it's not mentioned in the recipe because it's probably second nature to experienced cooks, which ain't me.
The recipe should make clear that the pork pan should be uncovered when it goes in the oven.

This was a FANTASTIC dinner. I brined my pork tenderloin overnight before doing the marinade and it was very tender. The carrots are addictive. I served this with fluffy white rice.

For the sauce, when I pulled it out of the oven, it was way too liquid and I didn't think I was going to get what I needed from the oven, so I finished it off on the stove in the same Pyrex pan I used in the oven. That was the way to go and it got to the right consistency in about 2 minutes.

We first made this and I just sautéed the carrots and ginger in butter and they browned, and ginger got this delicious caramelization on the carrots and crispness on the ginger. We made it again and I realized I didn’t follow it properly so I followed the recipe with the water and found the carrots to be disappointing and too soft. To be fair, we like a little crunch remaining in our cooked carrots and while the sauté version didn’t have much crunch since they were so thinly cut, they weren’t mushy. We will make this again, but will stick to the sauté without the water for a nice caramelization.

title: Spinach-Endive Salad With Miso Dressing Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: This one's a keeper. The dressing is bright and just a little tart, perfect for the salad ingredients.
Found 5 flavor options of furikake at our Japanese grocery. Will any do, or is there a specific one to use?
I usually get the “nori komi” flavor; I assume that’s akin to the one of the classic flavors because it always has the most shelf space at my market. It’s fun to try different ones, though.
Shown is curly leaf spinach as opposed to flat.  It is much sturdier than flat.

I found the dressing to be more of a paste, so added more lime juice, rice wine, oil and water to get something that could be considered “Drizzle-able”. Did anyone else have this issue? The taste was delicious though, but salty enough to lead me go very lightly with the furikake. I used half a bunch of chopped and massaged lacinato kale mixed with bright crisp greens for crunch, no endive in a 30 mile radius. Grilled two little tuna steaks med rare, sliced, and drizzled with ponzu to serve on salad. Right on with the eggs and avocado.
@Justine agree with the need to add water, good tip!
I have been using the dressing on a mixed green salad with blueberries, strawberry slices, goat cheese and pistachios. Really yummy

Agree with another user who suggested adding water until dressing resembles a familiar vinaigrette consistency. Added finely chopped shallot because I had some leftover, nice touch! 

Cooked perfect jammy eggs at 10 minutes

Delightful w pork tenderloin and carrots! Loved the miso dressing! I was lacking furikake (and time to make it ) so I took a sheet of seaweed (roasted in avocado oil and sea salt) and crumbled it in, adding a beautiful deep green color.

Unlike some commenters, I had no trouble with the dressing being paste-like. It was perfectly fluid. It was, however, quite tart, and I had trouble detecting the miso. A perfectly fine vinaigrette, but this didn’t feel particularly special to me.

title: Matcha-Coconut Custard Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: canned Asian coconut milk or the kind that comes in a carton?
I’m not aware of any coconut milk that comes in a carton. Perhaps you’re thinking of coconut water? That’s very different and not what’s called for here.
Canned. The carton is coconut water.

@D. Carton coconut milk has a viscosity closer to almond, oat, or cows milk and is used for cereal, milk-based coffee drinks, drinking plain, etc. Canned coconut milk is generally much thicker and richer.

There is actual coconut milk (not the thin watery version) that comes in a carton. 

If you're not sure, look at the nutrition label. Real coconut milk has lots of fat and a shocking number of calories per cup.
any subs for gelatin? Agar?

@christina agar-agar has to be boiled to set and the resulting texture is different from pudding made with gelatin…I have substituted it when I cook for vegetarians…one good thing is that mixtures made with agar can be reboiled (with more added) if the first try makes it too loose. Because it sets at room temperature you will know if you need to add more agar and reboil.
I used 0.75 teaspoons of agar instead of the gelatin and it worked well, but the results were somewhat on the firm side. Next time I'm trying a bit less agar.

For anyone confused there are Tetra Pak (cartons) of coconut milk. They are NOT the „coconut milk“ sold at regular grocery stores or big box stores (even though sometimes they have them). They are far superior to the canned stuff. Look for the Thai brand Arroy-D or the Kara brand from Indonesia.

This recipe turned out absolutely delicious. The matcha and the coconut was an amazing combination. I was tempted to substitute the sugar for honey but wanted to try the recipe as presented first. I may try that next time.

On a side note, the quantity of people who couldn't figure out what coconut milk is makes me concerned for the future of this nation.
@Sean I am not the sort of person who says “lol!” As a habit but… Reading your comment, I legitimately laughed out loud.
Same question: there is coconut milk that comes in a carton that’s not coconut water.  It’s in the dairy aisle with all of the other “milks”.  Which type should be used?

Gave this a try but used the coconut milk in the carton you purchase in the dairy aisle.  I wish the ingredients list had been more specific and made it clear you need to use the CANNED type of coconut milk.  As it was the recipe came out completely soupy in an unappetizing olive color and tastes like I was drinking tea leaves.  The matcha I purchased was expensive and hated to have wasted it on this failure.
The canned milk has a better texture and more fat, so I would choose that over the carton kind @Lynn D.
I made this  and the end product was quite soupy.  After the blooming period the water completely absorbed into the gelatin,  and was almost dry/caked.  Any tips ?
@Sam I had the same experience. It tasted good but didn’t set up properly. I served it at a party and people were drinking it rather than eating it with a spoon. 🤦🏻‍♀️
As called for this custard is WAY too sweet.  Since using gelatin it is easy to cut back on sugar without sacrificing final consistency

Thanks to other commenters here, I went to my local Asian grocery and got a Tetra-Pak of Aroy-D coconut milk. Never knew this stuff existed, and will be using it in everything from now on!

And the recipe turned out great! Perfect consistency and flavour. Went a little light on the sugar (1/3 cup) and thought it was perfect, although my wife thought a little more sugar wouldn’t hurt.
Thinking of making this with egg yolks instead of gelatin, more like a real custard. Any thoughts?
Native forest makes coconut cream in a carton.
There is coconut milk, packaged in a carton at our local Asian market—possibly a Thai brand?  Forget the packaging and make sure you get the right ingredient.
Does anyone have a trick for getting the matcha to dissolve?   I fear half of it went down the drain or trapped in the sieve.

Inspired first NYT Cooking comment!

I used 1.5 cans of unsweetened coconut milk; substituted date syrup for granulated sugar; interpreted "just below simmering" as 175 degrees; and was too lazy to strain.  Had creamy, smooth, bright green custard for breakfast after overnight in refrigerator.  Next week I will experiment with boxed coconut milk and coquito flavors (star anise, cinnamon, cloves and nutmeg).  My guess is that the gelatin will deal with either canned or boxed coconut milk.
Smooth and delicious. Thank you, Mr Tanis.
Has anyone tried replacing the sugar with puréed banana or something? Trying to reduce sugars.
Does anyone think the sugar could be reduced to 1/3 or 1/4 cup?
Not sure why this didn't work for me. Took forever  to set up at all and tasted like lawn clippings.

I tried this recipe and it failed in two ways.  One I learned later from another recipe that Matcha gets bitter when you add it to a substrate that is too hot.  And secondly, even though I fallowed the recipe religiously it never congealed.  There was a hard crust on the top made of coconut fat but the main "pudding" was liquid even after letting it sit overnight.

I used one half coconut milk and one half evaporated coconut milk, which is what I had on hand. The latter is much thicker, of course, but the results were wonderfully smooth and creamy— and not at all sweet to my taste.
is there a way to use honey instead of sugar? would I have to cut the amount of coconut milk to compensate for additional liquid?

Followed the recipe and just used a touch less sugar (90 instead of 100 grams). I think it could definitely be reduced more, as suggested by other commenters. Also I used nearly two cans of full fat coconut milk from Trader Joe’s for reference. First time using gelatin, and it kind of turned me off the final product! Had a weird smell that reminded me it’s made of hooves haha. For someone who loves matcha and coconut, I was very surprised (and saddened) to not enjoy the final product at all! To me, it was cloying and artificial tasting. I’ve had better succeed getting my matcha dessert fix via creme brûlée. Best of luck to other cooks!
Will this keep a few days in the refrigerator?
I would add some dairy free whipped cream to go on top and then sprinkle the dried coconut on top of it p.s my uncle would love that 😃

title: Spiced Apple Fritters With Hot Honey Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I hasve my great grandmother Sophie's apple fritter recipe (she fried them in  lard), and she notes that she always grates the apples "for better flavor". This recipe is lovely as well, no lard required.
@Aine o like the grated apple idea!!

If you are looking at this and wondering how to make it gluten and dairy free, you can buy a box of GF vanilla cake or muffin mix and add dairy free milk and the apples. Keep the batter on the thicker side. Or... if you ever happen to be in the Walla Walla, WA airport there is a gluten free bakery in the airport that makes these and they are outta this world tasty.

@Ra Subbing Bob’s Red Mill Gluten free 1:1 flour and almond milk also worked perfectly for a GF and DF version. They were declared to be delicious by everyone including those who didn’t need to be eating GF or DF.

Well I had no cast iron pan, Dutch oven or thermometer & did not want to deep fry. We don't do that much here in health conscious Nor Cal! Thought I could wing it. Used canola oil to fry, not deep fry. Used deep sided steel stock pot & guessed at the temp. They burned & blackened quickly. Kept turning temp down, still blackened quickly w some raw batter inside. But, I poured some maple syrup on them & ate them anyway! The idea is great, my approach could succeed w grated apples & better pot.

@KImberly C Submerging in the hot oil is what allows the middle to cook through before the outside is burned - it sounds like you were sautéing, which will not heat up the inside of a ball of dough quickly enough.

These are excellent and simple. Used a small pot as well and filled with oil vs a Dutch oven. Worked perfectly. 

Best was sprinkled with cinnamon sugar

I didn't care for these at all. The batter is very bland and bread like. I followed the directions and used honeycrisp apples. The apples and the honey were good but the batter was was just bad. I diced my apples into one centimeter cubes and they were still raw. I used a thermometer to keep the oil at the correct temperature. Very disappointing :(

Mine came out as greasy sponge balls. Not one of David Tanis's best efforts, which are usually excellent. I shredded the apples. It was all a lot of work for a poor outcome.  Unfortunately, they are heading for the garbage pail.
can these be frozen?
Wondering if adapt so could cook in convection oven or air fryer
Delish!  My first foray into deep frying. Couldn’t get crispy outside but that seems like the cook’s error

Batter came together easily and I used the suggestion of another commenter to shred the apples. I couldn't make this recipe work though. I used a Dutch oven, as suggested, but dont have a thermometer to check the temperature. I couldn't find the sweet spot, so each time they browned way too fast and were undercooked on the inside. Pity.
I added vanilla and extra cinnamon and sugar and they came out really good! Would be reeeeally good if you have almond extract

title: Cabbage Salad With Jalapeño-Lime Dressing Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: This salad is excellent. I paired it with the chipotle-honey chicken recipe that's also in the NYT Cooking app and served it for a friend's birthday. Easy as can be, and an absolute hit. It's refreshing,.acidic, and the maceration gives the cabbage a less raw texture that adds to the experience.
@Mun Bun I also paired this with the honey chipotle chicken and used it as the base for the soft tacos. Oh my it was amazing! Everyone raved and I’ve made that chicken a dozen times with less raves 😂
@Mun Bun I needed this comment! I was looking for a slaw for that exact recipe and hoped someone would say it worked for them!!
Use with fish in fish tacos
Excellent salad, nice break after all the rich holiday food. I served it mixed with Nisha Vora’s Super Savory Grated Tofu, and the combination was fantastic. It would also make a good taco filling.
Very good.Used ingredients as listed. Mixed spices, lime juice, pepper, jalapeños, in bowl and then added cabbage and radishes.  Garnished with avocado.

I added thinly sliced mini cucumber and thin sliced green onions to the salad.  I also added agave to the dressing to cut the acid.  lastly i added the jalapeno and cilantro to the dressing and blended it.  amazing.  Created more of a slaw like consistency.

Anyone else think it odd that the picture depicts a slaw, while the salad as written has you presenting the cabbage like kimchi, resulting in a somewhat wilted, wet-ish salad? Next time, I'll just prep like a regular slaw. Salting the cabbage does nothing for this salad!
@MRo salting the cabbage helps tenderize it and draw out excess water.
How far ahead can this be made?
I blended the avocado with the dressing ingredients and it was luscious!

100 percent YES! This was a total hit. Made on New Year's Day with a vegan pot of black-eyed peas. I'm one of those people with the cilantro-tastes-like-soap gene so I used a big handful of chopped parsley instead. Otherwise followed as written, and it was INCREDIBLE. Don't skip the avocado, it adds a perfect creamy note.

We loved this salad! Added, thinly sliced red onion, and reading through these comments, we love the idea of adding a small bit of sugar or agave to balance the acid. We agree, avocados are a must. Added Italian sausage, thinly sliced to the slaw and made a whole meal out of it.
Salad was very tasty and easy to make. I did rinse the salt off the cabbages before drying it as I did not want to have too much salt.
Naâ cabbage ok
What could I substitute for avocado that would be as unctuous?
@Bougeotte Whipped tofu ricotta (see Nisha Vora recipe on this app)? A creamy hummus or other bean dip?
What if I subbed lemons for the limes?  Why not?
How long in advance of serving can I make this?
@Question I’ve eaten it as a leftover several days after making. It was still delicious.
This was so good. Next time I will shred the cabbage with a mandoline and maybe add in red cabbage and sliced cucumber.

Next time, I would shred the cabbage finely. A food processor or a mandoline would work. With 1/4-inch slices as in the recipe, the cabbage seemed too chunky and tough, even after pre-salting. The flavors are nicely balanced. I would add more jalapeno, but that's just me.

I liked this salad but wish it had a little more creaminess. It kind of just felt like a big crunchy salad. I modified it with using romaine and some cabbage. I only had 1.5 avocados and I would’ve put in an extra one. Also more dressing! I ended up adding more olive oil and juice from half a lemon. Tasty overall!
Is it very spicy? I can handle a little spice but nothing too hot
@Elizabeth Elizabeth:  Try seeding a jalapeno and removing the white pith. Then chop it unto small pieces.  That should remove most of the heat.  Bon Apetit!

Does anyone know if it’s possible to become more tolerant of spicy hot foods? There’s no way I could tolerate a tablespoon of jalapenos in this recipe. Radishes are okay. Horseradish is borderline. I really want to spice up my meals, but one bite of a jalapeno and I can't taste anything else.

This is absolutely delicious!!  I confess I was so eager to try it that I had to sub jarred jalapeños  and frozen cilantro for fresh, but the flavor was excellent anyway.  Next time I promise to make this the "right" way.  I did use a bit of red cabbage for color.  I will make a note not to prepare the cabbage too far in advance of serving as it starts to soften a little too much if left sitting very long.  This will be on regular rotation at our house!!
100% awesome.  In the rotation!
Added lots extra lime juice and a teaspoon of sugar to balance flavors.
Found it a bit bland! Maybe up the lime juice?
This is spectacular and easy to make!! I use it as a side dish with Asian food and as a topping for tacos.
This was delicious. I used Serrano peppers for more heat.
Easy excellence, try all the additions you want.

I made this salad twice, to accompany "I Can't Believe It's Not Chicken" super savory grated tofu and rice, as another commentor suggested.  I added a small amount of chopped broccoli, and also a 9 oz. package of shelled edamame from Trader Joe's, just before serving.  My boyfriend said the edamame really makes this salad. :-) For me, the star is the avocado, but serve it on the side, and let people add it to their salad, because the salad won't keep well with avocado in it.

This was my first time making a cabbage salad and have two requests for the authors: 1) specify how much salt to use, and/or give instructions on whether/how to rinse it off. 2) Cutting a cabbage into "slices" is not helpful for the end result. Should say something like 'cut into ribbons.' I was trying to figure out how to toss AND keep the slices intact, when that's totally unnecessary as it turns out.
This is one of my most favorite salads to go with any mexican dish! my kids like it too, sometimes I will pickle radishes so I have them on hand, also a yummy addition here!
I used a red cabbage because it’s pretty and added broccoli sprouts . Used pickled jalapeños because it’s what I had. It was very good

title: Black-Eyed Peas With Pork Belly and Greens Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody:
Nice recipe. Pressure cooking/Instant Pot works well for dried peas (20-25 mins pressure+natural release). 
  For vegans: Mashing at least half the peas after cooking (plus crushed tomatoes) adds umami. An Indian (Mughlai) recipe uses loads of browned onion with tomato, ginger, garlic, garam masala, mild chilies, garnished with chopped cilantro (or parsley for cilantro haters).
  I would serve the greens on the side. (Raw mustard greens make a great salad: a spicier, cheaper sub for arugula.)
Me think 1.5 lb of dry beans will yield a lot more than 6 to 8 servings, especially when combinef wv2 lbs of meat and 1.5 lbs of kale!!!
And if you are not a pork eater then what?
Thanks for your question. My question too.

@T. Conway
Smoked turkey wings work well!!!!

T. Conway - If you eat poultry, smoked turkey thighs from Diestel Farms are good (can order online at https://diestelturkey.com/). I have used in gumbos and soups; they pack a lot of smoky flavor. They are raised humanely and without growth hormones or antibiotics.
@T. Conway Mushrooms impart a nice umami flavor. Add a bit more veggie oil to get a bit of richness.
I would use smoked pork belly (e.g. BACON)

Another weirdly inaccurate picture of the dish, as prepared. We're using WATER, so where'd that dark broth depicted come from?? Taste is good, but I felt there were a lot of weird steps, like cooking the garlic & kale separately from the soup, then adding in. Same for the pork belly. Why not start with browing the meat, then simmering in the water? Why not build the flavor profile by adding in everything to the same pot, not separate?

Made this for New Year’s Day. But followed the photo, not the recipe. I am surprised no one else commented on the obvious — the pork belly in the photo is almost one inch crisped chunks. The recipe says to chop the pork into 1/2 chunks then crisp before adding to the stew. Duh, major disconnect here. The food stylist took way too many liberties and the editors did not catch the disparity. To achieve the result in the photo (which is ultimately a grand version of its humble roots,) cut the pork belly into larger chunks and crisp in a cast iron skillet.

Except for the colour - mine turned out  grayish unlike the pic in the recipe - it was super delicious. I used leftover rolled pork belly roast from Christmas, chopped and crisped, and mustard greens. 5 stars.
@ssgokavi I added two tablespoon of tomato paste, which added color to the dish.

Appears to be a great recipe that I will try in a few days. But there has been a serious omission, where's the penny? Although the peas themselves give good luck, the person who finds the penny in their beans will have extra good luck for the year. And no, I don't want to hear a word about the penny shortage.

Way too fussy and barely southern at all.

To do southern, use frozen peas if you can get 'em -- Whole Foods carries them. Southerners like 'em fresh, but not available in winter.

Add onion, carrot, and celery if ya like, otherwise just the peas and water (or preferably BROTH), some salt and pepper, and a smoked pork hock. 

Appropriate greens would be collards, cooked separately in broth with another hock. And for good luck, serve with hog jowls. UH -- no thanks -- carve up a ham.
can I sub uncured bacon for the pork belly?
@Lynda Spiegel yes, just don’t simmer as long, it’ll cook faster.

Adjusted the recipe for 4. I browned a piece of pork shoulder in my instant pot. Then removed it and put in chopped collard greens and all the other ingredients with dried black eyed peas, pressure set for 8 minutes Added the lovely shredded pork shoulder at the end and served with a salad. Absolutely delicious!
I could only get canned black eyed peas. Tye conversion of 1.5 pounds peas to cans id 6 15-ounce cans, and so far I’ve opened and drained 4 cans and it looks like a lot!!
Followed the recipe exactly. A lot of time went into this and frankly wasn’t worth it. On the bland side, and the pork belly was over the top fatty.
I agree 100% A LOT of unnecessary steps too!

Best black eyed peas I’ve ever made! My chef son suggested I let the meat cook in the liquid before removing it, which kept it tender and flavorful. Used rancho gordo peas and extra garlic. Everyone loved them!

Excellent recipe that received raves from my guests with decades of peas and greens eating experience.

I'll just add that my favorite part of any greens is the stems - after separating them from the leaves, slice thinly and cook for about 15 minutes before adding the leaves. Ups the taste and texture of the dish.
Can you store this in the freezer? If yes, how do you freeze it and how long will it stay fresh in the freezer?
Beans and greens are delicious. Not sure the time spent on pork belly was worth it and not very tasty. Might have just used thick slice bacon for flavor.

I've made this twice now, exactly as written, and feel like it really gives a layered set of flavors. In response to the commenter that asked why cook the pork belly and greens separately from the rest, I see that it is specifically the different layers of flavor (and texture, because the crispness of the pork belly is very additive) that make this recipe so good. Doing it the usual way of browning the meat first and cooking it all together would not have had this same result. Great recipe!

I don't eat pork - and prefer vegetarian black eyed peas. 
Any suggestions?

Never had black eyed beans.  We had home made Boston baked beans by my Dad, an old Navy recipe, he served 20 years, 1934 to 1954.  On ship in Pacific during WWII.  Thanks, Dad.  Hot dogs and canned brown bread on Saturday nights. 

Mom, Syrian, so Chickpeas in Hummus and many delicious dishes.  And I’ve made bean dishes.  BEB here is 5 lbs for $10.79 so Hoppin’ John out for me.  That’d last me several more New Years.  I’ll look tomorrow for 1 pound bag.  Recipe looks great!

@ScratchcooK: Walmart.com sells a 6-pack (1 lb/pack) of dried black-eyed peas for $9.96, vs $8.76. for a 6pack of dried chickpeas: both are economical protein sources.
This recipe discards the pork belly skin: I wouldn't. Dice it, brown the pieces (with the tiniest amount of added oil- it will release some flavorful fat), and  pressure cook with the peas to break down the collagen to gelatin, which adds body/flavor to the dish.

T. Conway, if you don't eat pork, but eat poultry, smoked turkey thighs from Diestel Farms are good. I have used in gumbo and soups; they pack a lot of smoky flavor. And they are humanely raised without hormones, antibiotics or growth stimulants.
I served it over couscous and it was very tasty. What do you do with the onion half? Do you just leave it in the pot until you finish the peas and greens?

@T.Conway, I've been making a vegan version this recipe for several New Year's now. We always add some liquid smoke and/or smoked paprika, and might throw in some vegan sausage this year. TBH, I think it works fine without the meat, if what you're looking for is a tasty black-eyed peas dish to start your year off right.
Yes yes yes NO CLOVE yes yes yes!
Followed recipe except used lacinato kale as greens.  Didn’t have fennel, so left it out.  Very tasty and all ingredients for good luck!

Unsurprisingly, this was very tasty!

I had to improvise a bit, and cut out the overnight soak and marinade: start soaking beans when you start cooking; put all rub ingredients into the pot with the frozen hunk of pork belly I had (maybe 1/4 lb.), plus some thick-cut bacon, maybe 1/2 lb. Do the simmer, pull out bacon earlier. Added a couple of sausages at the feeding stage of the pork belly. Nicely balanced, very tasty.

This recipe solves the issue with the weird texture bacon gets when you cook it with the peas or beans. The crispy pork belly is a really great contrast with the peas.  I used fresh-frozen purple hull peas. Initially I cut back on salt, but had to significantly adjust salt and pepper when the peas were cooked, which greatly improved the flavor. Serve with Tabasco or other hot sauce for those who like things a touch spicier.

Cooked the peas with the pork belly - saves an hour. Removed the pork belly, chopped it & fried in pan without oil, then added garlic & chili flakes, then the sliced greens & covered until wilted. Added vinegar directly to the soup so as not to turn the greens brown. Great recipe anyway!
This was an ok dish.  Seemed like a lot of steps for basic black eyed peas, which we eat often.  The pork was a bit dry, and the next day we ate the peas without it.  Nothing to rave about.
1.5 cups of dried blackeyed peas? 1.5 lb will feed an army.

Great spices & flavors. I confess I didn’t see the point of cooking the pork separately from the beans. So I diced up the pork and then cooked it with the beans & onion in the slow cooker for ~4 hrs. Did the rest as directed; yum!

title: Lemon Curd Roulade Recipe
author: David Tanis

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 10 minutes, plus 3 hours’ cooling and chilling
stats.Cook Time: 55 minutes, plus 3 hours’ cooling and chilling

comments.commentBody:
When I make pumpkin rolls and cocoa rolls I find you get a nicer shape to the roll if you place a damp tea towel on a flat surface and turn the sheet pan onto it. Carefully remove the parchment and roll the cake up from the short side with the towel. 
Refrigerate for 3-4 hours for best results before filling. 
(I actually have 4 dedicated linen towels for this that I wash separately without perfumed detergent or softener and store in a plastic bag until the next time!)

@Famharris
So helpful! Thank you!
I've had issues turning out a jelly roll pan (its a me thing) so I roll in the parchment and wrap that in a damp tea towel - I agree that some additional humidity is a good thing
Occurs to me that substituting cranberry curd for lemon curd might make a delicious alternative.
Good Morning: Step 1 seems like the wrong time to heat the oven to 400 degrees, especially when one considers how long it will take to chill the curd completely in step 4.

@VSB I think recipe authors do this because it is convenient to see the oven temp at the very top of the recipe. You are correct that it is silly to run the oven for an hour or more with nothing in it. But when the time comes to bake one doesn’t have to search for the temp.
This is inspiring me to mash up 2 David Tanis recipes, replacing the lemon curd with cranberry curd from his magnificent tart.

The first time I made the sponge, it did not bake long enough, even though I increased the time. When I rolled it, it stuck to the parchment (it was excessively wet and sticky) and I had to sadly throw it out. After doing a little research, it looks like nearly every recipe for a roulade requires that the egg whites be whipped first to ensure an airy texture, and hopefully, a fully baked sponge!

My biggest gripe with this recipe is that it isn’t a roulade. This is more like a super inflated under stuffed Swiss Roll. The giveaway is the sponge. A roulade is a roll done with meringue. Use a sponge and it becomes a Swiss Roll. As far as Swiss Rolls go the pictures show a poor example. A good Swiss Roll has a thin layer of sponge and a healthy amount of stuff inside. Typically the curd should be melded with a cream of some kind.

I made this today, following the recipe exactly and I’m sad to say it’s just not very good. The curd is far too tart. The cake is dry and bland. The whipped cream icing doesn’t really add any interest. Overall, it’s very one-dimensional and flat. Very disappointing.

The recipe calls for either all purpose flour or cake flour. They bake up differently. But maybe using all purpose flour would make the cake easier to roll? I wonder why the author does not specify a preference.

My experience with NYT cooking recipes has thus far been great, they turn out well on the first try. This recipe was vexxing. The cook time on the curd is not long enough and the baking time of the jelly roll isn't long enough either. I had to do both twice and with the curd I used an old Betty Crocker recipe instead. Bad time to have redo things right before the holiday.

Turning out the cake (turn out of pan, peel paper off) onto a damp (wet then thoroughly wrung out) linen tea towel is the method i use for a roll.  The cake will cool quickly, the filling is added and the cake will roll.  No need to pre-roll in my experience.

I thought this was great, but the whipped cream topping definitely needed some additional sugar/flavor. There’s a lot of liquid in the curd as well so takes a bit longer to thicken. I used Meyer lemons so maybe the sweetness helped. A real Sunday project but was fun and delicious!
Should the whites get into a stiff peaks consistency or softer?
Any reason not to use store bought lemon curd?

@bibi

You can but homemade curd taste different and better with the buttery finish and fresh lemons but I’m not a food snob so I could go either way depending on time
Is 5 or 6 minutes baking long enough?
Bonne Mamam is nearly as good as home made.

Not quite a total fail, but close. I took the cake out at 8 mins, but it really wasn't done, which I found out as I rolled it. for the second attempt I used the suggested whipped whites and cooked 14 minutes, then put it on damp towel (but not quite damp enough - it stuck a little). It rolled up fine, but a lot of the lemon curd oozed out. It is a nice shape. I'll frost it tomorrow and pass extra curd and cranberry and blueberry coulis.
so scrumptious I almost had a heart attack at how beautiful the pic was!!!

This is a wonderful recipe to serve to guests. Very impressive and delicious served with sliced strawberries. I made the cake exactly as the recipe is written, baking it only six minutes, and it rolled beautifully. The lemon curd was Mary Berry's online recipe, using three whole eggs. There was lots left over for another roulade and it keeps in the fridge for three months. I always add a bit of powdered milk to cream while whipping, as it stabilizes it.

title: Meatballs With Lentils and Greens Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody:
I made this pretty close to as written. I liked it overall, but I found the smoked paprika flavour a bit strong. I would use 2 teaspoons instead of 2 tablespoons next time.

Also, I forgot to add the olive oil to the meatballs, and it didn't seem to have a big effect. I used a beef/pork/veal mix and they were still plenty juicy.
Big hit with the family.  Made exactly as written.  Flavorful, tender meatballs; lentils and chard a lovely combination.  Will be making again.

Terrific and complete meal, with a lovely contrast of textures and flavors. Nothing special about the lentils until you serve with greens: earthy lentils, sweet leeks, slightly bitter greens (I used turnip, since chard was unavailable).
We loved the meatballs, although I can see that some might prefer less (maybe half) paprika. They would also be good made with ground lamb.
Prep was easy. Mix the meatballs early in day; the rest can be finished off in 30-45 minutes.
Ground lamb suggestion Is brilliant!

Another great Tanis dish.  Used lamb for meatballs. Lentils & greens would be good solo.  Made as written, notes by step #:
#1:  Smoked paprika is a lot.  Consider reduction.  Meat mix easier to handle when refrigerated several hours. May not need all the panade (milk bread). 
#4: Leeks take longer than 6 minutes to softened & light brown. Don't rush this.
#6: Let meatballs come to room temp (@ 60 min) before roasting & still took more than 15 min to done. (Tested w/insta read thermometer).
I made these. Forgot the olive oil but they still turned out fine. I put in a little less of the paprika, the amount specified seemed like a lot. Used a garlic press and shallots instead of onions.

This is only about the lentils. I love a copious veggie side that isn't salad. Used Trader Joe's bagged kale because that's what I had and it was amazing. Just be sure to remove the tough stems. Thanks for the delicious healthy weeknight meal.

A very simple, delicious and forgiving recipe.  There was no way the boulettes would cook in fifteen minutes.  I cooked them a bit longer and finished them under the broiler and they were perfect.  Traditionally the lentils would have some diced carrots, so I added them at the start and at the end gave them a smidgeon of mustard and vinegar.

@marie-claude garneau I agree. Mine took longer than 15 minutes. And I needed to brown them under the broiler. Maybe it’s because mine sat in the fridge all up until I added the bread, per instructions. Room temp meat cooks faster

This dish was lovely. Taught me a different way to prepare lentils and made them so nuanced. The chard at the market was not great so I used shredded Brussel sprouts. They worked  nicely with the spices used in the tender polpette. The directions made prep and cooking easy. I think I will prepare and freeze batches of these polpettes and have them ready for a quick meal. 
I served this dish with a side of steamed carrots and a loaf of hot bread. 
This was a great cold evening family supper.
Flavor was amazing. Mine took a bit longer than 15 minutes possibly because they came straight from the fridge. Recipe is a keeper.
regular parsley or flat-leaf like in the Picture?
Thank you David, yet again!  Made tonight as written on a cold San Francisco evening.  Very easy, big flavors.  The polpette are so tender!  Next time will try with pasta.
Excellent, unexpected flavor playoffs. Green lentils were mush at 15 minutes, though. Next time might try black. And would make more greens/less meat.

Delicious leek-chard, good lentils, disastrous meatballs. Made w/ turkey b/c any ground meat was supposed to work, accd'g to the story. With the too-wet bread, a soggy mess. At 15 minutes' cooking time, they looked raw, so I gave them 5 mins. more. Then, Insta therm. 165, but still not brown. 5 add'l. mins, barely better. Oven is exact. May try w/ lamb and half the panade, but otherwise, will sub a better meatball recipe. Disappointing.
Is the garlic sliced, minced or smashed?

The recipe calls for the garlic in the meatballs to be "smashed to a paste" and the garlic in the greens to be grated. The difference might be important if you're sensitive to (or about) garlic flavors, but in my experience making a very similar dish, smashing all of the garlic through a garlic press is easy and delicious. I also use twice as much cumin, at least, so take that for what it's worth!
Recipe says smaxhed for meatballs, but grated for the lentils, although in actual fact it won't make much diverence!
In the meatballs it’s a paste and in the leeks it’s grated, but I just chopped a lot for both cause my press is missing!
Can you soak the white bread in water instead of milk?
Judy S - You ask if you can soak the bread in water instead of milk.  That soaked bread is known as panade to use in meatballs and yes, you sure can use water or broth instead of milk.
So if you let the meat rest overnight do you not need to add the bread in at all?

@B. Feld

It doesn’t seem to indicate that in my reading. The bread is important.

@Beth
 
I agree it’s confusing. 

I think the recipe could read : 

“Prepare the meatballs: Put bread cubes in 1 cup milk to soak for about 15 minutes, turning occasionally. In a mixing bowl, combine the ground meat, onion, olive oil, pimentón, crushed red pepper, cumin, garlic, salt, pepper and, when it’s soft, the soaked bread and any remaining milk  (unless refrigerating overnight in which case add bread before cook) . Mix very well with hands, kneading to incorporate ingredients evenly. Cover and refrigerate, at least 1 hour and up to overnight (Longer yields better flavor.)

?
Would multigrain bread work in this if I cut out the seeded crust? It’s all I’ve got on hand

@May

Yes, it will probably work, but it may taste slightly different and have a slightly different texture. Give it a try!
I assume at some point after step 5 you mix the veg and the meat?  Recipe doesn't say.
@Paul Z step 8.
Paul Z - You ask, "I assume at some point after step 5 you mix the veg and the meat?".  No, the veg and greens are used atop the lentils.  See Step 8.
Is step 6 referring to the meatballs?

I made this dairy free and gluten free- used GF bread crumbs and used far less "milk". Did not let sit overnite. Wore latex gloves to mix meat and spices. Cooked meatballs in the toaster oven on a silpan mat on the bottom of the toaster tray. Served them with homemade marinara sauce. By not letting it sit overnite you can have dinner in about 40 minutes.
This recipe doesn’t use green lentils. The lentils pictured are brown lentils. The used green lentils and they were mush. Delicious but I’ll use brown lentils next time!

We really enjoyed this. Used ground beef but lamb would be delicious too. Adjusted amounts for one pound of ground beef and 3/4 cups lentils. Reduced smoked paprika after reading comments but otherwise followed recipe. Roasted carrots were perfect on the side and added nice color to the plates. Will make again.

title: Pear and Radicchio Salad With Blue Cheese Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: This is an outstanding salad. Didn't have pomegranate seeds so we added chopped toasted walnuts and a drizzle of honey. Cannot wait to make it again.
Ugh, I love David Tanis. No trends, no gimmicks, just really good food. A lovely salad

This sounds fantastic, love all three of the ingredients for salads. I'd add pistachios,  sunflower seeds or pepitas for extra crunch. This would go well over warm grains if you wanted to add heft to the salad
@rb pistachios would be a perfect addition!

Note to Meems re substituting olives for cheese -- It's not just saltiness that the cheese adds to the salad but creaminess. There are vegan cheeses that might work for you, or you might consider blending up something creamy (e.g. firm tofu or raw, soaked cashews) with something a little salty with a little umami (e.g., white miso). You could lightly dot this or drizzle it on the finished salad to keep your meal kosher. Different flavor profile than with cheese, but might be just as tasty.
@Donna S thank you!
Worth the pomegranate effort! Perfect for winter

It was fantastic. My only comment, like most NYT recipes, I have to calculate the cook time like calculating dog years - add about 7 minutes to every 1. It took me a solid 45 minutes to make. But worth it!
I want to serve it with the lamb meatballs but I keep kosher (no meat and milk together)  so what does the community think about adding some olives for the saltiness?

@Meems
How about adding a couple of dollops of silken tofu which has been mixed with nutritional yeast. That way you still get the cheesy notes with none of the dairy product.
@Meems there are lots of wonderful cashew based cheeses available that could be a great substitute.  They tend to be a bit pricey since but they are delicious!
I used feta instead of bleu and added toasted walnuts. A big hit in my household!

This would make a festive appetizer with the diced pears tossed with the vinaigrette piled onto a radicchio leaf, sprinkled with the blue cheese, pomegranate seeds and honey drizzle. Big grinds of black pepper to finish!!
Use large or small shallots
One of the most delicious salads I have ever made!
I don't think I see a specific instruction to slice the pears...

This was excellent. I made it as written, but will use slightly fewer pears (2 vs. 3) and add maybe a tbsp more pomegranate seeds. Just personal preference.

Served it with a pan roast chicken and a nice bread. I will absolutely add this to the rotation.
Just delightful.  Made it three times already.
Made this with store bought POM pomegranate seeds and Point Reyes Blue cheese.  It was delicious!
Deeeerlicious ! I made it as written and it was tasty. My new go to fall/winter salad. Thank you, David Tanis.

If the NYE host hadn’t made Beef Wellington (which was perfectly cooked) this salad would have been the star of the table!

Recipe is a keeper.
This is a wonderful salad.  I also used walnuts because I didn’t get pomegranate.  I will do that next time!

I was expecting to love this salad, as I love all the ingredients, especially the pairing of a juicy pear with bleu cheese. However, I found the sweetness of the balsamic vinegar overpowered and interfered with the pear-cheese alchemy. I would use a different dressing next time.

very tasty salad.  it took several weeks for me to find radicchio; then i could not find the arils and didn't have time to break down a pomegranate, so i added some walnuts.  nice combination - i see that someone else did this, too.  next time i think i will try black walnuts.
Very good with Cosmic Crisp apple, even though I had to do without the pomegranate arils.

@MML:  Same for me, and with ALL recipes, not only NYT. I have finally figured out that the timing reckoned for printed recipes appears always to be reckoned as the amount of time it would take on a cooking show or video—where all the prep (the “mise en place”) is already done. I’m persuaded that recipe writers never intend to include the time needed to pull all the ingredients out of storage, then measure, wash, peel, chop—all of that. That time is considerable!

@Barb:  And to make it into a “finger-food” appetizer, all the ingredients could be piled onto radicchio leaves, then each leaf rolled—*roughly* like for dolmas—skewered with a toothpick, and then drizzled with a bit of honey. Might want to scatter a little more crumbled cheese across it all, for visual contrast with the red leaves.

This is a very delicious and colorful salad - my friends beg me to bring it when they invite me to lunch or dinner. You can use any endive and red bib lettuce if radicchio is not available - and any type of nuts pretty much work too - just don’t leave out or substitute the pomegranate seeds!
Have made this salad with grilled radichhio and ungrilled. Prefer grilled but delicious either way.

title: Pecan Caramel Tart Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: The iconoclast in me decided long ago that making this tart with hazelnuts was even more delicious!
Would toasting the pecans before folding them into the caramel be a good step? I might give it a try.

I don't think so. The intense heat of the caramelized sugar roasts them. I am Swiss, my father's family having been from the very Engadine where Engadiner Nusstorte, or Tuorta da Nusch, is sold in every shop. We never toasted or roasted the nuts before adding them to the sugary, creamy mix.
I was wondering the same thing Jill, thank you for asking!

@Jill  I think by the time you add the cream and honey to the caramel it won’t be hot enough to toast the nuts enough for my taste. I’d consider toasting the nuts separately and adding them, still warm, to the caramel after it has cooled even a little more.
Reading the step on making the filling, my only thought is: Video please!
Oopps better link: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1026226-coconut-caramel-tart
There are a lot of videos out there for making caramel with tips to prevent recrystallization.
Looks like a good variant of the traditional PP.  The caramel provides the usual richness + more crust.  Definitely  a pie that needs to be served  in slivers.
I just made the filling but mine cooled into a hard toffee-like mass. It’s not pliable at all. What can I do to fix it? Reheat and add more cream?

This recipe is NOT a keeper. The crust is tough, hard, resistant to anything approach a neat cutting and not quite a jaw breaker to eat but close compared to a traditional butter pie crust.  The egg wash adds little. Beware to not burn the caramelized filling.
The innards test better today than yesterday, but, on balance, stick with the pecan pie. Yesterday, two guests clamored for chess pie, another sugar indulgence.
Not a huge fan of honey. What about maple syrup?

Trish - re your question of whether maple syrup would work in this to replace honey, my answer is that I don't think it will.  Honey is an invert syrup and it will respond differently than maple syrup which is about 2/3 sucrose.  If you want to swap in something for honey, consider another invert sugar like Golden Syrup or light corn syrup like Karo (no...this is not high fructose style).

@dimmerswitch I am not sure of the chemistry involved here, but want to share that I regularly use  maple syrup in my pecan pies.  I like the flavor better. Never had a problem with the pie setting or baking oddly.
I dislike the taste of honey and while I understand this is the traditional ingredient imparting the traditional taste, is there a substitute? Corn syrup? Lyle's?
Will this work with. 1-1 GF flour? Celiac here

@Susan My husband is celiac, too, and I’ve very successfully made a version of this tart with walnuts using this pastry recipe: https://epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/gluten-free-multi-purpose-sweet-pastry-dough.
Suggestions on how to make ahead for Thanksgiving? Would it be tasty and pretty if made 3 days ahead?
My family doesn’t care much for honey. Would maple syrup be an acceptable replacement?

Help me out here please!  I have a 9in. and an 11in. tart pan ( of course!).
Which would be a better choice?
Have been making this for years with walnuts and squiggled chocolate on top, think Jackson Pollack. Am going to try this version for Thanksgiving, with the chocolate…yum.
The size of the nuts is too big. Texture is tough. Would try again with nuts chopped finer.
nor are there enough pecans to fill the 10" tart pan

title: French Fisherman Stew (La Chaudrée) Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody:
I've been a recreational fisherman for 40 years, and cook my catches, this recipe has some issues in my opinion:
1 - Use fish stock > fish bouillon > clam broth instead of water, in that order of preference.
2 - The fish should be cut larger than 1X1" - it WILL break.  
3 - The fish does not need 10 minutes of cook time. Add fish; as soon as the broth returns to a boil add the mussels and COVER the pot. When they open in around 3-4 minutes then add the calamari for 60-90 seconds.

My favorite grocery story in a Downeast Maine town sells "chowder mix" -- the trimmings from whatever fresh fish they are selling that day. Cod, haddock, salmon, halibut, whatever. And it's several dollars per pound cheaper than the whole cuts. I make chowder often and love that scrappy, affordable mix. When scallops are (rarely) affordable, I get a few of those and add them, too.

If you have the time and don't mind "butchering" the fish yourself, along with the unused portion of the leeks, use the heads and bones to make a fish stock instead of using water.  This will add a ton of flavor.

@Tommy
I make stock from whatever creature leaves me with bones, shells, or other trimmings that are often discarded and yes, I use them in addition to, or in place of water.  It’s ok folks.
Maybe fennel?
I used a quart of fish stock from the fish store and added a pinch of saffron. Really excellent.
The recipe calls for 6 cups of water.
Could I use frozen seafood mix in this soup?

@Jason: I did. I used frozen “chowder mix,” (Maine thing), frozen mussels and squid. It’s what was on hand. I adjusted cooking times slightly. Used frozen fish stock (not water). Results were fine. Lovely flavor. Fresh is always best, but not always available.
The addition of some greens would be nice.
Maybe serve with a salad?
This reminds me of my favorite Scottish meal, Skink.  Mostly haddock, smoked preferred, and a fragrant milky base that I'll never forget.
How do you get broth if the only liquid added is wine?
@Fran Step 3 clearly says “add 6 cups water”.
Step 3: "....Add 6 cups water, and bring to a boil."

NYT Cooking follows the fusty, arcane recipe writing rule that water is never listed as an ingredient but is, instead, buried in the preparation steps. The theory is that, since everyone has water in their kitchen, it does not need to be mentioned at the outset. Even though that makes no sense, the odd quirk is strongly defended.

This came out really good. I made it per the recipe except I used fish stock in place of the 6 cups of water in Step 3 and no butter on the croutons. I just toasted the rounds. There is no dairy in this "chowder". Just a lot of delicious seafood.
when it comes to fish for this dish, how can you say cod or flounder? Flounder will fall apart and disintegrate in a minute. A poor second choice.
I often use flounder. It has never fallen apart. Add flounder late; it cooks quickly.
Don't underestimate the add-in of creme fraiche (as suggested); it really elevates the dish with a subtle creaminess. I used some heavy cream gone to sour which I regularly use for creme fraiche.

Lovely dinner. I used lobster stock I had in the freezer for 1/3 of the water that the recipe calls for. This was just the right amount (I got lucky); flavor of the broth was of the sea but not too intense. Fish, mussels, and calamari cooked perfectly in the time recipe indicates.
