I put the croutons (made from leftover restaurant rolls) on the bottom of our soup bowls and spooned fish and broth over them. Delicious, filling, and sort of sexy. Just zest, no parsley garnish.

Sounds delicious, but a shellfish allergy mitigates against things such as mussels or calamari.  I can substitute other fin fish, of course, but is there any variety to avoid?  For example, would salmon's strong flavor make it less than ideal?  Is there a variety of fish that would just turn to mush in a stew?

The fish name 'rockfish' is used regionally to describe different fish species. In the mid-Atlantic region it's striped bass, but it's not a term fisherman in NY or New England generally use. Snapper is an equally misused/useless term.

What you want is a white fish on the thick side with a firm texture. 

If you're making this in the fall in the northeast look for 'Blackfish' aka Tautog - there is no finer soup for fish or chowders and tis the season - see also Monkfish.
Fennel: yes. And SAFFRON

I followed this recipe as directed but also incorporated the comment to use fish broth and cooked the fish for less time...I was so disappointed. The fish fell apart, the broth was bland...what a mess. It sounded so good.
The recipe as written is on the bland side.   Replace some of the water with fish stock or clam juice.

wonderful winter supper with friends.  I followed the suggestion to use fish stock in lieu of water.  Served with Mr. Tanis’s salad of celery and fennel with radicchio and a chocolate mousse for dessert.  Simple, quick to cook, delicious.

title: Beet and Cucumber Salad Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody:
Do not boil the beets.
 Make a tinfoil pouch, drizzle beets with olive oil and a touch of kosher salt.
Seal the package and roast at 400 degrees for 39-45 minutes.  Let cool and peel.
Roasting intensifies the beets’ flavor, not boil it away,
@Josh truer words have never been said.
Trying this for lunch next week with eggs and perhaps feta.

I react to tarragon the way some people react to cilantro, I'm almost gagging just thinking of it...  any idea what to sub?  I love chives on anything but afraid they would overwhelm the rest of the salad, which sounds fabulous.  Basil, maybe?
Also, the vacuum packed beets that seem to be readily available are very tasty, and would save use of the oven and a pair of nitrile gloves I keep in stock for when I peel beets or pit cherries.
I would replace tarragon with finely chopped celery leaves.
@Fran Eckert I used vacuumed packed beets and skipped the tarragon because I didn’t have any. Still yummy.

This was good, but too much mustard in the vinaigrette.  Next time I would halve the mustard and puree some of the herbs in the dressing.  I added some feta to the salad and think it was a helpful addition. I roasted the beets in a foil pouch, as mentioned by Josh below.

@Carol the toasted beets in foil is the easiest hands-off way of cooking them then the outer skin slides right off. Goat cheese is also a very good combination. Scattered through or 1 inch slices of the log pressed down a bit and quickly toasted under the broiler for a “crunchy” version! Delicious!
To cook beets quickly, quarter them and steam them for 15 minutes. They taste great this way and they keep their nutrients intact.

Marvelous! Silky olive oil on cucumber and cooked silky beets.
A large chopped shallot and two tablespoons of Dijon seemed like they would overwhelm the oil and lemon juice; I only used a couple of tablespoons of chopped shallot and maybe an 1/8 tsp of Dijon, and the dressing came out fine. I might try more Dijon next time. @Fran Ekert, I skipped the tarragon.
Such a great summer dish! Added some arugula to the butter lettuce; subbed flat parsley for the tarragon; and paired it with a small portion of grilled salmon. Easy and delish!
Small beets will cook in about 30 min tops if boiled or steamed.
what is a good substitute for tarragon?

title: Plum-Cardamom Upside-Down Cake Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: And what kind of plum? Three types are at my grocery store: very big black plums, medium size purple plums (round and slighty red skin) and small Italian prune plums. I just made the classic plum torte recipe (which does not specific what type of plum) and it was a total failure: the ripe purple plums I got stayed firm and did not cook down like in the picture. The result was: a boring white cake with hard plum halves on top. So before I do this recipe PLEASE let us know: WHAT KIND OF PLUMS?!
@Nell, in his article, David Tanis recommends small Italian prune plums. Enjoy!
@Nell The accompanying article mentions the little purple Italian plums - and then links to Marion Burros’ plum tart - so maybe infer that these are the intended plums?
@Nell in the article he recommends the small Italian purple plums. It would be nice if that were in the head note too. Hope your next cake is better.

Approach with caution! The pear/plum confusion should have been a tip-off, but there are a number of problems. 1. The amount of plums called for is weirdly high and left me with a bunch of leftover slices (3-4 avg purple is enough). 2. Strongly recommend a parchment circle. 3. The beating times are way too long (maybe for by-hand vs. stand mixer)? I followed them against my better judgement and the batter collapsed. Cake still rose but had a chewy texture. Guessing 2-3 min is more accurate.
“Generously made with any kind of pear” while probably true,  plum is in the title so is this from a different recipe?  Perhaps I’ll try with 6 of one, half a dozen if the other.
Lynn, the response may be in the article linked above: "Then, finally, late-season plums, especially the little purple Italian ones are the perfect finish."
Plum or pear? Or both?

I ignored the pear reference and proceeded with my unripe unlabeled "purple" plums. to make the matter a little more interesting our guests were strictly vegan. Butter was substituted by olive oil and eggs were replaced by apple sauce. Normally i reduce the sugar amount but not in this case. to my surprise and delight with an extra 5 minutes baking time it was delicious. Make sure you don't rush the removal from the pan to allow some solidification.

Delicious and very, very easy. I used large, purple plums from the local farmstand which were almost overripe, and an extra 1/4 cup of brown sugar to help soak up the fruit juices. A big hit following the NYT's new recipe for pot roast (also terrific BTW).
@Faded elegance even better on the second day

Somewhere in the text, Tanis talks about using Italian prune plums, which are small and difficult to cut into sections and would be messy. In the photo of the cake, the plums are not small (nor are they pears). There is a mix up too.
Peaches would have been nicer.

I made the cake as directed. The plums I was gifted were small, so I halved them rather than slicing smaller. I used Italian plums. Three issues: I used melted butter as directed but my sugar, butter, eggs never got fluffy. Perhaps use softened butter instead. The brown sugar at the bottom of my pan leaked and burned at the bottom of the oven. Maybe my spring form pan is leakier than others? Knowing I should put a pan underneath to catch the drips would have been nice. Finally, after 45 minutes the top was so brown I took it out to check it. The toothpick came out clean. The toothpick lied. The middle of the cake fell as it cooled and is not fully cooked. If I had known that I would have just used foil to prevent more browning and put it in back in the oven. I'm not serving it to company so I can just cut around that bit. 
The cake is pretty and tastes good. It's a very sweet cake so some whipped cream or ice cream may be in order.

@Jen I had a similar experience. Baked the cake just over 55 minutes. It was getting very brown on top and the tester came out clean. Once out of the oven the cake fell and the center wasn’t fully baked.

Right now I’m debating whether the topping will glue itself to the bottom of my trusty springform pan.  I’ve already decided to forget the pears, and use ripe prune plums. I’ll slice them into wedges but will not be standing them on the bottom of the pan with the skin sticking up, but will lay them in a more normal pattern on their sides, perhaps approximating a Fibonacci spiral.

Delicious! Following everyone’s advice, I cut back both sugars by 60g or so. I ended up using 4 black plums, which probably weighed in around 1.5lbs. I also add extra cardamom because why not? Easy, unique and delicious

Made this cake as written. Used 4 1/2 very large plums from farmer's market found the amount of brown sugar to be too much causing a leak into oven floor so I will cut it in half next time. Baked the cake for 65 minutes which gave a deep brown to top but once the cake cooled and flipped, it looked amazing and the cake was moist, delicious and not too sweet. Easy to make especially if the butter is very soft which yielded a great thick whipped batter. Will certainly make it again and again.
Is there a type of plum that is preferable for this recipe?  Prune plums and larger plums cook quite differently!  Thank you!
The picture I believe shows the cut side of the plums facing up which I think means they should be cut side facing down before the batter is added?

I made this cake as indicated by the recipe. It was delicious, however, after I inverted the platter, the cake fell apart even though I used a round of parchment paper which was buttered.  
I would like to make this cake again, but I’m afraid the same catastrophe would befall me!
I like that the cake was not overly sweet. It was also very easy to make. I used regular plums.

I used the only organic plums they had at Whole Foods and they worked perfectly. They were hard to the touch, but when sliced thin, baked nicely to a soft texture. Also followed some tips from other reviewers; Decreased mixing time to 3 minutes, used a parchment paper circle at the bottom of the pan, and put the pan on a baking sheet. It would've been a disaster in my oven otherwise. I will definitely do this again! My brunch guests loved it!

Since I couldn't find Italian prune plums, I used the big purple plums and sliced them into thin wedges. It only took about 2 1/2 or 3 plums--nowhere near 2 lbs.  I reduced the brown sugar to half a cup and would probably reduce it to 1/3 c next time. I also reduced the sugar in the cake by half (2/3 c). The toothpick came out clean after about 35 minutes. The plums were nicely cooked and gooey. For me, it would be way too sweet with all of the sugar and I can't fathom baking it an entire hour.

in the article above and also on the day it was published it says any kind of plum.I used 6 medium size plums and I sliced them pretty thin because I didn’t read carefully.

i cut the sugar in half and it still seemed awfully sweet to me.

I think if i had beat it less than than suggested it would have been higher and a little fluffier.

My trusty springform leaked and since I stupidly didn’t put it on a sheet i soent some time scraping the oven.

All that said I thought it was delicious .

Made the recipe as instructed with the exception of using Bob’s Red Mill 1 to 1 Baking Flour to accommodate GF family. It was absolutely delicious. Met my Swedish craving for cardamom, without the hassle of making kardemumma bullar.

Excellent recipe. I would bake for 45 minutes and then test. My cake was in the oven for 55 minutes and on the dry side, though with excellent flavor from the cardamom.

I used Italian prune plums, and about 14 were needed to create a single-layer topping. I sliced each into 6 wedges. Next time, I'd add a few more plums.

I added about a Tsp cinnamon with the cardamom, and per other suggestions, put parchment down before the brown sugar/plums. It was really really good! It did take longer for the center to be done, but we may have beat it with the hand mixer too long. 
One idea for those that are saying it fell in the middle: When it says to sift the flour/baking powder/cardamom/salt together, I put those together all in a large measuring cup (or bowl or whatever you have). Then I sifted the flour mixture into the batter (Instead of sifting it into one container then dumping it into the batter as written, I actually sifted into the batter). Then all you need to do is give some good stirs & fold it all together. That may have helped keep it light and from collapsing. 
Who really knows. Baking is a fickle science! Especially in the dry high elevation Mountain West where I am!
Could you use prunes in the place of plums?

To Neil, those Italian plums work magic in the torte recipe—I’ve just made five since I’m overrun w plums and they were all successful, and one’s in the freezer!  I’m going to true this recipe w the same Italian plums.

I assume “pear” is an editing error (even though pears would work here). As to what kind of plum to use, refer to this, from the article:”…late-season plums, especially the little purple Italian ones.” In other words, any plum you find in the market this late in the season. Here, that would be black plums.

Nell, we've made the plum torte dozens and dozens of times, with all kinds of stone fruit. (My favorites are pluots and nectarines [separately or together] but YMMV.)

If your fruit didn't get cooked enough, I'd suggest double-checking the original recipe and making sure the oven is pre-heated. We've occasionally had very ripe fruit that soaked into the cake (for which the solution is to bake a little bit longer). Also, we use half or less the recommended amount of sugar, but YMMV.

Should the butter be melted or softened? I never used melted in a cake, so just checking…
Thanks!
I had trouble with the topping not carmelizing evenly.  Other recipes seem to call for melting the sugar with some butter before pouring into the pan.  Might need to experiment.
A great simple recipe but I have to say that even having reduced sugar by more than a 1/3 I find it wayyyy too sweet. I wonder if there is a difference in how sweet sugar is in US and Europe?

Sugar in the US and Europe are equally sweet. American recipes however tend to be much sweeter than European ones and many NYT Cooking readers cut down on the amount of sugar called for in a given recipe. Adjust the amount to suit your taste;

So disappointing!!  I threw it out.  The top became brown and crispy (so I covered it about 1/2-way through) but the center didn't cook through at all--even after an hour and 10 minutes and a clean toothpick in various spots.   Put a pan _with sides_ beneath the springform pan.  The sugar/juice leaked all over the oven--even with a sheet of tinfoil under it.  I will try again with a different "cake" recipe (but sub cardamom for cinnamon). Heed the warnings in other comments!!

I used large plums that were called apricot plums or something like that. I let them ripen in room temperature for a week. Then I peeled them:  boiled them for 4-5 minutes and immersed in cold water after that. The peels came off easily. Then I soaked the slices in lemon juice while making the cake. 
I reduced the brown sugar to a good 1/2 cup. Spread that in the pan and put the plum wedges on top. I also reduced the sugar in the cake by almost half. The cake was beautiful and delicious.
My son made this yesterday and we all LOVED it!! He made it exactly as written, and I’ll be making this soon myself to share with friends. Might add a tiny bit more cardamom.

Two questions before I dive into this recipe.  Planning to use Italian prune plums.  While the directions say skin side DOWN in the pan, the photo clearly depicts otherwise.  Should I just go with making a nice flesh side down pattern?
I do not own a stand mixer, only a hand mixer.  Pointers on if that will work, or go with a true hand whisking?
Thanks in advance!

Bake at 335 degrees in toaster oven
Add nutmeg, ginger and almond extract (½ t)
cut sugar by 60 g.
topping - 2/3 light and 1/3 dark brown and squeeze lemon over the plums once put in
slivered almonds around sides once plums are set in
spinkle slivered almonds on top when out of oven

It's giving giant muffin! Loved it. Reduced sugar by 60g in the sponge, used around 1/3 c. sugar to toss the plums and the sweetness was perfect for my guests who commented "its not too sweet!" Used small black plums and the color came out a gorgeous mix of pinks and purples. As I was prepping the batter I noticed the plum/lemon juices were leaking in the springfoam so I drained the pan before adding the batter. My resulting cake was a touch on the dry side, so not sure of that affected things.

I just finished eating the first bite of this cake and WOW - one of, if not the best dessert I've ever made. The plums get all jammy and caramelized and sit on top of a nutty spongey vanilla cake. I would recommend only cooking for 50 min at 350 as the cake bottom got a bit crackly but overall an incredible recipe. I didn't have cardamom but did 1/2 tsp of cinnamon and 1/2 tsp of ground nut meg and it's a delicious pairing.

Made the recipe as written. The cake is delicious, but similar to other comments, it took a while for the middle to be done (cake tester came out clean after about 1hr 20m at 350 for me). I would cut the brown sugar by half next time - mine ended up with hardened clumps among the plums (I probably packed it too tight to make the even layer), which made the topping sickly sweet in parts. TBH, if I were to make another plum cake, I’d probably stick with the NYT classic Plum Torte.

Definitely use parchment paper on the bottom or prepare to clean your oven.  Agree with earlier poster who said beating time was too long.  2-3 min.  I used plumcots vs plums and that worked well.  Couldn't taste the cardamon, alas.  It did go together quickly
I did not have cardamom, used cinnamon, nutmeg and ginger. It’s was delicious!

Flavors are good but the cake collapsed- maybe too much air whipped into it after 10 minutes of beating?  

Also 2 tsp of baking powder is a lot…

Plums caramelized nicely (although my oven is a mess! Can’t believe I forgot to put a sheep pan under the springform!) 

We had just the right number of plums to eat up (and not enough for the torte) so I might try it again if I’m in another I-have-7 plums-that-need-to-be-eaten-ASAP situation, but i need to figure out rising mods.

Delicious! Sweet yet adult taste imparted by the cardamon. I was inspired by red flesh plums I found at Costco; so sweet and juicy had to eat them over the sink. Anyway, followed the recipe as written but did put parchment paper in the bottom of the springform pan and a cookie sheet under to catch the dripping juices. Excellent!

title: Persimmon Salad With Glazed Walnuts and Feta Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: Scaled back by half and this delicious, healthy and beautiful salad was easy to make early Fall dinner for two. Too early for Fuyu here so swapped in local Bartlett but otherwise as written.  Added pinch of sugar to dressing.  Chose Valbreso brand for feta. First pass at nuts seemed too buttery so remade them using a little less butter and outcome was just right for crispy glaze.  Staying out of them before dinner was the challenge. ;-)
Delicious! Added about 1Tbsp honey to balance the dressing & will skip lemon juice next time & just add more balsamic vinegar- was quite tart. Great recipe for fall!
Could not find persimmons in Phoenix.  Plumcots were a good alternative.

Very good!  I used what I had except for the persimmon (only 1 though). Subbed baby arugula and spinach, sesame oil, red onions and premade salad topper of chopped walnuts and dried cherries.  I too thought the dressing was a tart and added 2 tsp of honey. Great flavor combination and love feta...used a bit more than the 1/2C.

Beautiful and delicious.  My guests all loved it! I doubled the  feta for the topping and it wasn’t too much and for the dressing used 2 T of balsamic in place of the lemon juice.  The walnuts are perfect in this dish just be careful not to burn them as I did my first batch!  (4 minutes of toasting was plenty My husband loved the burnt ones as an extra snack).   I had persimmons for this but I would make again with other fruit if persimmons are not available— pears or apples would be great.
Not a fan of bitter greens so subbed arugula & romaine. Left out red wine vinegar & doubled balsamic. Added pomegranate seeds.  Big hit!
So surprised no one said “I didn’t have walnut oil so I used …” I have everything on hand except walnut oil… any ideas? I have a decent selection of oils, but no walnut oil.

Terrific complement to a pot luck Thanksgiving feast. You get sweet, bitter, and salty and a colorful presentation that makes for plenty of oohs and aahs.  I did not use all of the walnuts, but our hosts were happy to keep the leftovers.  At the suggestion of others here, I only used balsamic vinegar - a rather light-weight one.  When that proved too acidic, I threw in a squeeze of agave and boom - perfection. Just do it!
I followed the recipe and it was delicious!
Salad was delicious despite no walnut oil. Made it with persimmons and sautéed walnuts in oil and no sugar. Definitely will make several times paired with roast chicken. .

a thanksgiving revelation. so great tasting and the various textures and colors made it special. love persimmons and adding them to other recipes now that i know what they are. be careful with those caramelized nuts though. they burn quickly!

I made this for Thanksgiving dinner and it got rave reviews.  Was told it was the perfect complement to the heavier Thanksgiving dishes.  I was able to find ripe persimmons at our local market, and added some baby red and green lettuces to balance out the bitter greens.  Definitely a keeper!
I added pomegranate seeds for another bright touch! I've made this for several events and it always gets way more attention than a side salad should.
This is a lovely salad and vinaigrette. The three acids in the vinaigrette yielded a satisfying dressing to the salad.  Thank you!
has anyone ever tried this salad with wild american persimmons?  there's not much fruit (mostly seeds) in each piece, but the flavor is excellent in the ones i scavenge in missouri.  just wondering.

title: Lemon Garlic Roast Chicken With Squash Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I recommend against using a cast-iron skillet (as I usually do) as a roasting pan for this dish.  Turmeric is soluble in oil and will penetrate the pore of the cast iron necessitating a lengthy clean up with soap and water and then reseasoning.
@Michael the acidity of the lemon juice will eat away the seasoning of a cast-iron skillet… Be careful unless your pan is very very well seasoned
@Mary No, it won’t. Acid does not eat through polymerized oil.
Unpeeled garlic cloves? How does that work?
@Jennifer. Break up a head of garlic. You’ll have many cloves. Now, leave them be with their papery skins on.
@SAF but why? doesn't the papery skin interfere with eating the garlic?
@itinerant veteran once the garlic cloves are cooked, they become soft and delicious! You just squeeze them out of the papery skins and voila!

So much fuss in the comments here!

I marinated the chicken in a bowl, bunged it on a sheet pan with the lemon slices--and (yikes!) unpeeled garlic--sprinkled the rosemary and some chili flakes over it (I use chili flakes like pepper if I'm roasting)

15 minutes in I added the squash to the side and stirred leeks into the chicken fat.

Cooked another 30 or so, sprinkled the parsley-lemon on and it was wonderful

(yeah I halved it to fit on one sheet pan)
I’m wondering about the “1/2 cup of rosemary leaves” striped from 6 springs. It can’t be 1/2 cup right? A TBS or two maybe. Hoping the author can clarify. Thanks! Looks yummy
I'm going to try this this weekend with delicata squash straight from my garden. I'm thinking that some crisped up sage leaves would work with this as well.
@Rebecca you’re on the money! It is a marriage made in heaven between squash and sage (with a little garlic thrown in to spice things up!)
Roasting the food with unpeeled garlic will more subtly flavor the dish. At the end, you can squeeze the roasted garlic out of its skin for a richer roast garlic taste.

@MCBrown   Rather than fishing out each individual unpeeled garlic clove when the meal is ready to be eaten, during prep, I think I’m going to peel most of the loose papery skin off of an entire bulb of garlic, cut off the top 1/4 or 1/3 of the bulb, invert the bulb so the flat side is making contact with the bottom of the roasting pan in a little olive oil and then when the chicken comes out of the oven, squeeze the roasted garlic cloves out of the bulb, all at one time.

@P R
That’s an excellent idea. Plus, it’s fun to squeeze the garlic.

You can squeeze the roasted garlic out of each the roasted cloves, like butter. It's pretty easy. I would prefer cut off the top of a head of cloves, and then just squeeze it all out at once and use a knife to spread it. Personal preference. The individual cloves seem a little fussy.
Way too sour. I'd cut the lemon jiice down a lot and maybe add zest to the marinade. Thinking about preserved lemons next time. The dish needs more lemon flavor and less acidity.
@David I made it as written and I totally agree! Perhaps lots less lemon and a little cream? Regardless, it smells amazing when cooked.

Why not roast squash with the chicken? Start the thighs, add the squash about halfway thru.  I did, using half a butternut squash, the whole thing cooked in 30 minutes (an hour for thighs is overkill, as someone noted).

I have made chicken this way for years but never used the tumeric or smoked paprika. Cannot wait to try. The roasted unpeeled garlic cloves add to the eating experience , slows you down and lets you enjoy the lovely melange of flavors, one for each bite of chicken.  Try it just once.

@Paulette Your answer is the one everyone needs to read! Each person eating can decide the amount of garlic they wish to consume and the peeling is easy, slows you down and is part of the experience! Well said!

Trusting David Tanis recipes, I followed this to the letter, omitting only some of the rosemary (a little rosemary goes a long way for me) and the parsley-lemon zest topping (because I forgot to buy parsley). The results were excellent. I especially liked how much the simple lemon marinade tenderized and flavored the chicken, and the roasted leeks, garlic, and lemon were delicious. Many thanks for this one.

Yummy! Everyone loved it. Based on above comments I used about 1/2 the lemon it calls for and I added chicken stock to the dish so it wouldn’t dry out, which created a great sauce. And the roasted garlic cloves are delicious on toasted sourdough!
Too much lemon flavor.  Next time I’ll cut the quantity of lemon juice and  slices in half.

I used two pounds of boneless chicken thighs which turned out wonderfully and made this a weeknight meal as they cooked in 30 minutes in a convection oven. I put the squash in after ten minutes. 1/4 cup of lemon juice was perfect, skipped the additional cut lemons. Used herbes de Provence since I had run out of rosemary.
Can this be made with chicken breasts instead of thighs?

I love lemon but this was overwhelming. Next time I will let the chicken marinade in the lemon juice and then toss it and roast. The dish is filled with strong flavors that mellow and the leek, garlic, squash, rosemary combo is good on its own.

I don't understand why Tanis recommends rubbing spices into the chicken skin and then dousing the thighs in lemon juice. Won't the juice and later addition of oil wash off the spices? I added the spices to the lemon juice along with a little olive oil and marinated the chicken in a bowl. I sliced two lemons and scattered them with some smashed (peeled) garlic. I subbed thick slices of shallots for the leeks (unavailable). On a sheet pan, everything was cooked in less than an hour.

I used two sprigs of rosemary on the chicken (a quarter-cup, max) and a half-sprig on the squash. One head-equivalent of garlic, peeled, went between the thighs. I only had one leek, so substituted a yellow onion, sectioned. We preferred the onion to the leek in the finished dish. One sliced lemon sufficed. I used bottled fresh lemon juice. A quarter cup would have been enough. I also added chunks of peeled Russet potato. They soaked up the juices on one side and roasted on the other. Delicious!
Why are the garlic cloves unpeeled? Are they eaten that way?

I'm also puzzled about this, I'm not whether we're expected to peel all the cloves, or to squeeze the soft, roasted garlics out of the skins - both a LOT of work. I'd just buy a jar of peeled garlic cloves.
Try with skinless, boneless chicken, butternut, halve the recipe, one sheet pan.

We made this with just 3 chicken thighs and 1 squash, and cooked everything on a single sheet pan. Gave the thighs, leeks, and garlic a 15 minute head start and then added the squash and continued roasting for another 30 minutes. 45 minutes total at 400 was plenty of time. Enjoyed all of the flavors with this relatively easy preparation.
Seconding Norma’s recent query below… Can this recipe be adapted to chicken breasts ?  If so, how? Not a fan of dark meat chicken.

@Xanthegirl.   Chicken thighs aren’t very dark any more.  Due to the way the birds are raised, and  the fact that they are brought to market younger than chickens used to be back in the day.  Thighs are better for most of these recipes as they can take longer heat and absorb more flavors.  Try some, you will be surprised.  I see a lot of comments like this.  Things - and thighs - have changed.

@Xanthegirl and thirding! With all due respect not looking for more opinions on how chicken thighs are “better.”  From my research (if we call googling that!) straight lemon juice may not be the best for breasts which is the main thing I’m questioning about making the swap.

@Xanthegirl. Come on everyone - Of course breasts can be subbed.  Probably even better with pork chops.  You can check the internal temp or judge by smell and a poke for doneness.  I usually find breasts cook about 15 minutes faster but depends on size, if bone in etc.   This is a pretty common roasting prep  -not rocket science!

Excellent, exactly as written. It won the dinner party! Wonderful served with crusty bread. I'm glad I left the garlic cloves unpeeled, but I did slice the top off each to make it easier to squeeze each out. Next time, I'll dice the rosemary and be more generous with the turmeric and smoked paprika. For those upset about the measurement of rosemary (1/2 cup), this is all the detail you need. A little more or a little less won't materially change the flavors in this recipe.

Wonderful lemony delight -- perfect for this little flash of summer in Oct. Just be sure you read the lemon parts carefully (juice/sliced/zest), as they're sort of confusing as written. We couldn't find delicata anywhere but kabocha worked great and had a similar edible skin and shape (butternut seems like it wouldn't quite go).

Used sliced butternut as couldn’t find delicate. Love the lemony flavor, halved the amount of garlic. Tossed the finished squash with arugula and spinach. Served chicken pan contents over squash and greens. Poured pan juices over the whole thing. The juxtaposition of the sweet butternut squash and the tart lemon juice was rich.
I wonder if preserved lemons might be a good addition - they give a different sort of lemon flavor. But how much to use and when to add it?
@deedub-sf i’m wondering the same thing too.
@sja when would you add them?  I’ve not cooked with preserved lemons before.
Why are they not being cooked at the same since the recommendation is to cook them on the same temperature and on separate racks?
probably should mention that laws of the universe dictate cutting squash into rings before you can seed them;)
Extremely sour and bitter, as written. As some other comments suggest, I would recommend swapping lemon juice for lemon zest in the marinade.
Waaaaaaaay too lemony. It ruined the chicken. Will definitely not make again.

Made it 2x. The first exactly as written and it was delicious. Then, I made it with pressed tofu with a couple of modifications. First, I made a marinade. I put the lemon juice, spices, peeled garlic, salt, pepper, and rosemary into a mini blender. I put the mixture into a ziplock with the pressed tofu for about an hour. Baking pan had lemon slices with leeks, etc, but baked at 400 for 40 minutes. Used butternut squash (with sage) & put baking sheet in lower shelf under tofu pan for 35 minutes. It was really tasty!

This dish came out beautifully and I was surprised that the thighs didn't get overcooked after an hour of roasting.  I would put the leeks underneath the chicken rather than on top so they don't get dry and brittle during the long roasting process.

I assembled the entire recipe, leaving it to marinade for several hours.  I substituted the rosemary for  Burlap and Barrel’s herbs de Provence and sprinkled twice as much olive oil on but half the lemon juice.  A roasting pan the size of the chicken thighs, rather than a sheet pan keeps the juices and marinade in resulting in a very moist and flavorful dish. It was roasted at 400 for about 50 minutes.  Quite delicious and will go into my favorites folder.
Michael, why not line your cast iron skillet with foil?
I wonder if you could adapt this for a whole chicken, spatchcock
How do you seed the squash and then cut into rings? Seems like you would have to seed each individually if they are rings.

I thought this also… Wondering how tedious is this going to be and if I should even try it? I think what I would do is cut the squash in half first take the seeds out and then cut the rings so they'll be half rings.

I would say don't seed them at all. I never do (with delicata), and the seeds become wonderfully crisp and totally edible. However, if you wish to de-seed the squash first, then unlike, say, butternut squash, where the fibers stubbornly hold onto the seeds, all you need to do is slice the squash into rings as directed and use the side of a spoon or a paring knife to do a once-around on the inside of the ring. The relatively small amount of fiber and seeds will release pretty easily.

You either cut it into slices first and scrape out each ring or cut it across the middle and scoop out the seeds from each half and then cut it into rings. The instruction made no sense as did the lack of instruction for prepping leeks. Also if you need six lemons for the marinade plus the slices that should be clear in the ingredients list while we're at it.

Way too lemon-y for me. Marinate in it.  Then chicken/lemon slices make an intensely lemony jus in the pan.  Then zest at the end. 
Could read: 5 lemons total, 1 zested, that one and 2 more juiced, 2 sliced thin.   Don't get all the unpeeled garlic.  Ok. You end up with roasted garlic.  But then all the skins on the plate, too?  Leeks , eh. Squash is plain.  Fine.  But put on top of the chicken?

I just came in from taking a few lemons off my tree here in California, and then tried to figure out how many lemons we are talking about. The recipe never specifies! As you say, seems like 5 to me also: 3 to juice, including 1 of those also to zest, plus 2 to slice. Have to go get 2 more! Thanks

I have made this 3 times. Beautiful dish with delicata squash festooning the plate, and deep lemon flavor. I noticed that guests hesitated to touch the roasted garlic so, the most recent time, I squeezed it out before serving and passed sliced baguette to spread the garlic. I have a Meyer lemon tree so I use lots of lemons. I marinate an hour in a bowl, cook on a cookie sheet, make earlier in the day, refrigerate until dinnertime and reheat at 350. The dish pairs well with a good Pinot Noir.

Made exactly as published. We had it for 3 meals and thoroughly enjoyed it each time.  My husband is a garlic fiend and was very happy.   After you pour off the extra oil, you have a lovely sauce. Really enjoyed the parsley and lemon zest sprinkled on top!
Great recipe! I added cinnamon, nutmeg and a little maple syrup to the squash and it was outstanding! Nice contrast with the tartness of the lemon chicken. Whole family loved it.

I made this as directed and it was wonderful! There wasn't any squash in the market so that was left out, and my parsley was faded so the gremolata didn't get made, but otherwise the flavors were bright and delicious.

Not sure why the turmeric and paprika (which I used) needs to be added before lemon juice and rubbed into the chicken? The justice will wash away the spices. Better to simply combine the juice and species in a bowl and toss the chicken—no need to rub the spices into the chicken—they won’t stay there anyway.
The photo shows whole rings of squash. Isn’t it easier to cut the squash lengthwise in half and then scoop out the seeds and then just have half moons of squash?
Loved it just the way it is written. Easy to prepare with lots of delicious lemony sauce to spoon over my wild rice. We all agreed that it needs to stay in the rotation!
An hour to cook the thighs makes the skin that wonderful crispy texture that we love. I think the combination of spices is perfection. Yummy!

Nice. However, the unpeeled garlic that diners can squeeze out of their skins seems a bit precious. I suggest peeling it. Alternatively, one could lop the head of garlic in half, top with olive oil, wrap in aluminum foil and cook along side the chicken. Then, squeeze the half cloves out on top of the chicken. It also might be better to thinly slice the leeks.

I made this with 5 chicken thighs but the total weight of the package was only 1.7 lbs so I halved all the other ingredients. I added a 1/4 cup of white wine drizzled with the olive oil. Would probably double that for more sauce next time. Overall LOVED this!

The flavors are delicious including the 1/2 cup of rosemary. The delicata squash was also delicious. 

However, I made adjustments. They did not work well. As follows: I used 3 lbs. boneless skinless thighs, peeled garlic, regular size leeks cut 1-1/2” long but still circular & reduced the lemon juice to 1/3 cup. Roasted in a 2-1/2 “ deep roasting pan for 60 minutes at 400 degrees F, the thigh temperature was 160 to 180 F, there was about 1/2 inch of liquid in the bottom of the pan, the garlic was still hard, the leeks were still hard. To cope, I poured the liquid, leeks and garlic into a saucepan & boiled to reduce liquid & soften the veg. The leeks cooked enough but the garlic only softened somewhat. The lemon slices never browned, they stewed. 

What did I do wrong? I’m guessing that the garlic needs to stay unpeeled, the leeks need to be less than 1 inch diameter. I don’t know why I had so much liquid at the end.  I would love to hear idea’s about that. Thanks.
Sounds like your experience mirrored mine. I suggest adding chicken stock after the lemon juice marinade and placing the leeks and garlic into the pan first, nestling the chicken on top.
And the tumeric will stain your hands and fingernails.

I think this is a flawed recipe. It was way too sour and lacked depth of flavor. Scattering the flavor components on top of the chicken added almost no interest to the dish. Were I to make it again I'd add chicken stock after the lemon juice marinade and put the aliums (alia?) in the pan first, nestling the chicken on top to produce a flavorful sauce. I'd also remove the skins from the garlic before serving.

We were not big fans of this dish. The lemon peel became so tart in the sauce that we took them out halfway through the cooking.
The delicata squash was delicious.
We might try this again, just adding some lemon juice before baking or squeezed on after.
My family doesn’t like rosemary. Any thoughts about a substitute? Thyme? Sage?
@GB tarragon maybe?  It should go well with these flavors. Otherwise oregano would work well too.

This recipe is perfection as written.  Roast for the full hour or you’ll miss out on the sticky caramelized lemon and crispy rosemary.  Bone in, skin on thighs were made for this type of cooking.  This came together so nicely. Enjoy!

I used an aluminum sheet pan for the marinating and the baking. The lemon juice reacted with the aluminum and discolored the pan, potentially leaching aluminum into the food. A glass roasting pan would’ve been better.
Sounded interesting, but really just a deconstructed tagine without dried fruit to balance the lemon's acidity. Flavors didn't blend well, and was way too sour. Disappointing.
Made with chicken breasts cut into thigh-sized chunks and it was absolutely delicious.

I wish I had more thinly sliced the leeks - for some reason they didn’t get quite as soft as I had hoped. I also used acorn squash in lieu of delicata since I couldn’t find any! Was still delish. I would actually say this is a fine weeknight meal. Although it cooks for an hour there is hardly any hands on prep. The garlic cloves aren’t even peeled! Essentially no chopping at all.
This is much easier to make if you cut the delicata squash length-wise first, scrape out the seeds, then cut it into half-rounds, rather than circles.  Looks just as pretty and tastes just as good!

This was not bad but if I made it again would skip the lemon slices, I felt they added bitterness. The squash was great, did half moons so it was easier to de seed. Having said that I won’t make again, there are too many other great chicken recipes on this site.

Delicious and easy.  Re: earlier comments, I did use somewhat less rosemary but did not find amount of lemon excessive.  Of course you need to adjust amounts based on weight of chicken you are cooking.  I did 1 lb. (3 normal-sized thighs) and so used about 1/6 cup of lemon.
lemon slices made it way too bitter

I really enjoyed this recipe.  Didn't have leeks, no matter, used thin sliced sweet onion. Most important part is the lemon marinade, it made the chicken succulent and tender. The little pillows of roasted garlic were amazing to schmear on the chicken at the table.  Best of all, I had abundant rosemary from my garden that gave the whole dish a fragrant flavor reminiscent of early fall days spent in Tuscany.  I'll make this again.

I was arguing with my husband if I should peel the garlic or not. It turned out that some of the husks came off naturally/easily and also it didn't bother me that the husks were in the dish. I enjoyed the creamy texture when the garlic is roasted in the husk. 
I couldn't find small leeks and used big size, cutting into half lengthwise. It turned out the layers got separated and some of them which didn't touch the oil got dried up. I should have, maybe, cut them in half crosswise.

Wow! This was fantastic. I used acorn squash - halved, cooked upside down for an hour on a separate sheet - because it was on hand and that worked very well. Otherwise cooked as written and highly recommended!
Meh. Squash was hard to eat with the rind on the delicate’s a had and the chicken flavor was fine but nothing to write home about. Roasted garlic was great on sourdough baguette
this was fantastic! we cut back on the lemon but otherwise followed the recipe. every bite was delicious.
Make sure the thighs finishing temperature is at least 190 degrees or they will be stringy and tough.
I’m thinking that delicata will last for awhile in the fridge.  I’d like to make this 3 weeks from now and delicata is available now but isn’t always.  Any thoughts?  Thanks.
@Sheila F I store delicata in a cool, dark closet for months! The dampness of the fridge would worry me…. But for a few weeks maybe?
There were no leeks at the grocery store so I substituted shallots. Also used acorn squash because no delicata. Delicious.
With all the lemon in this chicken, I recommend using a non-reactive pan such as stainless steel, glass, or ceramic. Otherwise with aluminum or cast iron you might leach out metal from the pan.
This was good. I wouldn’t describe it as “show-stopping,” but solid.

I made a version of this, using an onion cut into wedges in place of leek and lemon garlic marinated chicken breast from Wegmans.  Really delicious. And easy. Dinner in half hour, had red chard sauté with it.

Wouldn't give this a 5. For all the work, the flavor was lacking. I would make it again, but give it some "heat."

I would not use 2" pieces of leek, really not use them at all. Maybe spring onions, or green onions.

Disappointed.

@MCBrown the problem is that the number of lemons called for is very unsubtle.  I peeled the cloves and didn't notice their flavour unless directly in my mouth because everything just tasted like lemon.
Can you use chicken breast for this recipe?

Yes. But it will be done sooner than thighs.

Cook to 160 - 165 F internal temperature.

You can use chicken breast for sure, the issue is how tricky it is not to dry it out to shoe leather.  Chicken thighs are delicious and much more forgiving.  You can roast them until their skins crisp up deliciously and still have moist tender chicken underneath, 

And this recipe is also lovely with potatoes rather than squash.  Roasted lemony potatoes cooked with the chicken juices and lemon are amazing.

Loved this. Added small potatoes to the squash. The veg only needed about 20 minutes, and the chicken needs to rest, so I recommend adding the veg in the last 15 minutes (rather than 30), and removing the chicken to rest while the veg finishes. Delicious!
Mimaw thought it was too lemony.
The chicken was delicious.  Some of the garlic did not roast and the leeks were dried out.  Followed the recipe.  Any suggestions?

I used preserved lemon slices instead of regular. No leeks so I used a sweet onion cut instead of leek slices. Was super yummy. Also used acorn squash and roasted instead of delicata. Super reviews from family.

Too lemony for our taste, and the leeks weren't cooked enough to be palatable. I'll try again, but sub chicken broth for half the lemon juice, and skip the slices. Will also put the leeks and garlic UNDER the chicken and in the liquid so that they can braise and the chicken skin can potentially crisp up.
Lemony goodness, served with rice. Thigh skins didn't get crackly crips enough. Would recommend crisping in the pan first or firing up the broiler at the end.

Made it as directed - it was lovely.  Lightly lemony, the garlic I squeezed out of the peel and ate like butter and the amount of rosemary was yummy.  It does seem that people who are concerned about some of these things have not made the recipe yet.  Try it and then adapt if you need to.  Myself, I wouldn't change anything.

Wonderful! I reduced the recipe for two people, subbed a couple of green onions for the leeks (added them after 30 minutes), and peeled the garlic cloves. I put each thigh on a slice of rustic bread to add some bulk and absorb the delicious pan juices, but it wasn’t needed thanks to the squash. Next time I might cut the garlic cloves crosswise for a garlic-in-each-bite experience.
Great dish. Served with Brown rice to soak up all the juices.
We just made this a couple of days ago and enjoyed it twice. This is one of the best chicken recipes we have ever made! (We made it in a glass Pyrex dish)
Excellent!!

Not a great recipe.   The flavors don’t meld together and the lemon shows its bitter side in this approach, while the papery garlic cloves and the huge rosemary leaves create texture issues at the table.  And I worked with outstanding ingredients: fresh farmer chicken that was never frozen, organic leeks and squash directly from a regional organic producer, home grown rosemary… I followed the instructions exactly, too.

Fundamentally, this recipe is trying to do something that would work perfectly well in a Le Creuset with a lot less lemon, or using desalted preserved lemon.  The idea was clearly to do a winter vegetable dish (roast chicken with leeks and squash), but then deconstruct it and introduce Moroccan spices to get a Raj al Hamout effect.  It fails because the entire concept doesn’t hold up well on a baking sheet at 400F.

Very sorry, but for me this was a classic example of an inadequately tested dish that was inspired by ones that work.  You really need to try your own recipes a bit more systematically.
The recipe doesn't explain what part of the leeks to use...? Greens? Whites? Both?

Prepared as directed.  This is simply superb.  The lemon/smoked paprika/garlic/turmeric combination is unusual and contrasts well with the sweetness of the squash.  One caveat: next time I will peel the garlic cloves.  The extra couple of minutes will be worth not having to pick inedible bits of garlic husk off my plate.  David, what were you thinking?
If you peel the garlic coves, they will burn before the chicken is cooked.
If you can’t find delicata you can sub with white acorn squash. Delicious!
So, the cooked chicken sits around getting cold for 30 minutes while the squash cooks?  Is that right?
No. You put the chicken in the oven. Thirty minutes later put the squash in the oven. Total cooking time one hour.
The recipe clearly says 6 lemons

Halve the lemon juice. Put most of the leeks under the chicken. use rosemary and sage. Add the peeled garlic cloves with brussels sprouts lightly coated in EVOO or the pan juices for the final 20 minutes of roasting.

I wouldn’t make this again. It was a little complicated to make; that would have been OK if the overall presentation and flavors were exceptional, but they were not. I didn’t enjoy eating the lemon slices with the rest of the meal. I didn’t enjoy having to extract the garlic from the cloves. The delicata squash was hard to slice. Also, the delicata squash is not particularly tasty, unlike acorn, or butternut squash. Just, really, why bother?

Everything works beautifully! We won’t change a thing the next time we make this! The unpeeled garlic cloves cook throughly, and the skins are not a problem. I would suggest trying it the way it’s written, and you can always tweak that another time if it is an issue for you. Very well worth it absolutely delicious!

My family scarfed up the chicken and left behind the onions (I used shallots and vidalias as I didn’t have leeks), garlic, lemons, and some squash. I roasted more squash and put it all together with veg broth for a fantastic soup. Two meals!

I made this for the “day-after-Friendsgiving” dinner for 12. Recipe doubles easily and was fine with boneless thighs, roasted at same temperature for ~30-40 minutes. The big reveal, though, was that it tasted AMAZING the next day! A great combination of flavors and textures that benefited from the extra day to become more than the sum of its parts. Too bad we had so little left over 😋.

I’m looking forward to making this again with fewer lemon rounds and more leeks. I’ll also cook it for less time as the leeks burned and stuck to the pan. A smidge of chicken stock may fix that. The chicken was cooked perfectly, though!
I forgot to mention, next time I would not pour the lemon juice over the tops of the chicken, but let them sit in the marinade.

This was completely delicious. One tip…no need to add the squash after 30 minutes. I added mine in the beginning and well oiled….some under the chicken some not and they were caramelized and so tasty. Will make again and again. The turmeric and paprika so good together and gave the chicken a deep bronzed color.
Very tasty and easy. I used roasted garlic cloves which I added the last 15 mins or so of cooking. 35 mins was a long enough cooking time. I didn’t bother with the squash.  I will cook it again.
@Jennifer   Yes it does. After roasting, the cloves slip easily out of their jackets and are melt in the mouth delicious and mild in taste.

This was outstanding. Made it as written and wouldn't change a thing. I might have just lucked out with the garlic variety I had, but the skins were so thin once I got the main outer skin off, the remaining skins were soaked in the juices and so soft they were totally edible. highly recommend as a dinner for guests that delivers a lot of wow, but is really pretty easy.

Terrific flavors, and I’ll definitely make it again, but there are a few odd notes in the instructions. 

It calls for six lemons, one of which is zested but then not juiced; that’s a waste. I found that only four lemons were needed; one zested then juiced, one juiced, two sliced. It also tells you to seed the delicata twice, once before slicing it into full rings when it’s still whole (good luck with that!) and again after slicing. Leaving the delicata in rings effectively cuts the amount of squash you can roast on one pan in half; cutting the rings into half moons fixes that. The skins of our delicata were also unpleasantly hard, possibly because there wasn’t enough olive oil on them. 

Leaving such a large amount of rosemary leaves whole on top of the dish meant there were large, sharp, unpleasant spears of herbs in every bite. A smaller amount should be minced and added to the marinade, with whole sprigs placed underneath the chicken with the lemon slices. And marinating the chicken on a sheet pan instead of a bowl minimizes the depth of flavor in service of not washing one mixing bowl.
I made this as per the recipe, just substituted thyme for the rosemary as I was out of rosemary. It was exceptional. Can't wait to make it again.
for the amount of work, this recipe was a bust.  granted we used boneless skinless thighs and acorn squash, so some subbing, but adjusted cooking time accordingly.  really not interesting at all.
Made the recipe as written except cooked the delicata rings around the chicken in the same roasting pan. We thought it was perfect! Our pasture raised chicken thighs needed the full hour.

Great recipe, but the rosemary and lemon notes were way too strong for my tastes. Unless you serve the dish with a whole lot of rice or add a few more Delicata squash those flavors are overpowering. 1/2 a cup of rosemary stems? I’d cut it to 1/4 cup or less.
I found this recipe to be a total disappointment.  It was relatively bland, and definitely not worth 5 stars. .
very Lemony and moist.  didnt feel like the leeks did much, but the softened, roasted garlic was nice.

This was perfect! At the end I pulled the garlic cloves out and squeezed them out of the skin, then stirred everything together and squeezed a lemon over the top. The next time I might add in some chopped spinach or arugula for the last 10 minutes.
I used acorn squash since delicata was not available and butternut squash is so time-consuming to peel.  It was great.
The worst NYT recipe I’ve ever tried.  I had misgivings about cooking thighs for an hour but I assumed that you knew more than I did.  You didn’t!!   They were grotesquely overcooked and inedible.

title: Flan de Coco (Coconut Flan) Recipe
author: David Tanis

stats.Cook Time: 1 hour 25 minutes, plus cooling and chilling

comments.commentBody: You can make your own sweetened condensed coconut milk by combining a 13.5 oz. can of coconut milk and 1/4 cup of sugar in a saucepan. Over medium heat, mix together and bring to a low boil. Once it starts boiling, reduce the heat and let the mixture simmer rapidly for about 45 minutes, stirring frequently. It will thicken as it cools. Best to do it the day before.
@John M. I’m assuming that you need to double this recipe to have the amount of condensed coconut milk needed for the recipe.

My abuela made the best flan period. She would make the flan mixture as described in this article, but first she would get an empty full size coffee can and pack about 1 1/2" of shredded coconut at the bottom. She would drizzle her caramel mixture over the coconut would add a wallop of Jack Daniels for effect, then pour her flan mixture over this. She would take her can to the backyard where she would place the can in a ring of hot charcoal and cook for a few hours. truly perfect flan heaven
I enjoyed this recipe. the only thing I would change is add a little bit of vanilla extract.

Carmel has a number of challenges for many home cooks including matching the right consistency for the recipe. I find that the most helpful thing to get it right is knowing the temp to be reached. It's disappointing that this recipe doesn't state the temp; my sense of "dark brown" may be your amber, etc. Rick Martinez's recipe for Flan in NYT Cooking calls for a temp of 360 for "dark amber." Is that right for this recipe? At any rate, knowing the temp is really helpful.

@David C this was my first time ever making caramel and I had no problem whatsoever without a thermometer. This is not fancy caramel for candy making, you just coat the bottom of the pan. All I needed was patience (it took at least 15 minutes, maybe more) and to roll my saucepan around every few minutes.

this was phenomenal. I couldn't find condensed coconut milk so used regular instead. honestly one of the best things I've ever made. skipped the toasted coconut. will make again soon. love that it's GF as well. Highly recommend!!!!!
I added vanilla bean to the custard for a little zing!

I finally got around to making this flan and it was delicious. The coconut flavor was just right. I only had a 9" cake pan so it was flatter than with an 8" pan. I used a tsp. of vanilla extract thanks to a suggestion here. The next time I make caramel, I'll leave the heat on medium throughout the cook. Thanks, Mr. Tanis.
P.S. I don't know why this recipe hasn't made it into the NYT Cooking newsletter. It would get a lot more eyes that way.

Wow wow wow. Made this 4 times over the holidays and it got rave reviews! I used both coconut condensed milk as well as regular dairy condensed milk - both were great! I didn’t want to store egg whites so I actually used the whole eggs. As others commented, definitely add vanilla - I also threw in a dash of cinnamon! Served this with fresh berries and homemade chocolate whipped cream. Definitely plan to make again when I don’t feel like baking!
Would it be possible to substitute the sweetened condensed coconut milk with sweetened condensed milk and coconut extract?
Attracted by dairy-free title only to find its got eggs in it, sigh :-(
Eggs are dairy-free.
How long will this keep refrigerated?

I was invited to a dinner and asked to bring a gluten free dessert. This fit the bill.  I had to make it twice as the first time I used evaporated milk by accident (it was sitting in my pantry.) It actually came out ok- but lacked sugar since evaporated milk is unsweetened. On the second round I used all the right ingredients but substituted sweetened condensed regular milk instead of sweetened condensed coconut milk since I couldn’t find it in the store.  It was a winner. Everyone loved it and most had seconds. I will put this in my rotation as it was an easy win.  We are enjoying the failed attempt and eating it for breakfast!

Just made this and am so happy to have a dairy free flan!! You can get sweetened condensed coconut milk online if you don’t have local Asian markets. I found the batter be too sweet for my taste so I add a small can of cream of coconut. (NOTE: VIP to make sure that none of the coconut products you use have thickeners added). Also, following other reader suggestions, I added a teaspoonful of vanilla and a little bit of cinnamon. The sweetness level and texture were absolutely perfect. This can feed a crowd because it’s so rich. I even made a mess of my caramel when I poured it in my pie pan and it still came out wonderfully!

I split this into 6 ramekins to make individual servings. Like some others, I had a hard time finding sweetened condensed coconut milk so I used regular condensed mile along with the unsweetened coconut milk. The flavor and texture was still great. Next time I'll try John M's suggestion to make my own sweetened condensed coconut milk.

Followed the recipe to the letter and cooked it in a water bath at 350. I strained the mixture through a superfine mesh twice to make sure it would be smooth but it came out grainy and curdled. Inedible. Not sure what went wrong.
What kind of caramel sauce goes on top?

title: Sea Bass With Garlic Chips and Salsa Macha Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I made this because I have a jar of Trader Joe’s Salsa Macha that I am trying to use up so I can’t rate this salsa macha but I can say the fish cooked as directed topped with TJ’s salsa macha, fried garlic, lime and cilantro was a hit.

I made this tonight and it was fantastic! I swapped almonds for the peanuts and used branzino filets. I also used 2 guajillo and two Hatch chiles, as that’s what I had on hand.  The prep took nowhere near two hours, as another poster mentioned in their comments. Will definitely be making again in the near future as much delicious salsa is left.

Though it may be obvious, step 2 doesn't mention about taking the said ingredients out of the oil. Peanuts and garlic chips may be easier, but sesame seeds, kind of difficult without the proper utensils. All three items will cook very quickly in hot oil.

Step 9 doesn't mention the peanuts or sesame seeds. Very confusing!

The cooled oil with peanuts and garlic and sesame seeds in Step 2 is the "salsa mixture" from Step 4. That oil, with peanuts/garlic/sesame seeds in it, goes into blender with chilies and becomes the salsa.

There are two sets of frying being done here, in two separate pans. To make the salsa matcha, you lightly fry peanuts, garlic, and sesame seeds. You take that pan off the heat and let it cool down. Then, as stated in Step 4, you add the prepared chiles to the now-cooled oil, seeds, and peanuts, and blend all to a rough purée with more oil. That’s your salsa. You then cook more garlic in a separate pan of oil, remove the cooked garlic, and fry the fish in the garlicky-flavored oil.

This was very good, both flavorful and a bit spicy.  But a lot of work making the salsa, the garlic chips, and coating a frying the fish.  I was a professional chef at one of the busiest seafood restaurants in New Jersey, and it took me two hours to make this.  I'll make it again because of all of the leftover dried peppers.
Not sure I want to buy rice flour just for this. Can I use all regular flour?
I thought it was all straight forward and not hard and the results are fabulous.  I’ve made it twice to much applause.

title: Avocado-Black Bean Tostadas Recipe
author: David Tanis

stats.Cook Time: 30 minutes, plus chilling and cooling time

comments.commentBody: I do the pickle differently since I find simmering in the brine makes the jalapeño & onion too soft, bordering on mushy. Bring the brine to a boil until salt & sugar (I only use a t spoon) dissolve and pour over the veg since I like a bit more texture. Tostadas are so easy to make & versatile - even here in the ethnic wasteland of Vermont my local supermarket has premade (in Mexico no less) tostadas saving one step. Even easier to put the grilled fish on the top...
@Gale Glad you mentioned adding grilled fish.  I agree … it’s the missing link.

Tostadas are so versatile and healthy. Toppings to your liking! We simmer our beans with onion, garlic, peppers in veg broth before mashing. Also we toast our corn tortillas in the toaster oven just to chewy, eliminating the oil. Grated parm is my go to for cheese and whipped greek yogurt to sub for sour cream
I forgot to save the liquid from beans so had to add water. Very tasty and easy recipe. The lime and pickled onions are absolutely essential!

I made this for lunch today for myself and my 92-year-young father.  I used a quesidella cotija, which melted atop the refried black beans.  Then I layered as directed, topped with cottage cheese, lime juice and cilantro  It was very flavorful, but a bit difficult for dad to chew.  I may have overdone the tostada by a minute or two.
Mezzetta brand pickled red onions are fantastic and make this meal come together so quickly. It’s delish and I’ve added it to our weekly rotation. Chef’s kiss!

If you want good ready to use beans, go intot he latin American aisle. You will see many brands either pouches or cans, look for the dark green ones. The brand you want is called "DUCAL" they sell both red and black variety. they're delicious right out of the package.

This was so fire. How could it not be? Zapped some apple cider vinegar, sugar, and salt in the microwave before shoving my onions and jalepeños in the jar. Now that I have a big jar with the pickled veg, these will be a go to until I run out! And then I'll make more. Because it was so so good.

I make this all the time without the tortilla. Sometimes if I have too many tortillas in the fridge (it happens), I pan fry them and crumble them over the black beans. This recipe is so adaptable to different proteins and quick pickling different veggies. Or not. Plus I just can't have enough black beans in my life.
Refried pinto beans, a good cheese, shredded lettuce and, most importantly, a great, tomato, pequin pepper salsa make the BEST tostadas. IMHO.

The pickled onions and jalapeños made this outstanding.  Perfect example of an easy to make condiment that takes an otherwise straightforward dish to the next level and can be used in other dishes.  I skipped queso fresco (used a little sharp white cheddar instead) and mashed seasoned and warmed black beans with a little liquid from the can to sub for refried.  Ideal quick(ish) lunch.
I saw this recipe yesterday and couldn't stop thinking about it, so I just made one for myself close to midnight after my earlier dinner. Yum. Totally worth it.
Add carrots
My tortillas come out crispy on the ends, but tough in the middle. What could I be doing wrong?
MIne did the same.  If anyone else has a suggestion, it would be appreciated.

These were delicious.  I did find baking the soft corn tortillas didn’t make them crunchy enough to easily cut and had to resort to picking these up and eating them with my hands.  

I appreciated the tip from Gale to pour the boiling pickling liquid over the onions and jalapeños vs. cooking them in it.  I loved the touch of cinnamon it leant to them.  I also only used a teaspoon of sugar and salt vs. a tablespoon in the brine.

I sliced the avocados so they lay nicely on the refried black beans and I cubed the queso fresco to get a nice salty bite as one eats the finished tostada.

I love this recipe so much! It’s just right, and my family and I love this dinner. It’s hard finding recipes and restaurants as a vegetarian myself, but this recipe? Just right. The awesome sweet and salty part of this dish is so notable. Don’t think about this dish. Just give it a try!

Kids and adults in our household love the build-your-own aspect of this dish. Everyone gets involved and makes for a great family meal.  In addition to the suggested toppings, I always serve with a simple homemade roasted salsa verde - tomatillos, garlic, onion, serrano, and cilantro
Black beans mashed, flavored as indicated. Tad bit more nutritious and many places near me offer black means in various prostrations in place of re-frieds.

I found the tostadas in local Stop & Shop supermarket in Int’l aisle saving me time. I pickled the onions with part of a Serrano pepper, white vinegar & poured boiling water over them. Substituted a single serve of plain guacamole. Heated the refried beans in a saucepan & used a sprinkling of shredded Mexican 4 cheese on top with cilantro leaves. My amounts made 2 tostadas. Will make more later this week.
Excellent exactly as written.
Great recipe! Fast, delicious, and it could be endlessly varied with toppings.

Made this yesterday but did a few alterations. I added some shredded cabbage to the pickle and did not cook it as long as the recipe suggested. Was delicious. Daughter is a vegetarian but sauteed shrimps would be great with this. Good recipe.

title: Madhur Jaffrey’s Goan Shrimp Curry Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: We make a similar sauce and freeze it in small ziploc bags. Then cook any protein and/or veggies in the instant pot and then add the sauce. Simmer for a few minutes and the meal is ready.

@LTF
What a brilliant idea!

@LTF I am from goa and we make this dish almost everyday. I like your shortcut but you need to cook the frag shrimp in the coconut sauce to infuse the whole dish with it. Same with fish. This isn’t a recipe for meat or vegetables for which there are others.
How about freezing it in glass tupperware to avoid all the single use plastic clogging our oceans?

Hi I am from goa and we make this dish everyday but you need to add tamarind not lime. Tamarind gives it that sweetish sour taste so essential to it. The shrimp has to be fresh and we often use well washed shrimp heads too so the whole dish is infused with shrimp. We do the same for shrimp and rice.
Tamarind isn't easily found across many supermarkets (even in their international aisles).
@helga gomes how much tamarind? I have tamarind paste I use for pad Thai. Thank you!
@Curious cook just a teaspoon or a small ball will do.

Such a delicious dish! I always make sure when making things with shrimp to completely peel them before cooking, removing the tails also (which are oddly left on for the very pretty photo of this cooked dish here) because no one should have to paw through their hot food and remove shrimp tails as they are eating. I wouldn’t change a thing - the spices are perfect and it’s such a comforting and homey curry I plan to enjoy often!
@Christian, I agree. That said, the shells and tails when cooked in broth add a remarkable level of shrimp flavor. Compromise might be  tails off but added, to be removed before serving?

@Spring blooms

Some people eat the tails! My mom loves them. Crunch crunch.

@Christian another compromise, if buying shrimp with shells or just tails would be to shell and de-tail them and cook those in the liquid tied in cheese cloth. This would impart the flavor and be easily removed before serving.

This is an excellent quick dish with not too many steps or ingredients.  I am looking forward to making this sauce with tofu or chickpeas (and spinach) as a vegetarian dish.   Over the years I have learned much about Indian cooking from Madhur Jaffrey.  She is a treasure!
Subbed onions for the shallots and McCormack’s chili powder for the Kashmiri version. The dish was delicious!

I made it last night and I wanted to say, as other commenters from the region of the recipe have mentioned, this recipe is authentic. I wanted to also add that it's just very well written. These  generational household dishes are almost like creative writing assignments (open-ended) passed down from cook to cook and creating a list of steps to recreate them requires talent. I followed the steps and got the desired dish that was promised. This can be hit and miss with such recipes. For those who are criticizing the recipe, I would say that it's not the recipe but the dish itself is just one that wasn't to your tastes and that's fine, we all have foods we like and dislike. So happy this recipe was written, thank you Madhur Jaffery.
This is my wife’s favorite dish. The only thing i change is i add pre cooked cubed sweet potato.
Could you make it even easier by just adding a good curry powder to the coconut milk instead of the spices listed?
@Roberta It will be fine but will change the taste of dish.  Instead I would add ginger and garlic coarsely ground with a green chili like Serrano.
So easy and so delicious. Will definitely make again.
I don’t tolerate coconut milk well. What if I substituted heavy cream?

@Joel. 
By all means! I make the same recipe Ms Jeffrey introduced in QUICK AND RASY INDIAN COOKING! Which permits heavy cream and curry leaves) whic add heaps of flavor.

What if you pan fry the shrimp with the shallots instead of cooking them in the coconut milk which sounds like it would poach them. You could add them back in at the end. I don’t want the shrimp to be too soft.
If you cook the shrimp in the liquid it will impart more flavor to the dish overall, and if you cook it long enough, the shrimp will not be too soft.
I’d have to use mild smoked paprika. Can’t have ultra hot foods.   Wondering about the comment to use curry powder instead of the other spices

@john golden, Camden Maine. Curry powder is not at all the same thing (or composed of) the spices listed.  You can use a little curry powder and add salt and black pepper, garlic and ginger root.  I would also squeeze a 1/2 a lime.  That would be good too, but would not at all be the same flavor as what is intended with these spices. Good luck, let us know how it turns out!

@john golden, Camden Maine Kashmiri chile powder is not fiery hot (the food of Goan is, to my understanding, not as spicy as some other Indian regions) and it is available online. I doubled the spices when I made it tonight because with the spices as specified it was very pale and bland. The heat is a very low medium, or a slightly elevated mild.
Can anyone speak to the heat level of this dish? It sounds amazing and simple but I have some folks who can’t eat anything bordering on spicy.
@Lori leave out the hot ingredients- use garlic and freshly grated ginger instead.

@Lori
It was WAY TOO HOT for me. I order mild at Indian restaurants. Sad.

@Lori I used the Kashmiri chiles and left out the cayenne. It was a very low medium or slightly elevated mild. Cayenne offers mostly heat, so use it to whatever level you can tolerate. I doubled all the red other spices because it was very bland initially.

Was looking for something vegetal to round out this recipe, and added a pound of green beans to blister in the pan while frying the shallots in the beginning.  Great addition!!  Great recipe and very simple.
@Val - I added leftover green beans from last night’s dinner and was excellent.
Love the shrimp, but my wife is a vegetarian.  I bet this would go great with cauliflower and chickpeas.

Made this 3 times already. The first time was with the suggested lemon juice. It was ok, but missing something. The subsequent 2 times I used tamarind paste instead of the lemon juice. Absolutely changed the recipe for the best. Suddenly there was a depth of flavor previously missing! Great for weeknight dinners.
Is there a substitute for coconut milk. I’m allergic to it
@Clara, If you’re allergic to coconut, surely in the meantime you‘ve found out what your alternatives are, no?
Have you tried cashew milk or paste with water added? Or other ground nuts like walnut or almond.  I think you could also try dairy milk/half and half if you are able to eat dairy.

@OnWingsofSaffron I didn’t write the question, but while dairy is an obvious substitute I’m not sure how or when one would add it, since this recipe calls to simmer the coconut milk, and using dairy in the same way would cause it to curdle. Just to say I think it’s a reasonable question and it looks like I’m not the only one who has it.
Loved it! Made as instructed, but simmered the shrimp shells in the coconut milk and then strained before adding to the spice paste.

I am a huge fan of Madhur Jaffrey! I was able to meet her when I took a cooking class in London and have all her cookbooks and just love Indian food so much sadly, my husband does not but this recipe was perfect. He loved it. My kids loved it. It’s easy it takes no time at all and not spicy but good flavor. I doubled the amount of shrimp just because I have hungry eaters and I served it with jasmine rice and fresh, chopped cilantro and wedges of limes. 
This is a perfect recipe for those people who aren’t into curries, but you want to get them into curries. My husband is not a fan but he loved this recipe. He asked when I was gonna make it again and my kids loved it. It’s amazing quick easy perfect for a busy school night. .

I just have one essential question about this recipe: How can something so simple taste so outrageously good? Followed the recipe with two minor changes. Because I had  no Hot Paprika I used a mix of Smoked Paprika and Hot Chili Powder. I also added some frozen green peas with the shrimp, which played nicely with the sweetness of the coconut milk. I could honestly eat a vat of this!

Wonderful recipe! Added a tablespoon of tamarind concentrate to the paste when preparing. Threw in a bit of ground coriander too. If you do these steps, definitely taste before adding the lemon - tamarind is sour!

FYI - the sauce is even better if you prep in advance. I prepped the sauceearly afternoon, refrigerated, then reheated and added the shrimp - it was amazing!

I buy shrimp with shells (incluing the heads).  It's easy to peel the shrimp, and make a broth using water, carrots, black peppercorns, onions, celery, carrots and garlic and simmer for at least a couple of hours on low heat.  Strain and freeze the broth. It's great for seafood pastas, grain and rice dishes.
The Goan spice, which my neighbor brings to me from Goa, xacuti, also enhances this curry, and cilantro, always my go-to green for curries! Just planted a bed for next year....Great cook Ms Jaffrey.

I make a dish very similar to this (that also has carrots and peas in it). One thing to note, rice that actually comes from India has a better flavor and texture than ones that are domestic. Just my opinion.
Quite spicy yet boring. Added lots of lemon and salt. Still boring.
Can anyone suggest a substitute for the coconut milk? Sadly, I am allergic.
@Jan Cashew paste or cream would be good substitutes
Cream.

Substitutes for coconut milk: soy milk, oat milk, or cashew milk.  Possibly dairy products: cream, evaporated milk, or plain yogurt (but they may curdle especially with the spices.) I love to use silken tofu whisked to a thinner consistency.

When I have the time, I buy unpeeled shrimp. I peel the shrimp and add the shells to the simmering oil until they turn bright pink then fish them out before adding the shallots/onions. This fully flavors the resulting sauce with shrimpiness. NB there is more flavor in the shells than in the shrimp itself.
Slightly blistered a pound of green beans along with the shallots before adding listed spices, coconut milk and finally the shrimp.  Such a delicious and simple meal over basmati rice!
@Val I’ll try that next time, thanks.

In Goa, people hardly cook with lemon. The sour is got using Kokum or tamarind (raw mango when in season). Jaffrey does realize that her western audience do not keep Kokum or Tamarind or raw mango in their pantry and may find it difficult to get, so she uses Lemons. The only Goan ingredient here is Coconut milk which is present in most South Indian cooking.

In a pinch, can I use
Cooked shrimp?
@Catherine yes, though you will lose the shrimp flavor they add to the sauce. Just throw them in long enough to heat through.
I am seventy and this recipe proves the KISS rule. Keep it simple, stupid. Madhur Jaffrey is a food hero, her curry bible is tops and her World Vegetarian is a masterpiece.

That's a very stripped down version of the recipe which would be pretty bland. Sizzling fresh curry leaves and mustard seeds in hot oil for a minute before adding onions for 8 mins, ginger for 2 misn and garlic 1 minute before adding toasted and freshly ground cumin and coriander together with the chile powder. Fry for 2 minutes then add 200g chopped tomatoes, 400g coocnut milk and tablespoon of vinegar and sugar to taste. Simmer for 20 minutes then add fish and cook for a further 5 minutes.

Fab easy party dish. Double like a dream. 
1. Use shrimp shells and tails: infuse them in the coconut mike for 30 minutes on a low simmer. 
2. Toast spices well in the onion mix.
