3. Cilantro stems and roots, ( if your cilantro comes with) tied into gauze can be thrown in when the coconut milk is added, or with the coconut/shrimpbits to infuse. 
4. Add some fresh spinach at the last minute. Again, that’s just me. 
5. I use lime rather than lemon.
6. Serve with cilantro lime rice. Toast naan and a radicchio salad with orange, almonds and fresh herbs tossed with olive oil and Maldon or other flake Salk.
I was slightly disappointed with this dish, yes it was quick and easy, it lacked depth of flavor. I’ve saved the little remaining sauce and coconut milk for an improvised fish dish tomorrow.
This is a BANGER! Just loved it.
Since there are only two of us, I used half the shrimp but kept the same amount of sauce. For the sauce, I sauteed ginger with the shallots. I also added spinach to the sauce right before the shrimp.
You eat the shrimp tails
Quick and Easy. Not a look a character. Most Goan curries have a few more spices.

Purists may cringe, but I had only a half-pound of shrimp. Sauteed 4 oz. cubed paneer with the shallots for extra protein then made remainder of recipe as written (plus stirred in 2 teaspoons tamarind sauce). Served over spiralled steamed butternut squash and brown basmati rice. So easy and so delicious and so affordable! Plus disproved the myth that dairy and seafood can't mix - the sauce was equally kind to all ingredients and the total was more than a sum of its parts.

Enjoyed it as written. But I wanted some sweetness. On the second go I added a small finely chopped tomato and half a teaspoon of sugar.  The satisfaction over effort ratio of this dish is off the charts!

This was delicious and simple. I'm always looking to eat more veggies so I used 1 lb shrimp and three sliced Cubanelle peppers which I sauteed in the oil before adding the spices. The sauce was still pretty abundant, so next time I think I would add some sliced zucchini as well. My sauce was a brilliant yellow from the turmeric, not red like in the photo.
Used 2 pounds of shrimp, whole can of coconut milk and added a few handfuls of baby spinach at the end of cooking. Delicious
This was bland and boring, we made it exactly as the recipe calls for and I shant make it again!

This was a bit bland for my taste. I ended up adding some curry powder and that added more flavor. I also added some broccoli because I like veggies in my curries. Overall it was pretty good. I would make again with some adjustments.
Goan too. Definitely add 2 level tsp garam masala, before the coconut milk, every Indian has some in their pantry.

As written, this recipe is a good base but a little unexciting. The second time I made it, I also added some green chillies, string beans, cumin, garam masala, coriander powder, mustard seeds, ginger, and garlic in addition to the listed ingredients. Much better flavour and a more well rounded dish the second time around.

Easy & delicious, Even easier? Bengali shrimp curry: put in a pot mustard oil, sliced ginger, roughly chopped green onions, turmeric, 1 3” cinnamon stick, 2-3 cloves, 2-3 cardamom pods, 1 Serrano chili, salt, coconut milk, cleaned shrimp ( maybe a pound?) Cover & cook over low heat maybe 5 minutes, until shrimp is done.
So easy. And simple. I had frozen shrimp on hand and still tasty!
@Anne did you thaw the shrimp first or just cook from frozen?
@Lisa C defrost and drain them first, or else you’ll be adding extra water to the mix.

I would appreciate your help. This recipe came together fast which was great. We didn't like the sauce and I can't figure out what spice is off for us? We love pad thai and masala sauce just to give you an idea of our tastes. I had to buy coriander, would that be what tasted so different? I also added tamarind. I just can't figure it out. Thank you very much.
The addition of tamarind may have totally changed the flavor. I stuck to the recipe except for the addition of garlic and it was amazing.
@Curious the sauce is very bland as written, either reduce the coconut milk or double the spices ( except cayenne, which you can vary to set your desired spiciness).
Good stuff! One change I'll make next time -- use the whole can of coconut milk (to use it up) and add another half pound of shrimp to compensate. For me, the spices would still be plenty.
honestlY?  rather blah.  needed more complexity of flavors

Made this last night.  Good, but next time I'll add shredded ginger to the oil (before or along with the scallions) and also okra cut into 1" pieces after the onions are fried and before adding the spices.

I was disappointed in this recipe. I did have to use shrimp from the freezer but otherwise followed the recipe. We found it rather tasteless. I added hot sauce , more lemon juice, and more salt in an attempt to improve it. I would not make it again. I’m surprised it got rave reviews.
@Rosemary I doubled the spices as the original tasted very bland.
@Rosemary agree bland , I added some spinach for color but the sauce was thin as well. Not a keeper.

@Rosemary I’m a novice when it comes to making a curry anything but I did expect this to be spicier and thicker. A bit disappointed in the outcome but I did not have the recommended hot paprika so used sweet paprika.  Question for the cooks here… Would it help to thicken the sauce by simmering longer before putting in the shrimp? Never cooked with coconut milk before!
Maybe I made it wrong but it was super plain.

I liked this a lot with a few modifications (I’m Indian, and like spicy food).

I added some dried red chillies in the oil with shallots, used tamarind instead of lime (though I think lime is pretty fine for this purpose), added some frozen ginger and two jalepenos and sauteed prior to adding spices.

This recipe is delicious, and so easy - but I did tweak it slightly just for personal taste: I added a few curry leaves (which my local Indian restaurant does) and found I really complemented the existing flavors.
I add 3 serranos and a dash of curry powder and garam masala.
Sounds easy and delicious.  I can't do any hot spices.
This looks too simple. What about some vegetables?
@Mike I had the same thought! This needs veggies to make it a complete meal. I wonder if courgette or broccoli would go well with the
Im surprised there's no cumin in this. I'm making it tonight as per your recipe,
Remove the tails. Shouldn’t change a thing - the spices are perfect and it’s such a comforting and homey curry to enjoy often!
Disappointed with the bland flavour. I don't why  Madhur calls it "Goan".
It would be helpful to know if the coconut milk is sweetened or not.
As with most Goan recipes, this would be better with grated coconut (can be bought frozen at the right grocery store) instead of coconut milk!
Could a combination of smoked paprika and regular chili powder be used?
(Realizing the broad range of personal tastes) I’m wondering what people think of the heat level using the hot paprika or Kashmiri chile powder. Also wondering if lite coconut milk would work or not.
@Jeaniesee.    It was too spicy for my wife I think a reduction in the hot paprika would tone it done enough to eat.
@Jeaniesee Lite coconut milk is 50% coconut milk and 50% water. Whether it will work depends of how you feel about a thinner sauce.
If I’m using frozen shrimp, do they need to thaw first?

Yes, otherwise they'll release a lot of excess water into the dish. A trick is to thaw them in cold water, remove, and then allow to drain over a plate in the fridge for 20 minutes or so. It really improves the texture.
Double spices add 1T 5Tamarind paste
Needed a whole large lemon to cut the spice level, then it was perfect—just a heads up for those of you who are less spice tolerant.

This was SO good and so easy! I didn't have any shallots, so I substituted yellow onion. I'll have to try it again with shallots. I bet this would be good with tilapia.  I was glad I had extra rice to soak up the extra sauce for leftovers. Adding this to the easy meal rotation!

I made this last night but was lazy and followed a suggestion here of using good curry powder (Madras curry powder).....amazing!!!  I added sugar snap peas along with the shrimps because I wanted more veg.....perfect!!!

Kasmiri chili powder is pretty mild.
@Lori

I just made this and it was a hit with everyone including my preteen niece and nephew. I subbed garlic and grated ginger for the spice. I split the sauce and made half with shrimp and used the other half with tofu for a vegan version. The sauce wasn't as thick as I thought it would be but was still very good. I will definitely make this again.

I had high hopes for this recipe, but I find the flavor of the sauce surprisingly dull. Perhaps I should have upped the spices and added garlic and ginger. Or maybe the sauce is more flavorful with shrimp than with tofu, which is what I used because I had a package that was about to expire.

Yeah, I subbed in some roadkill antelope I had hanging a bit too long, and even though I upped the cayenne and added a bunch of cumin, it still didn’t work when I added molasses and vinegar to dial it back a bit.

I’m pretty sure that’s not the fault of the recipe, though.
I’m in south Louisiana and 1/4 tsp of cayenne in a small dish like this is pretty hot.
Delicious! We subbed cauliflower rice. Really enjoyed this meal.
I didn't include the cayenne, but it was still way too hot for us.  I should have used only 1 tsp of the hot paprika or less.  It was inedible.

When this problem arises, add more liquid and cook a starch to absorb the heat.
Potato: peel and using the peeler, cut a potato or two into thin slices, then cut into matchsticks for faster cooking.
Rice works but may take longer.
Pasta works better cooked separately beyond aldente then added.
Adding more vegetables helps, too. Sauté onions, microwave carrots or cauliflower.
Canned beans.

Ask me how I know.  :)
soooooo good. the list of ingredients and volumes of spice to coconut milk ratio is perfection. i made a few adjustments to technique, but who cares! thank you thank you thank you!

This is absolutely delicious and so very easy! We roasted some cauliflower to have along side, and ended up just tossing it in the curry itself when I realized I only had a pound of shrimp instead of the pound and a half called for. The curry is so delicious I just want to eat it off a spoon. Weeknight friendly and incredibly delish!
Tried this recipe last weekend.  Followed it exactly except did not leave shrimp tails on.  So delicious!  Will definitely make again.

Made jasmine rice with chicken broth (will do seafood stock next time - was just what we had), added smoked paprika at the end (had used regular for the dish - needed something more), and used scallops instead of shrimp. A delight!
Delicious!  I cut the recipe in half and it worked perfectly!

This was such a tasteless dish until I added garlic and MSG during cooking... I'll give it 2/5 based on this recipe alone because its edible and easy, but I would not adhere strictly to the instructions.

Always a good principle to follow: taste and adjust as you go.

It’s really not the recipe’s fault that it doesn’t land in one’s sweet spot.

Looks great. But I wish recipes didn't leave the amount of salt up for grabs. You wind up adding a tiny bit, tasting, adding more, tasting again, for too many iterations. Why not just state a middle-of-the-road amount and add "or to taste"?

Made it as written with Kashmiri chili powder, but without cayenne ('Cause SO). Unfortunately, it was just kind'a bland, although the heat wasn't insufficient.

I have no suggestions to fix it.

I read the suggestions and added garlic, ginger and red curry paste. But the dish lacked sufficient depth of flavor. Added more salt. Still came out sorta meh. This is my first attempt at making a curry dish and I'll need to explore other recipes to determine why this one was lackluster. In the meantime, I will file this recipe as do not make again....

Too hot for me while also  kinda bland. I prepared it exactly as described. My sauce was yellow, not orange. A little watery, too. 
I think the hotness needs to be mentioned in the recipe and maybe suggest alternate spices for people who lack stamina for heat?
Delicious but awfully soupy, is there an easy way to thicken the sauce? (And yes I drained and dried the shrimp).
tripped recipe and add two 8oc cans of diced tomatoes
Add vegetables -- sauté with shallots. Can probably double cayenne (maybe more)

We loved this dish. We did add some fresh garlic and ginger (along with the shallots), and diced a small tabasco chili as well. I do recommend using the Kashmiri red chile instead of just paprika, but be careful to see how hot it is (like ours, which we got from our local Indian grocer). We left our the cayenne and it was the perfect amount of heat.

Such an easy dish but for me the paprika added a slightly sour taste, which could be the idea, but I personally would omit next time and replace with perhaps fresh garlic and ground coriander. I didn’t have coconut milk only coconut cream which was very thick so diluted with just a small amount of liquid chicken stock. It worked.
@Jennifer Paprika comes in different strengths and must be fresh or it can sour.

This was a good quick meal. I used an onion instead of shallots. Shallot around my rural area means the opposite of the deep end of a swimming pool. I used some Rancho Gordo chili powder that I had because I didn’t have any hot paprika. Keeper recipe.
We make a similar sauce and freeze it in small ziploc bags. Then cook any protein and/or veggies in the instant pot and then add the sauce. Simmer for a few minutes and the meal is ready.
Delicious!   Served it with plain Udad Papad
Wish the app allowed half stars, cuz I would go 4.5 instead of 4. I probably doubled the spice amount and still found this a little bland
Also agree with Nina about ginger(I keep some in the freezer), tamarind and jalapenos.

Excellent dish. One of the few NY Times recipes that I didn't have to adjust the spices. Unfortunately, I bought low-fat coconut milk by accident, but I remedied that by adding a few tablespoons of heavy cream. I made the sauce ahead and then reheated it the next day and added the shrimp. Very fast, easy recipe, and quite versatile. In the future, I plan to add a few handfuls of greens and butternut squash.

First I seared some broccoli florets and thinly sliced stems. Pushed them aside in the pan. Then added the shallots to saute. To cut the heat down a bit for my spouse, I used smoked instead of hot paprika; and chipotle instead of cayenne pepper. A delicious, easy recipe.

I made this dish this evening. It’s delicious however my taste buds detected missing flavour. After I read the comments, tamarind might be a good idea. Also thought of anchovies-just a small one or two. It’s definitely a keeper in my book.
As suggested, I included a sliced yellow bell pepper, some diced seeded tomatoes, and added in 1 tsp of curry powder.  Served over basmati rice, it was delicious!  Definitely will make it again!
Triple the sauce and go heavy on the spices.

We loved this, with a few changes/additions (thank you to other commenters for your suggestions!). Very easy, quick recipe.

- 1 lb frozen thawed shrimp
- add 2 thawed frozen ginger cubes and 3 cloves chopped garlic to the shallots after 2 minutes
- then added 1 tsp curry powder, 1/2 tsp garam masala to recipe spices. (Used hot smoked paprika and 1/4 tsp cayenne. It was not too spicy.)
- 1 1/4 C coconut milk was right amount
- lay several stalks cilantro, tied, to steep with the coconut milk mixture.
- added 3-4 handfuls spinach along with shrimp
- lime juice instead of lemon
- served with jasmine rice
I followed the recipe and added chopped bok choy when I sautéed the shallots to have some green vegetables. It was delicious. A bit spicy but good flavor!

After all the great reviews, I was a little disappointed by this. Thought it was a little bland, and it definitely wasn't bright red like the photo.  I found another recipe for the same dish, and that one called for some cinnamon, cloves, cumin, coriander, and garlic plus tamarind.  I added a bit of each of those to the leftovers and liked it much better.
Very good
Can anyone tell me how spicy this dish is? I love a little spice but not mouth on fire.
@Carol M. I used 1tsp sweet Hungarian paprika, 1 tsp cayenne (total). It was pretty spicy but balanced out with the rice and a glass of rose. Not burning but hot
I think odd that this is called shrimp curry but there is no curry

Curry just means that it's a sauce, and usually that sauce is seasoned with (what I think are delicious!) spices! Not to presume the point of confusion here, but sometimes people refer to certain spice mixes as "curry," but there's no actual spice called curry.
Can you use Hot Hungarian Paprika instead of Kashmiri chili powder?
@Casanders I had to use regular paprika and cayenne powder as a substitute. I’m sure the Hungarian would work.
@Kevin McGovern. I used the hot Hungarian Paprika and cayenne. I love spicy but I think I would skip cayenne next time.  Thanks for replying.
Can I use frozen shrimp?
I did. I followed a suggestion here and thawed them in cold water and then drained them before using to reduce the water content. It turned out delicious.
Is the coconut milk supposed to be full-fat?
I made it for two. Made the sauce as for four, added shrimp for two (8 oz) and it was amazing. Used basmati rice. LOVED IT!
Another great posting David.  So glad it isn't another sauteed something with a cut up Chile thrown in and called fab.
Bland - lacking both flavor and heat
@Yz what did you use for paprika?
Used Bay Scallops instead of Shrimp. Delicious 😋.

Absolutely fantastic super quick recipe. I made this in a Dutch oven and stayed true to the recipe other than adding very thinly sliced red peppers at the end after the shrimp. My husband was very wary to eat this but I convinced him to try and he loved it. Over basmati rice was the finishing touch, This one is a keeper.
Wife thought it was too spicy, toddler threw it on the ground.  Much easier to buy a Thai curry paste that you like.  Would not make it again.

I appreciate any recipe. And, this was quick and easy. For me, I found the sauce a little underwhelming. I think next time I will add more paprika and maybe some cumin and/or garam masala. I’ll also sauté shallots with thin red chilis. It was tasty enough but just wasn’t as explosive or as layered as I would have liked.
I did add garlic for an extra layer and used large shallots, too.
Do I need to peel the shrimp? Is deveining enough?

Of course you need to peel the shrimp.
If you are not up to the task, peeled shrimp is readily available in the 16/20 per pound size
Check your local market. You can usually  buy peeled, tail-off and deveined shrimp in the frozen seafood section.
To be fair, I made this with salmon, not shrimp, but it was only okay. I don’t think the sauce would be substantially better with shrimp- just didn’t have much depth of flavor.
Delicious curry!  So easy and the sauce was excellent.  I had colossal (10-12) shrimp so I was able to reduce and concentrate the flavor.  Definitely a keeper.  I will try this one with chicken, too!

This is stunningly simple and delicious. I cook a lot of Indian food, both original and diaspora. Much of the time it's an all day sucker, which I enjoy. But this particular evening I was pressed for time, had excellent local shrimp and couldn't have been more pleased.
This looks delicious and I look forward to trying it. One question: should the shrimp be raw or cooked? Most recipes rarely say, but both kinds are available in the frozen section.
Raw since the directions tell you to cook it until it is opaque.
Never use already-cooked shrimp in a dish where they will be heated. Once done, even a little additional heat will turn them into hockey pucks.
Looks delicious, but PLEASE take the tails off the shrimp before cooking!
Amen. Leave tails on ONLY if they're intended to be eaten with fingers -- pretty much means traditional shrimp cocktail. If meant to eat with utensils, remove to the shrimp stock.
Add bell pepper, jalapeño, garlic, ginger, curry. Sear one side of shrimp first then set aside before adding back toward end. I served in a pita. So good!!!
This was really good. But what made it really great was the butter that I melted in the sauce just before serving. I started with 1 T., and that was so good that I added another.
My wife and I prepared this recipe for lunch today. Fantastic!
Delicious. I used a whole large onion instead of shallots, and gochugaru powder instead of hot paprika and cayenne. Next time I'll add garlic and the cayenne or red chili flakes.
I made this exactly according to the recipe.  It's the kind of recipe where you have everything anyway.  It was EXCELLENT.

I made it as written except that I used a little red boat salt instead of regular salt for more umami. It seemed a little bland as it was cooking, but once the salt and lemon were in, it was transformed. I love these simple dishes that have so much depth and seem so much more than the sum of their parts.

I found this medium good as-is, but will keep this in my pocket as a great starting point for whatever protein and veg I have on-hand. Good flavors, just very plain (that’s my bad for being surprised by that, reading the recipe— the final dish was shrimp + sauce + rice so I don’t know what I was expecting differently). Served with steamed basmati rice and garlic naan from TJ.
So delicious and so easy! bHighly recommended.
I liked it a lot, yet my young guests wanted more spice. I loved the cilantro at the end. I did add a few frozen peas, but next time I will add a little more vegetable, such as chopped baby broccoli.
Can I substitute Vzdouvan Curry instead of Turmeric?

I cut this in half for our family of two.  Cooked according to the recipe instructions but found that I was woefully short of the sauce when finished cooking.  If I repeat this again cut in half, I'd increase the amount of coconut milk significantly.  I used the hot paprika, not the Kashmiri chili powder because my Kashmiri chilies are whole and I didn't want to spend the time grinding them manually or in a spice grinder. Probably will take the time to grind them when repeating.
I made this dish as written with one addition…curry powder, just enough to give it a nice taste, fragrance and color.
High flavor to effort ratio.  Added more lemon.
This was such an easy, tasty recipe! My only adjustment would be a touch more cayenne (I like mine spicy!). This one definitely gets added to the rotation.
Very tasty! I omitted the cayenne and used only 1/2 t of Kashmiri powder, and it was more than spicy enough.

Made this exactly as is - absolutely easy & delicious, recommend doubling the sauce before shrimp is added & freeze extra sauce.  This dish was inhaled by all (and our first time trying curry!) Made with a side of basmati rice with veggie stock & a scoop of tomato paste THANK YOU!

Usually when I look at a curry recipe, it has a long list of ingredients I've never heard of, so I just order take-away.  In contrast, I had everything in my spice cabinet to make this (paprika, turmeric, cayenne, black pepper).  This comes together quickly and is delicious.  I even later ordered Kashmiri chile pepper, which I recommend (it has about the same amount of mild heat as the paprika version).  A suggestion was made below to include stir-fried green beans, which I'll try next time.

Don’t flatten the mixture, spoon it in a heap, gently round the sides, and make a slight indent in the top to hold the filling when cooked. Once cooked turn off oven, open door and leave it to cool down in the oven overnight, so it doesn’t crack. Decorate with fresh unsweetened whipped pure cream, fresh quartered strawberries and fresh passion fruit.  I think your recipe is for an Eton mess meringue where you have broken meringue, not an Australian pavlova, which is the best.
It's not that your comment is fascinating, it's fascinating that a comment which is clearly about a dessert was approved by the NY Times cooking section editors for a shrimp curry recipe!

@Jennifer
It looks like this comment is about a different recipe.
This recipe is on repeat at my house.  Really quick and simple, and quite delicious!

Personally, I LOVE the Goan-Style Shrimp Curry recipe published in the Times on March 5, 2008. It is fabulous and a go-to meal for family and friends. Pull it up in the archives. It's really worth giving it a try.
I was making it for two, so I only used 3/4 lb Georgia shrimp (lucky to live in Savannah, where fresh shrimp are plentiful)—used all the spices and 1/2 the coconut milk. Delicious.
It turned out too bland.  Perhaps it does not work with tasteless farm pond shrimp.  The coconut milk also diluted the spice.
It's very soupy and much too thin.
Absolutely amazing taste and SO EASY!! Quite impressive! Will definitely make again and again!

Absolutely fabulous, and ridiculously easy. You can usually count on five star NYT reviews and you can count on this one. Made exactly half for two, following the recipe precisely. It is delicious. Making half was a mistake: should’ve made the whole thing for leftovers!
Easy, fast, and delicious! We added chopped spinach to add a bit of greens. 🥬 thanks for the recipe AND the comments!

My low rating is for this recipe's inability to make adaptions. I think that if you are going to make it, be sure you have the ingredients called for in the recipe, especially the coconut milk and the lemon, as well as the cilantro if using - no substitute. This was also way too hot. Perhaps had I used coconut milk instead of Greek yogurt and lemon instead of lime, it would have been more enjoyable.
great- made as directed except left out the cayenne and replaced the Kashmiri with 1 3/4 t smoked paprika and 1/4 t India red chilu

Sadly, this turned out to be very disappointing. Four diners found it to be very bland. I simmered the sauce, followed the recipe precisely but result was meh. Don’t understand what I could do to improve.

I just made this. I followed the recipe exactly except I added no lemon.  I was in a big hurry, so I dumped in frozen shrimp without thawing them first, which made the sauce a little watery. The curry was pretty good but not fantastic. After when I was storing the leftovers, I removed the shrimp from the pan and decided to cook down the sauce a little, until it looked rich and thick. What a difference! The sauce was luscious. Fantastic for such a quick, simple curry.
Needs a bit of salt.
This is absolutely delicious. I didn't have hot paprika, so I substituted garam masala and chipotle chili instead.
How far in advance can the sauce be made? Can it be made the day before?
Someone was saying they make it and freeze the sauce, but I'm pretty sure you could make it the day before and it'd be even better the next day
A day or even two before, but everything comes together so quickly I am not sure it's worth it.

Very tasty but too spicy for my wife. I reduced the hot paprika a bit and used almost no cayenne. Any suggestions about how to tone it down without losing its distinctive flavor? What about substituting sweet paprika for hot?
@Richard I did half Kashmiri chili powder and half sweet paprika and skipped the cayenne. Still had a bit of a kick, but not too much.
Has anyone used frozen shrimp for this dish? If so would love thoughts/tips !

Yes, I just made it with frozen raw shrimp. Just let them thaw for a couple hours outside the fridge, wash them off, then cook as instructed. Mine were big so it took about 7 minutes to cook through. Try not to buy cooked frozen shrimp as they'll likely end up overdone.

Mine turned out way too hot and not flavorful.  I used kashmiri chili powder  instead of paprika - not sure if that was the problem- I did not add any cayenne. I might try it another time with paprika.
Can I double this?
This was so easy and so delicious! Next time I will double the sauce ! Will def be making again

Easy to make. Doesn’t take long. Maybe cook
Shrimp 4 minutes.

I thought I’d love this recipe but I didn’t because I found it boring. Maybe someone could help me figure out why.  I used the suggested hot paparika because I didn’t have the other spice but otherwise, I followed the recipe exactly.  The color of my dish was more turmeric yellow than the red in the picture. Any ideas would be welcome because it was so easy to make.
Just made it and completely agree with you. Very underwhelming -even though I added garlic and ginger to the fry mix
has anyone doubled this recipe with success?

@LRI I've made this repeatedly and I usually quadruple the recipe. I just did this tonight. I set aside three portions of sauce for freezing, and then made tonight's dinner with the remaining quarter.

I'm planning to make this for the first time in a couple of hours.  I just went through our pretty well stocked spice cabinet pulling out everything I'll need, and was surprised to discover that we don't have turmeric!
Could I just add Curry in greater or lesser amounts?  I really don't want to have to run back out to the market!
I checked the sauce 2-3 timed during preparation and ended up doubling the spices and adding the juice of an entire medium-sized lemon. A quick dish  and very tasty.

Both nearby stores were out of full fat coconut milk, so I reluctantly settled for “lite,” which I normally find flavorless and abhorrent (think non-fat milk -  what’s the point?). Once it reduced along with the well-bloomed spices in the oil and a splash of shrimp stock, it was fine - sort of. Meaning, the spicy heat was quite pronounced due to the lack of richness to distribute and temper it; but we are pepper bellies, so it was delicious anyway, and we cleaned the pan. Next time I will hold out for full fat coconut milk, especially if serving others besides my husband and myself. 
Overall winner, and fast once shrimp are cleaned and mise en place is set up. Make this!
This is so quick and easy! I reduced the chili powder based on past experience so my sauce was not as deeply colored, but the flavor was wonderful!

did once with lemon then again with tamarind concentrate that was 100% tamarind (got at Sprouts, AZ peeps) and did add depth of flavor - great tip! Drizzled in off a teaspoon to taste, put in when lemon juice is added in recipe

I know that most of us will use canned coconut milk, rather than fresh. The commonest brands in my local supermarket are Thai Kitchen and Chaokoh, and sometimes a generic brand or two. I’ve never found them to taste particularly “coconutty” (made up that word), so I did some online digging, and consulted a couple of Asian friends, and came up with Aroy-D. It’s 99.5% coconut milk (more than the others), and tastes great.
I haven't had a NYT recipe that I liked this much and was so easy/quick maybe ever?

One of our all-time favorite NYT recipes; easy, delicious, and endlessly adaptable. After making it several times as written, we started riffing--trying various kinds of seafood, adding various vegetables. As of last night, we've declared that the absolute best variation is with chunks of fresh tuna, and with broccolini as the veggie.
Can I just use regular paprika? Isn’t cayenne spicy anyways?

title: Tomato and Cucumber Salad Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I found that it tastes even better kept overnight in the fridge.

I would use less lemon juice and splash a little olive oil before serving next time. The recipe calls for the juice from a whole lemon or 3 tablespoons. I used the whole lemon and it turned out puckeringly sour. Next time I would use a couple of squeezes from 1/2 of a lemon.
Unbalanced.  Too acidic, too liquidy afterwards,

Delicious! I sprinkled the lemon as directions said, less than the instructions called for. To make it more of a meal, I fried some halloumi, which reminds me of Indian cheese, and added it diced on top. Also cut cukes into bite-size chunks. I’ll definitely make it again, perhaps with seasoned tofu.
I find it is better to cut the cucumber in half or quarters, lengthwise with the cherry tomatoes is a bit awkward bite.

Prepared as written and it was completely underwhelming. Poor enough that I’m not even interested in trying to fix it, and that’s from someone who is usually really happy with anything suggested by Madhur Jaffrey.
We use tempering a lot in South Indian cooking- I’ve never thought to use the technique on a cool dish. Excited to try this
A hit with us. Used Roma tomatoes as that was what I had.
good with a little avocado added

title: Gelo di Melone (Sicilian Watermelon Pudding) Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: Sugar doesn’t just provide sweetness to most desserts, it also provide tenderness, especially when something is thickened (like this recipe), baked (like a cake) or frozen (like ice cream).

@ZB
Thank you for teaching me this. Some sugar substitutes seem okay in very small doses. We have a Type I diabetic in the family and I’m looking for good dessert ideas and recipes.

@Lynne X i suggest,  try making a fruit granita using apple juice or white grape juice as the base. I wonder if that might be sweet enough without too much added flavor to hide the flavor of your main fruit, like berries.
How much do six cups of cubed watermelopn weigh?
@Ricardo 1¼ kilo or 1,250 grams or 2.75 pounds approx.

This was delicious. I didn’t notice a cornstarch flavor. Made it as is (I didn’t have the sweetest watermelon, so it wasn’t too sugary for me) and added a half teaspoon of orange blossom water to the cooked product. My watermelon loving small children devoured it.
What role does sugar play in this recipe? Watermelon is sweet enough without it.

@Marti sugar professional pastry chef here: the sugar is, indeed, necessary, to counteract the acid in the necessary lemon juice, to help with thickening and gelling, and yes, even a watermelon will need sweetening, especially after “juicing.” Anyone who cuts the sugar in a recipe because they think there’s “too much” or it’s unnecessary risks adversely affecting the quality, texture, moistness, tenderness, and other chemical reactions in the formula.

@JMBY  I reduce sugar amounts in everything and I haven’t noticed adverse effects- although I’m not making pastries professionally! So what do you suggest that people do, when they prefer things to be less sweet or they can’t tolerate as much sugar?
Why cube the watermelon when you’re just throwing it in the blender? A weight measurement would be so much easier.
Curious to know what your recommendation would be to substitute unflavored gelatin for the cornstarch?
@FoofDet I was wondering the same thing

I halved the recipe. After chilling for a few hours, I thought the cornstarch, which was a little noticeable at first, became all but undetectable. We upped the game by topping the pudding with meringue, which made this dessert divine. It tasted like summer.
I'm not a fan of super sweet desserts but I followed the recipe exactly with 1/2 cup sugar. It didn't taste too sweet for me!

This recipe turned out pretty well after a couple of modifications. 
1) The amount of sugar in the recipe is the minimum required, ie if your watermelon is insanely sweet. So, be prepared to add up to 50% more sugar.
2) Add a pinch or two of salt. It elevates the sweet flavor.
3) I must have used 50-60g of cornstarch since mine didn't dissolve completely (my fault).
4) I added 3T of cold butter at the end (like you would add in a lemon curd). Consistency is that of a curd as well.
Delicious!
75 Grams of cornstarch is too much, resulting texture was tough.  Will reduce to 60 or so next time.  It was tasty with the pistachios.
Wanted so much to love this. The pretty color. The lightness. But it was not a hit with anyone in my house.  Gelatinous. Not pleasing in any way.
I've made this several times but I always add Fiori di Sicilia. It's absolutely delightful!

A wonderful recipe for an unexpected summer dessert, and dead easy, especially using cubed watermelon from the produce section. I’ve made this a few times now, and agree with others that the level of sugar should depend on the sweetness of your watermelon, so taste it and definitely add more sugar if your fruit isn’t super-sweet. Maybe it’s my stove, but I consistently need more than 2-3 minutes for the mixture to thicken when cooking, more like 7 minutes. (It’s  kinda fun when it happens, because within several seconds the mixture moves from a loose liquid to something velvety.) 

David’s note about a few drops of rosewater is spot on, but err on the side of caution. I was a little too liberal with the rosewater on my first try, and ended up with something that was more of a rose dessert than a watermelon dessert.

I’ve made this twice. First time following the recipe. Prepping the watermelon was a lot of work. Second time, I used Simply Watermelon juice from Walmart Canada. As it had sugar and lemon juice, I used the juice and cornstarch only. A wonderful dessert both times.
It was delicious but the cornstarch was too prominent. Is there a way to fix that without jeopardizing the gelling effect?
Should it cool before going in the fridge? If someone answered in the next five minutes that would helpful ;-)

Thank you for this recipe! It was lovely and easy to make and a very refreshing, light desert on a hot day. My guests didn’t quite know what to make of it because they were expecting more of a pudding, but I was fine with handling the leftovers myself!

To avoid the cornstarch taste, try tapioca (cassava) starch (not flour). Cassava has almost no flavor and even if people noticed something they wouldn't be able to identify it. Interesting to see that Chef Google AI says that the sub ratio is 2:1 but real cooks write that the ratio is 1:1.

I used potato starch instead of corn starch cause that's what I had at hand (1:1). I used an entire small watermelon like those that you get in a 2-pack at Costco. I didn't sieve, mea culpa...my focus was on the four cups for the recipe...  really refreshing & certainly not too sweet. Easy, peasy; thanks for the recipe & right in time for watermelon season as we head towards the dreaded winter solstice.

Interesting. Very similar to Egyptian mahlabia. Made a half recipe, because I had bought a two pack of mini watermelons, tried one--and it was just OK. So used the other half of that one to make this dessert. After blending, add exactly 2 cups of juice.  Added a little more sugar (than half) because watermelon wasn't the best. Didn't have lemon so used lime juice.  Tasted good. The pistachios are a nice touch. We also added a little whipped cream.

Knocked Back (about sixty years)! My grandmother in the Middle East used to make buttery crescent cookies filled with Turkish Delight. She would bake for weeks in anticipation of my arrival. Once I got started, I couldn’t stop— so much so, that she stored them under lock & key! I flavored my dessert with rose water and served it with fresh whipped cream topped with pistachios. Last, but not least with ice water in a tall aluminum tumbler (just like the ones I remember). Thanks for the memories, Chef! Delicious.
Um, no. This was waste of watermelon. It is too sweet, and just not good. It is pretty though.
I too have a type 2 diabetic in the family and wondering about viable substitutions for the sugar and cornstarch?

I loved this and it was so easy. A great way to use up leftover watermelon too! The added mint was nice. One question though - I thought the cornstarch came through too prominently. May I use less without jeopardizing the consistency?
My family did not like the texture.  The pistachios overwhelmed the watermelon.
Made this per recipe. It lacked flavor. Perhaps my watermelon was not sweet enough. Adding the pistachio garnish helped. Would not make again.
Google AI says 6c. cubed watermelon weigh approx 2 lbs.
IMHO the 75g of cornstarch results in an serenely

Well, I either messed it up or it's not great. Followed the recipe exactly. After four hours in the fridge, it had the consistency of congealed pork fat. Slight sweet-vegetal flavor. More like a loose sweetened 1960's tomato aspic than a nice firm fruit pudding. Not terrible, but not worth trying again. Maybe a top layer of cream or whipped cream would pull it back. I still have three servings left. May try to rescue it. Otherwise, I can happily snack on the pistachios.

Delicious — tasted like good watermelon with the flavor a little amped up -  and very pretty.  I found this thickened very quickly (whisking constantly probably distracted me from noticing exactly when it reached the simmer) and next time I would aim for a softer texture.  In addition to the pistachios, I garnished with chopped cherries and some sprigs of mint — the mint really added something, so next time will add more and julienne the leaves.
Can one use gelatin granules or leaves instead of cornstarch?
Loved the flavor, texture was bothersome. Would add a lot more pistachios and make small individual servings if I make it again.
Yuck!! I followed directions to a t, whisking the mixture for over 20 minutes, it never thickened. Thought it might in the fridge, it didn’t and tasted like cornmeal. Wasted a watermelon!!!

title: Pesto Pasta With Corn and Green Beans Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I used slim asparagus in place of the green beans..much better

This was marvelous!  I sautéed shallots with the corn, and added garlic.  Added lemon juice to the pesto.  Cooked the green beans 3 minutes, min.  Cooked the pasta short of al dente and finished it with the sauce.  Lovely taste of summer.
If you haven’t tried sunflower seeds or pumpkin seeds in pesto you’re missing out.
@Denis raw or roasted?
I use raw pumpkin seeds but roasted would work too, I’d try both and see which is better!
As written, it comes out pretty bland. A squeeze of lemon juice in the pesto and at finishing goes a long way, but be prepared to go heavy on creative spice adjustments.
If you use good ingredients, it does not "come out pretty bland."
Swapped half the beans for garlic scapes which I seared before adding the corn. Otherwise made per recipe. OMG. Delicious

Excellent - the just-cooked, slightly chewy green beans and little pops of sweetness from the corn make this summery and special. And fun to eat. The classic combination of basil, olive oil, garlic and Parmesan serves as a sort of warm backdrop for vegetables. My husband and I squabbled over the leftovers.
Those beans will be very undercooked.
@Pups Agreed. I hate crunchy green beans. If I try this recipe I think I’ll put the beans in at the same time as the pasta.
Made as is with lots of black pepper and roasted cherry tomatoes. Delicious.

Loved this! No pecorino - doubled the Parmigiana Reggiano. Used high quality frozen corn and sautéed it with a dash of red pepper until the kernels browned. Cut the string beans lengthwise/french style. Rather than using two large pots, I cooked the corn in a small one and added it to the pasta which I cooked in a 5 qt. skillet. The crunch of the beans and corn added great texture to the dish. A splash of fresh lemon juice brightened everything. Want to try it again with other veggies.

No green beans in the house, so subbed julienned snow peas for green beans. Sautéed them with the corn in the butter before adding the pasta, pesto and cooking water. The result was a crunchy sweetness added to the flavors. But agree with previous posters, if I make this again it needs some punching up.
Delicious surprise for how good the pesto was made as the recipe is written since I typically add nuts (walnuts or pepitas since I can't use pignolia) to mine.   I'll use this again.

I really wish chefs wouldn’t call something pesto that isn’t pesto. Corn? Parsley? Pecorino? Even garlic is a no. And especially not 3
Cloves.

Maybe those chefs know the word  “pesto” has been used for different combinations of ingredients since Roman times? Many any of the ingredients in this recipe have been found in pesto variations since then. 

Sure, this isn’t what many consider the classic, pesto alla genovese, first documented in the 1800s, which is today considered the default when Italians say just “pesto.”

But it’s still a pesto.

@Christina 
Agree! Made this pesto recently, but...  had lots more carrot tops (from carrots bought @ farmers market) than basil, so... 
Gotta say Carrot Top Pesto, tho different, is awesome! On pasta, potatoes, omelettes, everything! 
Life's too short to be picky about ingredients ;-)
Garlic has been a staple of Ligurian cooking for centuries.

This chef says she used store-bought pesto, but then she does not say how much of it she used, I.e., 1 C., 2 C., etc.?  The recipe provides the amounts of ingredients if you make your own pesto, but if you use store-bought, then how much?  Thank you!  (Thanks also for the asparagus sub idea...).
A 6.7-ounce jar (Aldi) was perfect.

Please don't correct me if I'm wrong (because I'm not going to change), but green beans and 
good white potatoes are essential in this dish. 
Also great olive oil, excellent parmesan, and lightly toasted pine nuts. Sure you can add fresh corn, I have done so and it's quite good. Basil is one of the great joys of summer and pesto is the queen of preps. Respect your basil!

Green beans with white potatoes is a personal favorite, but it's not this dish. (Not everyone makes things a la Laura.) Great/excellent ingredients are always essential to any recipe. I'm not a huge fan of basil, but I don't disrespect it.
Should the corn kernels be cooked prior to putting them in the wide skillet with butter?
No, cooking them a few minutes in the skillet with butter should be plenty.

Okay for a rushed weeknight meal. The corn didn't bring as much flavor as I thought it would. I didn't have enough green beans, so I mixed in an equal amount of sugar pod peas, and they were a great addition.
Add some bread crumbs. Yum.

My comments agree with many of the previous ones (e.g., a spritz of lemon and some green onions sauteed with the corn).  Cook the beans and corn a minute or so longer.  Also I halved the recipe and it still feeds 4-5 people nicely as a side dish.  Basil directly from the garden in the summer, if you can grow even in patio pots is the best, so fresh and green.
Made this last night and I agree that it needs a squeeze of lemon. I also think it would be rally tasty to add some garlicky bread crumbs on top for flavor and texture. I'll try this next time.
Perfect summer recipe..just when we are drowning in green beans, basil and parsley from the garden. Some garden tomatoes on top would make a lovely garnish.
I so pleased to have a good pesto recipe without nuts, which are currently off my diet. Thank you David
Made this with garden's bounty- added a sliced zucchini- absolutely delicious, and good cold too.
Hey folks.  Let's use forks to eat this one.  Stay gorgeous.

The lead in to this recipe talks about toasted walnuts or pine nuts but they are never mentioned in the ingredients or the recipe. I would have expected them to be chopped and added to the “pesto” sauce.
No, this recipe is definitely nut-free, as specified  in the accompanying article.
The accompanying article does indeed say that "most" pestos contain walnuts or pine nuts. The author then adds that he prefers "a simpler version" which is what we have here.
I’m confused by the overview of the recipe on the app (just on my iPad but not my phone) specifying that walnuts or pine nuts are included.  Not the case.  Still, the pesto is quite good.
Don't be confused. Re-read the accompanying article. It mentions that walnuts or pine nuts are often included in pesto... but not in this simpler version.
I streamlined this by using a good refrigerated basil pesto and corn from the farmers market. I was a skeptic, but it was quite tasty for very little effort.

This was simply excellent. Full of flavour and colour. The whole family loved it.  I added a squeezed lemon and some lemon zest to the pesto, which turned out really well. We also included some cherry tomatoes with the corn. Wonderful. A keeper!
Riffed on this a little - pesto had toasted pistachio, no parsley.. added shallot to the corn.  Ended up tasty.

Very good made as written with some lemon juice added to the pesto. Next time I will try a shorter pasta shape to capture the corn better. The corn was great. I think the 2 min on the beans keeps this in the primavera zone which I like. Especially with fresh beans from the farmers market. Always enjoy David’s recipes.

Great, easy, delicious way to use up all that summer garden produce!  I had garlic scape and basil pesto on hand, so didn't follow that part of the recipe.  I put a bit of parsley into the corn sautee so the dish would have that flavor, too.  The finished product could use salt (the reason people may have called it "bland") and possibly a tiny lemon squeezy at the end.  
BTW: I subbed sugar snap peas for beans, cut into 2" pieces.  Worked like a charm!
used fresh peas from the garden and supplemented with available edemame - fresh and easy!

I made this dish and the tomato and pepper salad for a Sunday night dinner with friends this week.  Everyone loved it!  One pound of spaghetti produces a lot of food, enough for eight I think.  Excellent veggie dinner!  Loved everything about the article and dinner.  Thanks David Tanis!
10 stars.  Easy to make and absolutely delicious.  Great pesto even without nuts.
How much pesto does the recipe make? I want to use up my pesto from last summer that’s in the freezer and would like to know the amount.
You seem to know about pesto; you have homemade in the freezer. How much pesto would you normally use for a pound of pasta plus some vegetables? That's how much you would use here.
Made this for my kids, I cut the green beans a little smaller and put some shaved Parmesan on top and they loved it! Kept asking for more

Honestly didn't love this. It was ok but not that flavorful. We would recommend using any shaped pasta instead of spaghetti or linguine -- Stringy pasta means the corn and beans all end up at the bottom and are harder to capture on a fork.
If you make this again, use better ingredients if you want good flavor.

We didn’t think the green beans added much- in fact, we thought they competed with the pesto flavor.  We liked the corn. I charred both the beans and corn to up the pizzaz factor. Probably will not repeat although might try corn and red onion.
Delicious! Especially now when corn & green beans are in season!

Excellent! In the spirit of full disclosure, I used this recipe as a base. I had some homemade pesto already on hand— just enough for 8 ounces of spaghetti (3-4oz). Because I always appreciate the comments, I toasted some breadcrumbs in butter and garlic, and used those as a topping. Brilliant! Added a nice, salty crunch. Because I love everything lemony—especially herby and vegetal dishes—I squeezed in a good amount. I loved it! It’s seasonal & satisfying w/o being heavy. Will make again!
Pretty good, a little bland but easy enough to fix. Next time I'll add some diced red peppers. I think it's endlessly adaptable.
Oh, man. You've inspired me to add a spoonful of Calabrian peppers next time I make this.

This one is a keeper! Important not to overcook the pasta, corn or beans so they remain summery and fresh with a bit of bite. My only change was to use less olive oil and sub a generous splash of lemon juice in its place. Delicious as a side for grilled salmon.

The French cousin of Italian pesto is pistou, made without nuts, sometimes with a smidge of tomato in the mix which adds a little acidity. It's typically stirred into the wonderful vegetable soup "Soupe au Pistou."
Walnuts also a great sub. Personally I would use 1/2 C olive oil for 2 C basil
What would walnuts sub for? This is meant to be a pesto specifically without any nuts.
excellent..used our allotment runner beans,   wild garlic pesto from the freezer and frozen corn.
@John “wild garlic”: ramps, or something different? I’d be tempted to saute some ramps. But that’s becoming a different recipe, isn’t it?
I made this with Salmia

I used my own pesto from the freezer, “classic” with pine nuts, etc, and as my beans were on the fatter side, added them to the pasta for the last eight minutes of cooking. The mix of pecorino and Parmesan was a nice touch. This was delicious, a nice summery recipe. Served with a summer tomato salad with fresh basil and croutons. What’s not to like!?
I didn’t love the beans, but the rest was good.
Sautéed onions and ground turkey with the corn.
How is that remotely the same dish?

All hail David Tanis! Although I was at my own table in Brooklyn, I felt myself transported home––and to the front room upstairs at CP, where I spent many evenings peering through that amazing pine to the sun sinking into the bay. My thanks for that.
Half corn and 2/3 beans
How do you reheat this delicious dish?
I would be happy to eat this at room temperature. You can use the microwave, but probably not if there's a lot of cheese on it. (Cheese should be passed at the table, as the recipe recommends.)

I made my usual pesto with pine nuts, garlic and Parmesan, then did the rest as written. Loved it so much! I would eat it every meatless Monday until corn season ends - in about two weeks. I might have to freeze some sweet corn to extend it.

Use lemon basil if you have it! I usually follow a recipe as written first time around, even used the butter in this one. But I went to cut some basil from my garden, and the lemon basil was flowering and was begging to be trimmed back. It seemed like it would be perfect to try with this vegetable pasta recipe and it was! Also, I liked the texture of the sauce. It came out looking a little thin, but actually was not.

With fresh cut corn off the cob, fresh green beans and pesto from my basil garden, this was an excellent dish made with garlic/basil flavored linguini. One caveat; I had to add meat and happen to have chicken meatballs from Ikea in the freezer. Maybe not for everyone but it soaked up the flavors and was a perfect meal!

I made this with pesto from my freezer (no parsley or pecorino) and substituted chickpeas for the corn, adding some grated Parmesan to serve. It was still quite good, though I had to add a little more salt than I expected. I will make it again.
Delicious. We wanted a hardier pesto. Less oil. Will make more next time.
Why does the pasta in the photo accompanying this recipe look so pale? When I add pesto to pasta the pasta turns green?

title: Tomato-Pepper Salad With Tapenade Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I always pay attention to the dishes David Tanis pairs in a meal.  It is never so much that one would feel over-filled, yet it is satisfying.  This recipe hits all the flavor notes and has plenty of chewiness to it.  He is creative with vegetables and seasoning combinations. This was delicious and I’m sure I am healthier for it.

I think I would pass on the sardines or maybe just one in the tapenade. It seems a bit too pronounced, when I personally am looking for the olive flavor. Definitely no sardines if I am entertaining my vegan family for sure.
I added a teaspoon of maple syrup in the dressing and for the salad stick with classic basil.  To be honest I often diverge from original recipes sometimes because of availability, sometimes my own preferences in flavors. 
This is a great recipe for jumping off for me.
@Diane There are no sardines in this recipe. I wish people would make a recipe before they comment on it. Even if they change it up or take something out.
Anchovy fillets are included in the recipe@ACG

Wow, why have I never made my own olive tapenade?  This is a great recipe!Very straightforward so much so that I could throw it together after a long afternoon of kayaking. The lemon is key and I’m the kind of person who puts the opened tin of anchovies on the plate and eats them with the meal.  Also, I think I forgot how alittle Dijon really amps up fresh tomatoes! Yumm. Flexible too. I used basil and had some fresh moz on the side. It all went well with the sunset in the back yard. Thanks!

Very good, especially the tapenade with those crispy garlic toasts you buy in the stores. I made the salad with jarred roasted red bell peppers which I drained on paper towels. I took this to a large 4th of July party and for once, I felt I had brought one of the best dishes. Watch the cayenne, though, in the tapenade.

title: No-Bake Peaches and Cream Cake Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: any recommendations for a NA syrup?

@Sarah 
I was thinking of using white grape juice, almond extract and no alcohol or extra sugar.
Maybe a touch of lemon with the juice to balance the sweetness.  Apple cider or peach juice with some citrus might also be good!
@Colleen Why not try a zero proof brandy.

Made with a few amendments and it was very delicious, a great summer recipe! 
Amendments:
- halved the recipe, as that was still a plenty big serving (for 5, we still had about 1/3 leftovers)
- replaced some rum with amaretto since I hadn’t had any almond extract 
- approximately halved sugar - we don’t like it so sweet and peaches were so sweet and delicious 
- just added a little sugar zu the peaches instead of jam
- used as many ladyfingers as needed to make 2 solid cake layers - this amounted to about double the lady fingers recommended per recipe 
-> would peel the peaches next time, even if everyone said it was fine, I didn’t like it so much
-> I would opt for yellow peaches next time

I could barely fit this in a 9x9 pan and it was piled high. I should have added a few more ladyfingers to make a continuous layer with no gaps, but I only had 24 so stuck with 12 per layer. I think it needed more “cake”. Served with sliced almonds for a little crunch. Wouldn’t make again.
@Becky I used a wide mouth spring form pan and the 24 pieces fit perfectly. I transferred to a plate for presentation, cutting and serving.
Similar sentiment, it needed more "cake." I would reduce the whipped cream and/or ricotta by 30-50% if I made this again.
Biscotti is a good substitute for the crisp ladyfingers, which I sometimes have a hard time finding. They also retain a bit more crunch than ladyfingers, which I like.
The extracts contain alcohol too, but for the white wine in the syrup simply use the common substitutes for that…apple juice or white grape juice (perhaps with a splash of vinegar.)
I think in a pinch you could substitute mascarpone for crème fraiche and Cointreau instead of brandy?  But it sounds like a yummy recipe.

@Carolyn S
Labneh also subs well for crème fraiche.  And I frequently use cottage cheese instead of ricotta.  Just as tasty and SÓ much cheaper.
Heaven. I’d go with 1/4 cup of powdered sugar in the whipped cream though.

This is my new favorite summer dessert. I modified this recipe the second time I made it and preferred it:
1. Omit the ricotta. For me it was a texture issue.
2. Instead of ladyfingers, I used Trader Joe’s French Galette Butter Cookies.
3. I salted my peaches. 
4. I used only 2 Tbsp of jam (I cut the recipe ingredients in half for 4-5 servings).
5. A bread loaf pan works well for 4-5 servings (1/2 recipe ingredients).

So good!
@Tracy Thank you! I was looking for someone who didn’t use the ricotta (-:
I'm curious as to the obsession with putting almond extract in almost every dessert these days. To me, it overwhelms all other flavors.

@SNB I am curious why you generalize the occasional recipe using almond extract as an obsession. It’s easier to leave it out if you don’t like than it is to write a condescending comment about other people‘à taste preferences.

@Debbie

Dear ms Debbie. SMB and I must be reading the same recipes. I agree. The dessert recipes I am perusing seem to have almond extract. I worked on a recipe two years ago with almond extract and the wife said “not my favorite” - 2 hours lost - after that I agree with you most wholeheartedly I skip the almond extract. I am even going as far as using walnuts instead of almonds. God willing you will take my reply with the humor that my brain intended. At my observation.

It might be you have a sensitivity to it. I know sometimes when people pick up on flavors more intensely the aversion is based on fact that their body just doesn't want it. For example, I know someone who said she couldn't stand baklava and similar desserts because they use rose water and it was off-putting. I said baklava uses rose water? (I love rose) 😊
Which kind of ladyfingers! The crispy, hard ones or the soft cake-like ones?

@Robin
I was wondering this same thing. Thanks for asking.

@Robin
Get the hard, crunchy ladyfingers.
@Sabine I could only get the soft ones where I live so I let them sit out overnight to dry out. Perfect!

Very summery! Made this with a peach simple syrup for the lady fingers since guests cannot drink any alcohol. Put sugar, water and some cut up peaches into pot and reduced by half. Dipped the lady fingers into this and layered in pan. Then alternated whipped cream-mascarpone mix and layers of peaches and also raspberries. Ended with the cream mixture. Everyone loved, so creamy and good!! (Have a little peach simple syrup leftover, too, for those who can drink cocktails!!!)
@GC Thank you for this! Sounds like the perfect adjustments for us.

Regarding the interest on non-alcohol syrup.

The wine can be replaced with some white grape juice plus some water, and maybe a few drops of lemon juice.

The contribution of the brandy, aside from alcohol, will be a subtle sweetness. Probably achieved in this recipe by adding a quarter cup of water and a tsp of brown sugar to the syrup recipe at the beginning, before simmering.
@Tom 190 thank you! I am making to take to work and want to make sure everyone who wants to have some is able to!
I doubled the whip cream recipe (and let the grandkids have at the leftovers).
Should the peaches be skinned? It doesn't say so but I can't imagine the skin would add anything to the dessert other than unnecessary roughage.

I was really looking forward to this dessert, however after making it, I think this recipe needs to be rethought. The ratios are all out of whack. Too few ladyfingers for this amount of cream. Too much peaches. It did not fit into a 9-inch pan at all. Three pounds of sliced peaches alone would barely fit I to a pan this size. And the flavors were flat. I spent a lot of money on excellent peaches, good quality dairy, and imported ladyfingers. No one enjoyed it. The peaches absolutely should be peeled/deskinned otherwise it’s a mess to cut through them. I’d advise dicing the peaches rather than slicing and macerating with 1-2T of honey and a bit of cinnamon or other warming spice along with the recipe’s jam ingredient. The cream wasn’t sweet enough. I’d also add a dash of cardamom or cloves for flavor depth and excitement. So much went uneaten into the yard waste, a real disappointment. Sorry David Tanis. I’m a fan, but this one goes back to the test kitchen.

NA recommendations for the syrup: Several good NA wines are available now. Two that I've seen widely available are Fre from California (around $10) and Zero Zero from Spain (around $10). As for NA liquors, rum seems to be the spirit that's easiest to emulate, and my brand recommendation is Ritual.
I'm assuming Italian ladyfingers are the hard crunchy ones, rather than the spongy ones used in trifles. Does anyone know?
Yes - I’m in Italy now - hard crunchy ladyfingers are the norm here for making tiramisu and the like
What’s the point of the rimmed baking sheet? Honestly asking.

Holy cow, this is one of the best things I've ever made, or eaten.  Followed the recipe exactly except had no creme fraiche so used plain greek yogurt.  easily getting 8 servings, it was piled high in my 9x9 pan.  Like a peach version of tiramisu. proportions of cake to cream to fruit was perfection.  i did peel the peaches as I don't care for a fuzzy skin.  WILL MAKE AGAIN ASAP lol

We used fresh local peaches and made a half peach & half ginger syrup as we had neither wine nor spirits. The mise en place took us a while. I do suggest future recipes put the recommended utensils and pans in a list rather than embed them in the recipe. I prefer the yield be included with the times.  Perhaps everything that will be divided could be identified in the ingredients. Finally, I understand serving yields are a guide, but 8-10 servings from a 9x9 pan is, shall we say, challenging.

I have never commented negatively on a recipe before but this…save yourself time, money and trouble. Just eat a fresh peach. What a waste. Way too many things masking the flavor of some perfectly good peaches.
I live in the Hill Country of Texas and during peach season, I sometimes have an abundance of peaches. I think this would might be a good way to use the ones past their peak.

@Marisa. That such a good problem, too many  peaches. You should make a  PEACH  spritzer. Just make a simply syrup flavored with peaches and obviously strain the syrup so it’s liquid and add ice cold sparkling water with ice.

I’ll make this recipe again, but will substitute the wine and only leave the brandy. The rest was light and fresh, but so much alcohol made it overwhelming and a bad after taste for my family. Also rather than dunk the last fingers, I’ll lay them out and brush the liquid on so they keep a little longer. Use ripe peaches! Otherwise, was good and will try making again.
So it’s trifle?
@Rebecca my thoughts exactly, just with a different layering order. Still looks delicious!!
Is everyone agreeing that this is best with peaches unpeeled, as recipe suggests?  Or did some of you peel off the skin to eliminate fuzz in your dessert?

@Skin on or off peaches? 
Rudis, above, noted many of the positive aspects of fruit skins and in terms of balancing a sweet dessert with some of the healthier aspects of nutrition, I think I’ll stick the skins in a blender.  With the dearth of fiber in the US diet, I’ll take it everywhere I can get it

@Skin on or off peaches?
I wanted a smooth, luscious, soft mouth feel and thought leaving the skin on would impact that.  Also, I wanted to serve rectangular sections, and I was concerned the skins would interfere with nice edges.  So, I blanched the peaches for 60 seconds, plunged them into ice water, and removed the skins.  That's just my preference, though; not a right or wrong opinion.

@Team Peach Fuzz
Clever to use blender!

Cassandra asked: "I'm assuming Italian ladyfingers are the hard crunchy ones, rather than the spongy ones used in trifles. Does anyone know?" To which I'd say, when you put the crunchy ones in trifles—dipped in a liquid and left to rest up against cream of some kind—they magically soften into something close to cake. I think traditionally you use the crunchy ones in trifles, which become soft. Hope that helps!
wow. did anyone actually make this recipe as written?
@Mimi Yes and I gave it 1*

This was delicious but I suggest halving the whipped cream mixture.  Also the ladyfingers soaked up the syrup very quickly and I ran out but used the excess peach juice for the rest.  Suggest adding the peach juice to the syrup so I would have had enough.  Used a 9X12 pyrex pan and did one layer as the two-layer would have been too high in my 9X9 pan.  Loved the almonds on top.

Nectarines have been sweeter than peaches so far this year and made a perfect fruit for this tiramisu. I couldn’t get crème fraiche easily so subbed with a mix of Greek yogurt with sour cream. Definitely will try again.
I used crunchy Italian almond cookies in my tiramisu, and I’d use them in this. Better flavor than ladyfingers

I make many versions of tiramisu during the summer using whatever fresh fruit is available. 
When I make peach versions I use Distilleries de Provence Rinquinquin a La Peche Liqueur France as the "syrup" just adding a touch of sweetener.  
I have found that the Goya brand of dried lady fingers works perfectly and are often half the price of imported or "Italian" style options.
Any ideas for a gluten-free substitution for the ladyfingers?  Would a gf yellow cake work?  So excited about making this--until I reached the last ingredient LOL!

A quick search reveals both commercial options and recipes for gluten-free lady fingers.

This looks like such a good recipe!
The brand Schar makes ladyfingers, and their products are usually good. Those are what I'm going to use when I try this!

I made exactly as the recipe stated and did not take off the peach skin.  that part came out fine.  However, i didn't care for the brandy in the syrup, or maybe I put too much in by accident. Or maybe it was really cheap (i.e., not good) brandy?  :-)  It created a very harsh alcoholic palette for an otherwise lovely desert (and I generally love alcohol in deserts). Next time i will probably use rum or (as someone else suggested), Cointreau.
Substituted orange juice for the alcohol which brought out the almond flavor nicely

Schar makes GF ladyfingers that work well for this recipe (our local Kings carries the brand or you can order). Delish, though thanks to other comments I used less dairy & slightly fewer peaches, & sweetened peaches with a tiny bit of sugar rather than buying a jar of peach preserves. I omitted almond extract & subbed amaretto for the brandy. Will make again, everyone loves ice box cake in the summer.

I’ve been making a version of this as a “peach tiramisu” for many years and it’s always a hit. I use almond milk to soak the ladyfingers and a mascarpone/egg cream to layer with the peaches. 

I will try this version!

Can anyone do this in 10 minutes? Not I! Cook the syrup, dip the lady fingers, slice the peaches, whip the cream, Oh My!
Needed only half the whipped cream, found the crème fraîche added very little (except more calories!), peaches were too chunky and did not need the jam (again, just calories), the ricotta added no taste, I needed a deeper pan, and it weighed a ton!

Should have just put the peaches on some vanilla ice cream.

I love all things tiramisu! But can’t eat prepared ladyfingers due to gluten. Is there any other store bought cake-like thing that would work? Or if I need to bake my own, what type of GF cake should I try to make?
I've found GF ladyfingers on Amazon in the past.

Thanks to whoever recommended the spring form pan, it came out beautiful. I used vanilla instead of the almond extract. Am thinking of leaving out the creme Franche so it will be lighter. Also would be beautiful with strawberries and raspberries when peaches are out of season.

How fabulous to find a dessert similar to the beloved Tiramisu. Having read the recipe & all helpful readers' comments I will make it directly into a crystal serving dish. Flat peaches are in season now & will consider almond or pistachio biscotti instead of lady fingers, or Amaretti biscuits alternatively. Endless possibilities. Yes I always use Amaretto & will sprinkle slivered almonds and or pistachios. And good idea to replace the wine with grape juice. Thank you

It was ok, 9" baking dish is too small, the second layer was mounded way up high.  I agree with other users to sprinkle additional sugar over the top and serve more syrup on the side.  I also agree with another user who added toasted sliced almonds to the top for some crunch.  The recipe is on the right track though, just needs some tinkering!

Personally, I don't like using lady fingers, so I'm going to bake a simple vanilla box cake, slice in half and brush with syrup.  Layer as recipe states.  I recently made a lemon 'tiramisu' and the lady fingers just turned to mush (used Goya brand - all I could find), that's why I'm averse to using them.
@KaneSugar an Italian ladyfinger brand holds up much better.
This is wa-a-ay too boozy! No one enjoyed it, or even ate their whole serving. I would leave out the wine/rum soak and put the rum in the whipping cream instead.
@Max C I boiled off some the alcohol in making the syrup and the taste was divine

I live in a small rural town, no ladyfingers available and I love to make this recipe today. I could make ladyfingers, but I could also make an awesome sturdy vanilla sponge (used it for trifle last weekend and I have a little crush on it at the moment). I know the result would be softer, different texture. Maybe good? Has anyone tried it with a softer cake, and if so, what did you think?
@CooksFish try vanilla wafers!
The sponge would work for sure, it would be more like a trifle than a tiramisu, but still delicious.
Would fresh frozen apricots work in this recipe?  Our apricot tree runeth over this year and I have 4 gallln bags filled with beautiful halved apricots.  Looking for another delicious use than jam.
I would definitely give it a shot, sounds delicious!

Great recipe for a hot summer in Texas. Our peaches were not great this year so I used frozen and defrosted and sliced them. People thought they were fresh! All loved it! I did leave out the almond extract too.
Why bother with making up a dipping liquid with almond extract? I thinned out amaretto with a little water & it was delicious!
Tried this with ripe, peeled mangos and the last of some guava preserves I had in the fridge. Decadently delish.

Add a little vanilla if not using brandy.  It does well with both almond and peaches.  I might sub a sparkling wine for the still white, do the brandy (maybe cut in half) and the vanilla. If avoiding alcohol all together, I'd use mango nectar instead of apple juice -- more flavor and no need to sweeten.

This is good, but really relies on the peaches being perfect. Though my peaches were ripe for New England, it tasted a little sharp. I made it the second time with mangoes, replaced most of the whipping cream with coconut cream and just made a small amount of regular 
whipped cream for the very top. I  left out some of the almond extract amd toasted coconut to sprinkle on top. The peach version was good, but the mango version was spectacular.
lovely trifle... but I wasn't crazy about the ricotta texture. I would omit next time for mascarpone. decrease the lemon zest a bit.

Fantastic!  Only problem is I correctly though to buy a second pkg of cookies and I needed 4 beyond the first pkg with about 1 “ cut off 3 of them.  Also, plots of peaches left and the whip cream mixture. I “sold” most of the extra peaches by leaving them out and letting people decide if they wanted more peaches.  The extra whip cream was never going to fit in my 8”x 8” sq pan.  A future solution would be to use a 9x13 pan, 2 pkgs cookies, and only one layer of the crème fraiche but everything else as recipe dictates.  We had 9 generous servings as is but the 9x13 pan would have served 12-14. I would make this again in a heartbeat!

Very boozy and not enough subtlety in flavor overall. Also not much variance in texture (too runny). I cut the almond extract in half which was a good call, otherwise it probably would've been overpowering.

Not bad, but wouldn't make again.
How do you keep the lady finger from tearing apart when adding the thick ricotta?
@Jude I did not encounter that problem at all. I did let them soak a little bit longer for the pieces I needed to tear apart to fill out the pan.

@Jude

You need the hard, crunchy kind of ladyfingers. They do not tear.
@Jude Same problem here - I ended up mixing whip cream into it to make it lighter and more spreadable
Would this work with gf ladyfingers?

This is just like and English trifle though we do not put sugar in the cream!
Delicious!!
If allergic to nuts, is there a substitute to use for the almond extract or can it just be left out?
@CSV you could use any syrup or extract you think would be pleasing. Ginger would be nice.
Can canned peaches be used here?

I only used half the amount of almond extract as it does take over the dessert. Although i weighed the peaches, there were too many for the amount of ricotta, whipped cream and biscuits. 9 inch square pan would have needed to higher.

Used wine but no brandy. White grape or apple juice would probably work but I would water it down. I used far less sugar and it still tasted heavenly. The sugar of the peaches and peach jam were plenty

This sounds delicious but like some others here, I’m looking for an alcohol-free version. I’m wondering if a brewed tea (earl grey perhaps) would work as a sub? I hate to branch off and change the flavor profile without trying it so maybe alcohol-free wine or a white grape juice with lemon is the best option for now.
What about trying a peach nectar juice?  May need some lemon juice so it's not only/too sweet.
Ladyfingers (Savoiardi)and Biscotti are NOT interchangeable...

The 9" pan is the wrong size.  This requires an 8" pan.  If a 9" pan is used, you need 14-16 lady fingers per layer, and there is not enough ricotta or whipped cream/ creme fresh for the layers.  We replaced the almond extract with more rum, as the almond overpowered everything the first time we made it.

Let’s start with the fact that Tiramisu is my absolute favorite dessert (NYT Cooking Classic Tiramisu is the bomb) and peaches are my favorite fruit. This is not super sweet but quite delicious. Add more powdered sugar to the ricotta if you like a sweeter dessert. Might experiment with Mascarpone mixed into the ricotta. I used a 10” springform pan instead of the 9” square and needed it for the volume and the presentation was nicer.
Has anyone tried using peach schnapps in lieu of the syrup? Seems like an easy substitute. Would it make it too boozy?
Just not that great. Definitely doesn't fit in a 9x9 pan. Peach flavor wasn't pronounced, not enough lady fingers, ricotta made it heavier than needed. Better options out there. Would skip it.

I’m certain that substituting Veuve Clicquot for the wine did no harm, but my rendition of the dish was meh.  My Mississippi grandmother used to toast slabs of pound cake, pile them with macerated peaches and then shovel on hand-churned vanilla ice cream.  I’m going to stick with that…
@Jon Mea culpa.  Apparently, waiting a full 24 hours before diving in key here.   Divine.
Made this yesterday, The ricota made a rather hard,rather dry layer, so if I make this again I would just leave it out.

Made a honey simple syrup with orange amaro 
Skipped the creme fresh and almond and add cardamom to whipped creme. Only 3tbs of sugar
Used ricotta di campagna and didn’t add any sugar
Peaches were a little under ripe so added brown sugar to mascerate

I most enjoyed this dessert as a topping for plain oatmeal the next morning. 
The sliced peaches made for some peach skin strands that made the dish’s texture less appealing, so next time I would cut each slice in half again to shorten the resulting peach skin bits. I would also reduce the almond extract by half, and use a larger cake pan.
Amazing! I used peach schnapps instead of rum, and used enough lady fingers to cover the pan- a bit more than the recipe calls for. This is easy and delicious! Everyone in my family loved it.

The ration of peaches/ladyfingers to “filling” isn’t quite right in this recipe.  All we could taste was the whipped cream and ricotta mixture.  I would suggest reducing the amount of both of these, particularly the whipped cream.  I did add some of the liquid from the peaches to the ricotta mixture to thin it and that helped with the thickness some of the other reviewers mentioned.  Also, toasted and chopped macadamia nuts on top were delicious.
