I made changes: Made the syrup as per the recipe. Layered the bottom of a 9-inch pan with dipped ladyfingers. Covered that with 15 oz of lightly sweetened, whipped ricotta (used food processor). Covered that with a layer of sliced peaches mixed with jam. Covered that with another layer of ladyfingers. Topped with cream: I used 1.5 cups of whipping cream, two tbsp. of powdered sugar, and 4 oz. of mascarpone, whipped until fairly stiff. Chilled. Ditched the créme fraîche completely.

This turned out to be way more cake than my 9" pan would hold. Also, although it tasted good, it just seemed way more cream filling than necessary, what with whipped cream, cream fraiche and ricotta. If I make it again I will try to eliminate some of those calories. I mean, I know - dessert. But still, it seemed excessive.

🍀Didn’t have almond extract (nor is it something I’ll ever use so I didn’t buy it) so I toasted up some almonds and shaved them on top of the cake at the end. 

I recommend getting a deeeep pan for this. I should have used my ceramic bread loaf pan. This recipe would have been better if the height of the pan was specified. I ended up having to use two different pans, each with one layer of each topping.
What fruit could I substitute for the peaches? mango?

@Susan
Mango would be an excellent choice.

Strawberries! Blueberries? Nectarines...I'm thinking most stone fruit or things that macerate well, which could then simply be prepared with a light dusting of sugar before being layered into the dessert.
@Rebekka I’m with you! Why ruin beautiful fresh fruit with jam?
You don’t peel the peaches???
@Jan Coston well, Rudin and I might not, but I’m not certain where  -or if- the majority lies.

I made the syrup with wine and cointreau because that is what I had and have a lot left over. Maybe make only 1/2 next time. I could not find lady fingers. Instead I used Walker's Ginger cookies, though thought of biscotti might work, too, but they looked too big and thick and bulky. The cookies soaked up the syrup nicely and was able to crumble pieces into the spaces between the larger cookies. Used a 10" spring form pan and filled it to the brim. In the fridge now awaiting the taste test.

I made the syrup with wine and cointreau because that is what I had and have a lot left over. Maybe make only 1/2 next time. I could not find lady fingers. Instead I used Walker's Ginger cookies, though thought of biscotti might work, too, but they looked too big and thick and bulky. The cookies soaked up the syrup nicely and was able to crumble pieces into the spaces between the larger cookies. Used a 10" spring form pan and filled it to the brim. In the fridge now awaiting the taste test.
I couldn't find creme fraiche, so I made my own. However, it turned out a little runny. Can I still use it for this recipe?

@Kg i'm guessing it's because in a 9 x 9 pan you're piling this really high and some of it drips over the edges ( at least mine did!).  Also, if you're messy like me, placing it on the rimmed sheet while you're preparing it will keep your countertop cleaner lol

Tiramisu typically does not include ricotta, just whipped cream, and therefore it freezes well.  I want to make this but freeze half for another time.  Does anyone know if the ricotta will weep after thawing?  If so, I'll just skip it.

I loved this take on Tiramisu! I didn't have a tall enough container as I used a 7-8 inch diameter for this so had to improvise. I used cream cheese and Honey Greek yogurt for the creme fraiche which worked well. Maybe something a bit more contrasting to soak the ladyfingers in next time, not sure what though.

Peaches 'n' Creammmmm Mmmm!  Luscious!   Followed the directions almost as written.  I found perfectly ripe local peaches.  Decided to blanch and peel them because I wanted a smooth, soft mouth feel.  Used peach brandy and a dry rose.  Didn't have the right size pan, so I couldn't add the final layer of whipped cream.  Just served it on the side. It looked pretty with peaches on top.   Actually made 2 cakes: one gluten free using a recipe for GF ladyfingers  I found online.
I’m wondering why sugar is needed in the whipped cream? If I was making a Victoria Sandwich with strawberries and cream I wouldn’t put sugar in the cream.
Any suggestions for gluten-free substitutions for the lady fingers?
I ordered GF ladyfingers on line and got them quickly.  But they were HUGE!  "Giant" fingers.  But they worked fine, soaked up the syrup very quickly.  Good dessert

@akopper for a slightly different flavour profile, I’d go with amaretti, especially the soft type, but also the other ones would do. Peaches and amaretti are a match made in heaven and something very popular in Italian summer desserts. Just be sure to cut down the sugar content accordingly as amaretti are very sweet by themselves. 
Btw this is a substitution I’d suggest to everyone, not just for gluten free requirements.
Absolutely THE best!
Looks great, but unless you are living in a major metropolitan area, it's impossible to find ladyfingers these days.
Can the cake be prepared the day ahead of time and kept in the fridge?
How do I halve this so I can make if for just my husband and myself?
So, what's the purpose of the rimmed baking sheet? I don't get it.
@Sylvie probably to contain any drips
Any way to make this with no alcohol?
Try replace the brandy try peach kombucha and blend in some peach jelly while using peach juice and lemon to replace the white wine.
There is also a Tanis Raspberry Nectarine no bake recipe on this site, quite similar.

I prepped two ripe peaches last night for eating with ice cream... or whatever... so this is definitely timely! Like others, I will use the basic concept but with far "lighter" and non-alcoholic ingredients. The essential sensation I crave (it's August, people) is the flavor of the peaches, a bit of sweetness, and cool, soothing topping. Could be vanilla ice cream, could be commercial whipped toppings, or lots of heavy cream just drizzled on top? :0

We and our company loved the dessert - the key was local peaches!  Thank you Chiles peach orchard in Crozet, VA.  As I looked at the recipe I was concerned that a typical 9 in pan would not be deep enough. This came to prove true…it’s also not clear what constitutes a ‘lady ginger’.  Our packages of lady fingers contain 12 split fingers.  I decided a finger was both halves BUT 12 would not cover a 9 inch pan…I used a 10 inch springform pan (yes it leaked, definitely put it in a lipped jelly roll pan), a layer of half fingers (24) lightly sprinkled with the sugar sauce (I knew it would leak down into the layer).
the Loopy Whisk (all of her recipes are amazing)  has a gf ladyfingers recipe. also Schar brand makes a gf ladyfinger@Julie

This was a disappointing recipe. It was basically peaches and cream. The amounts called for were way too much for a 9 inch square pan. I used a larger pan and still ended up with tons of peaches and whipped cream left over.
Are there gluten fre  lady fingers? Has anyone tried making a gluten free form of this? It sounds so divine.
@Kate There are! World Market had regular and GF in their Italian cookie section.
Maybe this is a dumb question, but do you peel the peaches? It just says halved, pitted and cut in slices. I don't have any experience baking with peaches so... wondering
I assume the peaches have the skin removed, yes?
Do you peel the peaches?
@Colleen yes, peel the peaches

This is a very expensive dish to make...for the pice I would just whip up some sweetened heavy cream, slice a very ripe and juicy peach and layer it on a crunchy Italian lady finder.  Plus, while it all fit in a 9 inch square pyrex dish, it was way over the top.  When serving it, the tirimisu collapsed and it was just a mound--not very appetizing.  I did skin the peaches and not sure if I would do it again.

This was exquisite! I read all the comments about the ratio of cake to cream, and the use of almond extract, and how the volume of all the layers was too much for the 9-inch pan. Have to say, none of these were an issue. This is a tasty recipe, perfect for the dog days of summer when the peaches are at their peak.
I mixed the cream and ricotta together, used mascarpone instead of sour cream. I didn't use all the cream mixture.
What would make a good substitute for almond extract?
The flavors did not blend,and it was too boozy. Mash the peaches, and use juice in place of rum.
This was a huge hit at a very foodie-focused gathering.  Absolutely delicious!
Hello, I’m new to baking. Do we think mangos could work with this? I live in Hawaii and have best access to tropical fruits.

To Die For! I used hazelnut as I had no almond essence. Frangelico in lieu of brandy, with a generous dollop also added to the cream mix.  I also reduced the sugar by a quarter as us Europeans don’t have such a sweet tooth. My husband and both my young adult sons loved it.
My dinner guest canceled. Can I freeze this?

I agree with the cook who said the totality of the ingredients were too much for the 9" x 9" pan. I used a 10" springform pan and I still had some of the whipped cream/creme fraiche mixture left over. This is a fairly good recipe and the appearance is impressive but it's putzy. I think I can make something very similar with less effort. Also, I would skip the ricotta next time; it doesn't add anything except density.
made 1/4 recipe and I think it will last me 4 servings. subbed greek yogurt for creme fraiche and honey for jam. such a huge payoff for the effort!

Based on other comments, I used a springform pan and needed the extra height. 3 lbs of sliced peaches would not fit. I probably used 2 lbs. if using a springform pan be sure and have rimmed cookie sheet underneath as there is leakage. I used a pizza pan.
We substituted PEAR RUM and it was sublimmme. This is the perfect *chilled* late-summer cake. We will make it again.

@Tracy I agree about the ricotta texture. It was off-putting for me. I would perhaps try some lemon curd whipped into a creamy layer instead. 
Don't think I honestly could detect the tiny bit of almond extract at all.

5 stars for flavor, but why do you need to put the 9” square pan on a baking sheet if not baking?  And how in the world do you fit these layers into a 9” square pan?  My pan was full to the brim before the last layer of whipped cream.  Cutting and serving was also an issue!  I’d highly recommend some way to cut lady fingers to fit a springform pan!

9x9 pans are uncommon — my baking dish is 8x8 — so I made this in a 9.5 inch deep dish pie plate.  It is about 10 sq inches (71 vs 81) smaller than 9x9.  It would be great if the Times used common sizes!
However, the heaping conglomeration won rave reviews.   Fresh SC peaches from the local farmers market were key.  Don’t bother if your peaches don’t cause instant states of bliss.  I used fresh cream from a very local dairy and bakery lady fingers.  The booze didn’t hurt either.

Like others, I don’t have a 9x9 pan and had to use an 8x8 … but I’m not sure those extra inches would have done much to contain this hulk of a dessert. That said, it was a hit with my guests and worth the effort. I really appreciated it wasn’t overly sweet. Next time I’ll reduce the peaches to 2 lbs because I had so many leftover.
This was a soggy mess and I followed it to the letter.
This is a lovely recipe.  A couple of notes:  be sure your peaches are very ripe and soft.  And it takes 48 ladyfingers for a 9 x 9 pan.  Makes enough for 10-12 people, despite what the recipe says.
I used Captain Morgan rum and mango chutney for a savory kick. Was delicious, will probably always make it like that!

Made this for a large dinner party and it went down really well. I think it would be better with half the volume of cream. Used tinned peaches and mango juice instead of the alcohol. I omitted the almond essence and on reflection the cream lacked a bit of flavour. Will make again with a few tweaks. It definitely needs to be in a bigger receptacle !
Ripe should read “very ripe.”Mine were a little too firm.
I got the last box of ladyfingers (only 12), so there was way less cake than optimum. Still, everyone loved it. But next time, I'll make sure to have enough ladyfingers.

title: Miso Crab Cakes Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: Anybody in New England who puréed a scallop would be sent to New York to become a Yankee fan.
@Dave Anyone who puts ginger and puréed scallops in their crab cakes should be deported

It would be more helpful for commentators to try something they wouldn't have thought of doing - like puréeing scallops - than just expressing skepticism. Then share whether it works.

I'm sure a lot of trial and experience went into these recipes, and delight comes when something unexpected works!
Hmm.  Puréeing scallops seems like a waste. Could the same  binder effect be achieved with the same amount of cod?
@Thom I agree…if anything it would be a scallop crab cake with the scallops cut into may 4 or 6 pieces…
Why, in my right mind, would I puree scallops?

Well, goodness!  I am not usually a huge scallop fan, but the idea of puréeing them and adding them as a binder rather than something like mayonnaise, and allowing their  sweetness and brininess to, along with the miso, complement the delicacy of the crabmeat, sounds simply divine.  Planning to give it a try! (And yes, pricey, but with 1.5 lbs crabmeat, .5 lbs of scallops is a matter of in for a penny in for a pound!)
I have been using bay scallops and a little cream or half n half in place of mayo for years as the binder for my crab cakes!
Rather than scallops use cheap farm-raised shrimp as a binder.

Made this for a special-occasion. The scallop-miso-egg binding does elevate the cakes. No salt needed given the natural salty-sweetness of the seafood. Used Kewpie mayo for the dip, foregoing the additional miso.

Overall, worth it! That includes pureeing the scallops. I don't share the sense of sacrilege; fine-dining restaurants puree all sorts of premium ingredients, and experimenting can make food (and life) more flavorful. The scallops aren't wasted but enjoyed as part of the cakes.
Why use pricey sea scallops for pureeing into a binder? Seems like bay scallops would work as well?
I use only lump crab meat and , green /red peppers finely diced . Egg white , panko bread crumbs and old bay seasoning . Baked not fried and they are delicious . Save the s allows for another day .
Riiight, we're going to buy sea scallops at $22-25 per lb. and then, puree them?
How would I adjust the recipe for frying in an air fryer rather than a pan?
@IK Maybe you could try it and report back here on how it worked out?

This is probably one of the most expensive recipes the NYT has posted in a long time .
To use crabmeat and purée scallops seems like a waste of two wonderful ingredients, each of which deserves it’s own recipe
Agreed. No reason not to just use mayo and a little bit of panko or saltines.

I made THIS recipe.  The scallop purée gave the cakes a sweet, creamy texture that I liked.  The mixture was too loose (maybe I didn’t dry the scallops very well) so I added a small amount of crushed crackers to the mixture.  After browning In fry pan, I finished them in a 350 degree toaster oven for 7 minutes

I followed the directions to make the cut cakes and the
dipping sauce faithfully except I didn’t coat in panko and fry. I baked at 400° for approximately 20 minutes. It was good! It might be too salty for some people - both the dressing and the crabcakes. 

I do like the taste of the miso but I would probably use less next time. The scallops worked really well as a binder. It was a very fish forward for a crabcake. 😂
What's the purpose of the scallops? Why can't the crab stand alone?
Any idea how to reheat the next day (without drying out) as suggested in the article in which this recipe appeared?
Can they be made without scallops? I am allergic to them. What is a good substitution?
Being allergic to shellfish I substituted deboned rotisserie chicken moistened with chicken stock.

As long time east coast crab lovers ( prior Baltimore resident) we voted this recipe the best one for crab cakes we have ever eaten! The dipping sauce and pickles are wonderful accompaniments. Thank you Mr. Tanis!

title: Chilled Cucumber-Spinach Soup  Recipe
author: David Tanis

stats.Cook Time: 30 minutes, plus cooling and chilling time

comments.commentBody: Step 2 is unclear: does the two cups of water that had been added to the spinach mixture also go in the sheet pan/platter to cool or is the spinach mixture alone removed from that water?? If the water also cools in the sheet pan, how can the slushy mixture be neatly transferred to the food processor/blender??
Personally, I see this as a job for my immersion blender.
@JudyF I used tongs to put the spinach in the blender from the sheet pan. Then I tilted the sheet pan and let the liquids pour from a corner into the blender. Did it in two batches. Worked well.
Presumably we strain the cooked stuff before spreading it out on a sheet pan? :)
My question exactly...

Haven't made it yet but if I do I plan to skip the sheet pan business and just let it cool in the pot before blending.  Washing sheet pans is a pain but waiting for something to cool is not.  I'll usually use a wide deep skillet for something like this anyway, allowing for faster cooling.
was delighted to see a summer cucumber soup recipe that did not include finishing with cream or yoghurt. Will try this one.

Made this exactly following recipe. Very bland!!!. Needs some type of spice. The color was lovely but big disappointment.
Toasted the tofu. Don't use silken. The extra firm works better.
Consider hot pepper flakes to amp taste or lemon juice.
I missed the part about peeling the cucumbers. Don't be like me.

One of the reasons I admire David Tanis so much is his subtlety. In early summer when you have access to freshly picked cucumbers with dense, crisp flesh that were grown in fields vs. greenhouses the size of NJ, the delicate flavor of this soup is a pleasure. Climate change unfortunately means that your spinach will not have been harvested under the same conditions, but if it didn't come in a plastic clamshell you might pull off a nice bowl of soup. You can always grab a bag of Flaming Cheetos afterwards.
This sure looks a lot like a smoothie recipe...

I’m on Team Bland. It just seems to be missing something—I think it needs some acid, and I gave it a splash of seasoned rice vinegar, but it kind of disappeared into the vague savoriness of it all. Not bad, just meh.
David I usually like your recipes but I don't know what went wrong here. Ended up with a tasteless, khaki colored watery liquid. Perhaps it was my fault,  but nevertheless awful!

Made it for lunch today and served in verines as a first course. I put it in the fridge to chill already served in verines and it went really fast. I served it 45 minutes later and it was chilled perfectly. 
For garnishes I just put Thai basil from my garden and a drizzle of roasted sesame oil. I skipped the soy sauce and the tofu since it was a first course and didn’t need the protein. If I had it as a main course however I would add the tofu but I think I’d still skip the soy sauce. The spinach flavor is so good that I don’t want to lose it.

Made this last night.  It was strange.  Not bad.  Just weird.  I won't be making it again. At first I didn't put though a strainer because I figured the Vitamix would do the job. But for the second bowl, I did strain and it was better.

I would recommend this as a soup base but that's about it. More flavor please. Here's what I added and suggest:
- I started to strain it as listed, which resulted in "spinach water." I left all the fiber instead. 
- I add garlic powder, onion powder and it was still on the bland side.
- Next time I make this, I'll leave out the sesame oil and soy and finish with fresh tomatoes, diced cucumbers, sour cream or plain yogurt and a drizzle of olive oil. Think "spinach-based gazpacho."

This one wasn't what I was hoping for (love cold soups and gazpachos of all colors). In tasting as I prepped, the spinach-leek-pimeton mixture was really nice, but in straining it you loose all the texture a chilled soup has--I added in a few spoons of the strained spinach to try to course correct but couldn't bring it back. Final result was watery and not flavorful. 

The miso crab cakes that went along with this summer supper WERE really lovely though. Not all was lost ;)

I saw others comments that this was bland so i used boxed veg broth instead of water, added a garlic clove and a dollop of white miso— used an immersion blender to keep things simple and didn’t strain— and it was still bland. Would not make this again. Nine of the foodies in my family liked it. Refuse to dump what i have left so thinking i’ll add some cream fraiche and see if that helps.

The texture confused me. I cranked a Vitamix up to 8 (had to add a couple Tbs of water so it wouldn’t seize up), and still got a texture like finely chopped spinach. Straining was impossible unless I wanted about a cup of clear, spinach flavored cucumber water and many cups of solid discard.

Agree with others that the flavor is basic. Maybe ancho or chipotle chili powder instead? Green hatch powder, maybe? Or go in a different direction and try baharat or curry. I’m inexperienced with cold soups so I’m afraid to just chuck in garlic. I really wanted to like this but the flavor doesn’t make up for the texture.

One really needs to add much more spice than is indicated in the recipe. In addition, this is not a recipe for silken tofu. Silken tofu is too fragile and does not lend itself to being cut into cubes. In order to make this recipe more palatable I added much more basil.

I can’t eat soy, so I substituted tahini for the tofu and just put a dollop on top of the soup when serving.  Then I dotted sesame oil and my substitute for soy sauce, coconut aminos, around the tahini also on top of the soup.  It was delicious and pretty and left a new flavor in my mouth with each bite.  In the recipe, I made sure to add plenty of salt and pepper and I also included all of the sesame oil and soy substitute from the ingredients in the blending.

I used 2-10 oz packages of fresh frozen spinach, 2 cups of water to cook briefly (so still bright green) and skipped the whole sheet pan since it’s getting blended anyway. I added a little more water but liked to have it thick.  I upped the amount of smoked paprika to 1 tsp.  To give it more flavor, I added about 1/3 c of white miso.  I increased the amount of tofu to make sure everyone got a good dollop of that tasty mixture.  It was an unusual treat for a hot day!

title: Melon-Mint Sorbet Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: can this be made without an ice cream machine?

I don't have an ice cream machine, but imagine that this melon-mint mix would make lovely popsicles (perhaps adding a shot of tequila to the mix for funzies).  Now I just need to remember where I stored my popsicle molds at the end of last summer...
Made according to recipe, it was a bit sweet for us so we would reduce agave next time. Did not make raspberry sauce but topped with fresh raspberries, delicious!
I don't have an ice cream maker, how can you make this as a granita?

Almost any fruit can be pureed as a granita -- but I always add some alcohol, would use Midori - Italian melon flavored here to inhibit freezing.   One can also partially freeze then beat it up!.   Have not made this recipe; did something with strawberries the other day  -- and Midori.
Replying to Auntie Mama: I believe Midori is a Japanese liqueur, not Italian. Delicious, though!
What if I don’t have agave can I use Honey

title: Oven Chicken Kebabs Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: Instead of the spices and salt you can use Penzey's Turkish seasoning. Serve the kebabs wrapped in a warm pita with chopped cucumber and tomato and slices of red onion and drizzled with a garlicky yogurt sauce flavored with dill, cumin, mint and lemon.
These kabobs were easy and really delicious! We like spicy and they did the trick. Served them with pita, hummus, tzatziki, cucumbers and tomatoes.

We prefer our picnics indoors — air-conditioned and no ants — and this was a delicious meal. This is a spicy meat mix, so if you are cooking for the timid of palate, you may want to tone it down a bit. Thanks, Julie, for the suggestion of tzatziki sauce. I suppose you could turn the meat into burgers or meatballs, but the skewers are fun. What to drink? Rose, of course!

I used lamb, and Aleppo pepper instead of marash, and shaped the meat into large quenelles instead of using skewers. It was very good served with saffron rice, but if anyone makes the cucumber salad that is suggested as a side dish in the article, I would advise going easy on the lemon juice.

As much as I try to make a recipe precisely the first time, I had to improvise a bit tonight (thawed, ground chicken and a very fresh onion meant extra moisture) and added some panko. I made patties instead, and, guess what? They were phenomenal. David Tanis writes great recipes, and this is a very forgiving one. I made a little white sauce with mayo, sour cream (no yogurt on hand), feta, lemon zest, garlic, s+p. This might be my new go-to chicken "burger" recipe for the summer. Happy accident!
Made it with lamb. Only had zatar- not plain sumac- it was delish.  Served with Tanis’ cucumber yogurt salad and cherry tomato salad with warmed pitas- everyone loved it.

The spice combo sounds yummy; ground chicken not so much.  I'd be tempted to try this with boneless/skinless chicken thighs pounded flat with a meat mallet, cut into strips, marinated in yogurt then rolled in the spices before skewering.  And maybe put some of the spice mix in the marinade. 

As for eating, I always perferred naan to pita bread; more flavorful.
If choosing to broil, don’t use parchment!
can you please suggest a way to use white meat. my partner's doctor said to stay away from dark meat; too fatty. thanks
@Lszap There are hundreds of recipes on this site for chicken breast. Perhaps try one of those?
@Lszap use white meat, and just accept that it won’t be as juicy/flavorful.

@Lszap snarky reply to a good question. This is basically a meatball with no binder on a stick. No binder is why the mixture needs to be so cold. So, add some binder (1/2 cup panko to an egg, egg beater to watch the cholesterol) to your white to ground chicken breast and a bit more olive oil. Good for you for watching out for your partner.
I don't know if it is a mistake or not, but two tablespoons of red pepper flakes is overwhelming, and I like spicy food.  I cut it back to two teaspoons and it still had plenty of "kick."

@Tommy

Maras pepper is milder than crushed peppers.  Two tablespoons just gives a little kick,
Amazing spice level! Didn’t have sumac so did a splash of lemon juice. Paired with rice and the roasted broccoli with tahini garlic sauce from NYTimes too!
Anyone try this with ground turkey?
I was going to do that, since I have no idea how to find "not white meat" ground chicken.
I did and it was great. I used the high-fat turkey, not the lean stuff. I forgot to put in the olive oil and it was juicy enough without it, and probably even more with it.

Edith, I check the meat/fat content listed on the ground chicken package. If it's very lean (for example, 98% meat, 2% fat), it's breast meat. But ~93% meat/7-8% fat is made with dark meat like thighs. But, of course, turkey would work as well.
Anyone try this with ground “impossible” meat???
Made 1 lb ground beef mixture. Changed proportions of spices. Rolled into meatballs.  Delicious!
you didn't make the recipe at all

Sandy is correct!

On a similar note, I used pork side ribs, basted with a honey-mesquite glaze, and did 'em low and slow on the BBQ

Delicious!
FYI...non white meat ground turkey is available at whole foods and sometimes chicken, also

I guess I will be the contrarian here.  I don’t have a “timid palate.”  When I tasted the results, I went back to look at the recipe to see if I had misread it and the tablespoons of cumin and pepper flakes were supposed to be teaspoons instead. The toasted ground cumin seeds and red-pepper flakes completely took over the flavor profile, and not in a good way.  So, I would cut those two ingredients in half for a better overall result.  I suspect that this comment will engender some “spice shaming” replies, but I’ll stick by my assessment that these spice proportions are out of whack.
These were really good. I had to substitute for sumac as I read that it may have the same proteins as nuts, and we have an allergy. I substituted dried lemon zest and it tasted great!
has anyone tried these in the air fryer?

Love this recipe. Made it a few times as is and it’s a keeper in my regular rotation .But yesterday decided to give it a Mediterranean flair and added fresh spinach kalmata olives and feta . So so good
Hypothetically, if one happened to to have ground chicken instead of turkey would this work ok?  Im no expert on ground meats but thinking they are functionally similar, no?

KJ, this recipe IS for ground chicken....

So, yes, if you have ground chicken, I think it should work out jes' fine!

For anyone who has made the recipe - is there a reason to use skewers if baking in oven? Why not just shape the meat and leave it alone? Have seen something similar on social media - you shape the meat into a sheet, and then divide with a spatula into kebab shaped/sized portions.

Hi Mike, My husband made these last weekend as written. I asked him to make again for dinner last night.I talked him into making meatballs because it made no sense to me to put them on skewers in the oven . I Love this recipe put with yogurt ,cucumber, garlic , parsley on greek flatbread . Yes meatballs are the way to go but beware they are addictive .
@Mike You do not need the skewers unless you are grilling. I just shaped the meat like a kebab and roasted it in the oven that way. It was great and no skewers to wash afterwards.
Mine came out very soft and mushy. Make sure that yours are thin, hopefully thatll do the trick

This is a very delicious meat recipe. I added an egg, some panko and used meat as basis for meatballs with tomato sauce. Served with pasta, roasted broccoli & roasted mushrooms. Will next try as meatloaf!

I'm confused!   You "used meat".  Well, the recipe calls for 'meat'! Chicken, specifically.


Do you mean you used some other kind of meat?
I have several packages of ground turkey breast. What, if anything, would I need to adjust to use this in the recipe.  Thanks

I agree with the comment on the amount of Red Pepper Flakes. 2 Tbs is too much for me. Also, I made these without skewers. Shaped them into meatballs and made some Tzatziki for dipping. Came out great!
Would these kabobs freeze and reheat well? I’d probably have leftovers from the full recipe.

Great recipe but is surprising in it's lack of garlic. I have never had kebabs without it and respectfully suggest these are not authentic because of that. We added a couple of tbsps of minced garlic and it took it up to the notch I'm used to. Also substituted Zatar for the sumac a couple of tsps of chili flakes for the red pepper flakes. Will make it again for sure!

I made this recipe with lamb, but otherwise as written. The lamb was very flavorful, juicy and my family loved it. Highly recommend this recipe - simple and delicious! I made saffron rice and roasted carrots on the side which were also great and paired well. I will make this as a gyro next time and believe that would be great as well.

I was cooking for only two, so halved the recipe. Couldn't find any ground chicken or turkey that wasn't white meat only, so opted for turkey and added a tablespoon of ghee. Otherwise made as instructed in the oven, and they were a big hit. Served with turmeric rice, tzatziki, and harissa. Yum.

Followed it to a tee, and would only make 2 modifications. Love the spice, but would put in half of the red pepper flakes as that made it spicy enough. Added 1/4 teaspoon ground allspice, which added a very nice additional flavor profile.  Otherwise, excellent!!

So tempting, but I'd suggest finding another option for this kind of dish. Yes, the cumin is toasted first, but 15 minutes of oven time didn't take the raw edge off the overall large volume of spices. To us, the kebabs were a mouthful of spices encased in salty ground meat. Love the idea, not here for the outcome.
Perfect recipe for an easy lamb based meal.
Has anyone subbed anything for the sumac with success?
Wonderful combo.  We really pulled back on the salt - a teaspoon rather than the suggested tablespoonful.  Used Korean chili flakes as a substitute.

@edith i often make my own ground chicken with boneless skinless chicken thighs.  I place them in the freezer for half hour or so to make them a bit more solid then use the food processor to "mince" it.  And since most ground chicken (and turkey) has a lot more moisture in it, this method typically makes most ground chicken recipes taste and hold shape so much better

I thought I had sumac and realized part way through the recipe I did not, so I subbed some lemon zest & smoked paprika in addition to the regular paprika. I reduced chili flakes so kids could eat them, too. So even though I strayed significantly from the spices in the recipe everything else was the same and they were delicious! Didnt bother skewering and shaped on the pan instead. Delicious in a pita with cucumber, pickled onions, lettuce & garlic sauce!

Very tasty and easy. Crowd pleasure for sure. I didn't toast the cumin and just put in already ground cumin. If you do this, make sure to adjust the amount since a tbsp of cumin seeds toasted and then ground would likely be less than 1 tbsp of already ground cumin. My kebabs were a little hot on the cumin. Although some tzatziki and pita would have sorted that out most likely!

I made one batch of chicken and one of lamb. I didn't have any sumac so I used lemon rind instead (internet suggested replacement) - about 2/3 tbsp. The lemon flavor was a little stronger than I would have liked but the skewers all got rave reviews from our guests. The lamb in particular. Next time I will probably drain the juice from the grated onion before adding so the raw mixture is a little less wet. Delicious and easy. Would make again.

These were awesome and super flavorful! 
like other comments, we opted for the meatball route (great idea for the dead of winter in Wisconsin) and baked them.
the recipe called for 2 lbs of chicken,  so I halved the spices, but only used a teaspoon of chili flakes and it was plenty spicy. 
I added one egg and a quarter cup of panko for the meatball route. baked at 375 for about 20 minutes. served with store bought tzatziki (sp?) sauce and a curry rice.

These were a little labor/time intensive, but really yummy. I cooked for a group, so used 3lbs chicken and 1.5x's the spice amounts. Used ground cumin, and could not find Sumac so used Za'atar instead, then went a little light on the cumin as a result. I am sensitive to spice so only used 2.5tsp pepper flakes for 3lbs meat. Turned out perfectly! Put them on greased foil instead of parchment since I was going to broil at the end. They were really delicious served on warmed pita with tzatziki.

This was great. I used what I had: smoked pepper flakes (1T was enough for us) instead of plain plus zatar instead of oregano, and then the rest of the seasoning as stated, and these turned out terrific. I baked them into little sliders to tuck into pitas and served with olives, hummus, and Melissa Clark's grilled eggplant salad and they were devoured.

title: Herbed Cucumber-Yogurt Salad Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I served the herbed cucumber-yogurt salad yesterday along with grilled beef kebabs and a couscous salad. It was delicous! This salad can’t be compared with tzatziki (I am not a big fan of). The texture of the thinly sliced cucumber and creamy yogurt but more important the taste of the mint, dill, lemon zest and juice (I did not add cayenne or chile) make this salad indeed to a „cooling, pleasently refreshing saucy accompaniment to grilled meat“. I will make it all summer long! Thank you David.

Slice/lightly salt cucumber/onion ahead and let drain.  Pre-mix the other ingredients.  Stir everything together shortly before serving.  Less chance of a watery salad.  Most any combo of fresh herbs can work.  My mother made a cucumber salad like this all summer.  She used sour cream.  And a small pinch of sugar to tame the acidity a bit.   Fond memories of this, sliced garden tomatoes, and a bit of steak, pork or chicken off the grill.  Perfect supper!

Maast O Khiar

Simple and delicious. For a bit more taste add a bit of sour cream.
This was overly acidic and I was careful with the lemon juice.
This is a common dish in South India and gees by the name of "Pachadi' or Raitha in North India. In addition to cucumber and onions it is sometimes embellished with gropes or sliced pineapple.

Was truly delish especially as a side to filet.. Does remind me of tzatziki but the mint gives it a more complex finish that compliments the garlic, and doesn't let it take over.  Mint is a remarkable herb I want to use in more salads..

To make the Yoghurt less soupy line a colander or 4 cup sieve with cheesecloth resting 2-3 inches above the bottom of a pot.  Then  put all or part of Yoghurt in, let drain for about 1 hour.  Remove the thickened Yoghurt add to other ingredients and fold together.  I’ve always made with diced, seeded cucumbers, adds less liquid.
Salt the cucumbers lightly before adding to yogurt. Thinly sliced is nicer texture and look than diced.
If you salt the cucumbers and let them drain for 15 minutes first, the salad will be less watery.

I have always made a simple cucumber/onion salad.  Instead today, I followed these intructions.  It was fine.  It would have been better with a little sour cream; the olive oil is probably unnecessary; the chili and cayenne made it spicy but that was not actually an improvement.  I think I'll go back to my traditional salad: thin cucumbers and onion; chopped dill; 1/3 sour cream to 2/3 full fat greek yogurt, salt and pepper - chill and serve. Lovely, no fuss.
Easy and really delicious. I have to admit I was a bit skeptical - would this really be such an improvement by just adding lemons? And the answer is yes.

Very good.  Easy and quick to make. Refreshing.   However, not special.  I would use less yogurt next time. And since I use homemade yogurt, which is already tart, I might add a touch of honey or sugar to lessen the sourness of the yogurt, but still keep the lemon for its flavor.
Make it the Latvian way, with thinly sliced cucumbers, dill, salt and sour cream instead.
@Anna In my house it’s the Hungarian way!

Flavors are great!  I didn’t have access to a chile, but did include the cayenne, although it was not noticeable, but I’ll increase next time.  The reason for the 4 stars was the amount of yogurt - I used the metric measurements and included the required grams of cucumber plus about 40 grams.  The recipe also calls for 3 cups of yogurt, which I also completed on grams according to the yogurt tub nutrition facts.  As I was adding it, I thought this is a lot of yogurt-I ended up adding 1.9 cups according to my calculations.  Unfortunately, it still a lot of yogurt.  It still tastes great, but it’s kinda a soupy mess of a cucumber salad as more water has been released by the cucumber because of the salt.  If that’s your jam, go for it, but I think I’ll do a third of the yogurt next time.
Not a fan of dill; so I suggest replacing it with fennel fronds.

I read through the comments first.   Salted the cucumbers, squeezed the water, then rinsed off all the salt.  Maceration.  Onions I soaked in water making them mild.  For the yogurt I had 11% so it was rich and creamy,  I followed the advice of another to prepare it separately - which I then added to the cucumbers.  Used 1/2 yogurt portion - it was plenty.  The extra fat yogurt was able to absorb the lemon juice so it wasn't runny.  Added honey to cut the acid.  Nice served in bibb lettuce bowl.

Try using Greek yogurt or Skyr. Even low fat versions of those can provide a thicker and creamier texture. 

This tastes good as a topping or dipping sauce for burritos, tacos, gyros, pita wraps, chopped salad greens, or mixed with beans or chickpeas… lots of options. 

I even like it on its own, as a topping on really good, crusty bread. By adding a little kefir or buttermilk to thin it, it can also be eaten with a spoon, as a cold soup. 

For vegans, this can be made with a thick, unsweetened vegan yogurt.
@Jojo Forgot the MOST delicious way for me to eat this: on top of freshly cooked potatoes (steamed whole, with skins on, peeled just before eating).
This was insanely salty. I added only two cups of Diamond Kosher Salt and that was too much. Suggest maybe 1+3/4 cup of DKS. You can use the leftover Diamond Kosher Salt on your margarita rim.
Delish! I used whole milk cultured buttermilk instead of yogurt-this is a keeper!

Use 2 lemons for zest and juice
Add more salt
Served myself in large leaves of red leaf lettuce
Im not sure why anyone needed to change anything about this recipe-its perfect as is. Delicious. Made exactly as written and terrific on a humid, hot day.
Tastes best if you peel the cucumbers first. Takes away bitter skin taste (and possible wax).

For me no lemon zest & 1/4 of lemon juiced. 
Serve w chick peas or grilled meat.
Excelent. Served with Thai salmon cakes.

I dont love cucumbers, but had a huge harvest this year, so made this in an effort to add to our dinner.  I have learned it is always important to salt the cucumbers first and let them drain...for at least an hour.  Added mint from the garden, didnt have chilis so relied on the ground cayenne for a little added spice.  Turned out really good and is an easy accompaniment to many meals.
A fresh and delicious lunch option. I will definitely be adding it to a rotation and will experiment pairing it with different well spiced proteins!

I made this to go with lamb meatballs.  I admit I cheated by using dried herbs rather than fresh, and a scallion instead of a purple onion.  It turned out very well.  Half a batch was plenty for the two of us.
This yields A LOT. I could have quartered the recipe and had enough as a side for my family of three

title: Tomato-Feta Salad Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I make this and add cooked quinoa to the salad
I like this with chunks of avocado as well.

Everyone raved about the salad. We served it with grillled hamburgers and corn on the cob. I prepared it two hours in advance and I believe the extra time allowed the flavors to fully develop. We’re making it again for a Memorial Day barbecue.
Would you prepare the tomatoes/olives 2 hours in advance or the whole thing?

What is better than reading a great recipe and realizing you have all of the ingredients handy?  Have made this 5 times since discovering the recipe.  And I never knew that you could chop the parsley and cilantro stems along with the leaves - makes for a much shorter prep than hassling with the herbs.  Once you try the recipe as written, add some other veggies (avocado suggested by others; thinly sliced red onion, etc).  Marinating the tomatoes brings out their flavor.

Halved the feta, reduced tomatoes by one-third, double the arugula. Substituted fresh basil for cilantro. Followed the advice of others and added chunked avocado. Fantastic summer salad. Made a dinner of it.
I add farro to this salad, making it a bit more substantial.
This was made as directed and was so tasty! I can see making more additions would be a great idea.
Just great. Half a recipe makes a great lunch for two (3 oz feta was plenty...)

Dill instead of cilantro. Avoid canned or jarred olives if you can because of their taste and sometimes waterlogged texture. You don’t need the best feta to make a salad but you should never use the worst.
Loved this. Didn’t have good olives, but added cucumber chunks.  The dressing was great. Next time I’d add some avocados as well.  This would work nicely with heirloom tomatoes as well.
I halved the tomatoes and the dressing but kept the olive amount the same. Skipped the cilantro and used only parsley--so fresh!
Can it be made the night before
Great salad. Added farro and served over lettuce and topped with grilled chicken. Subbed garlic for garlic scapes for a lighter garlic taste.

Really nice salad. Cut recipe down for just the two of us. Used diced local tomatoes.  Made two hours ahead and added herbs, arugula and feta at the end. Used black and green olives, and mint instead of cilantro because I didn’t have any.  Served with David Tannis’s seared scallops with peppercorns, and squid ink pasta we bought in Italy. Delicious dinner😋

Excellent salad, very easy and quick to make. Wonderful flavor.
Serve with grilled hamburgers or other meat, and toasted bread.
Served on lettuce bed. Add avocado to the lettuce or the tomatoes.

Pretty darned good cherry tomatoes are available all year long from some growers (Sunset for example) so while this is best with local farmers market grown or cherry tomatoes that are garden grown, it is still a delicious salad in off season months and so easy to make.  Good feta is key, of course, not the pre-crumbled cow's milk stuff.  We like Valbreso (French - sheep) or Mt. Vikos (Greek - sheep & goat).
Made this and added chickpeas! Delicious!
Delicious. I typically am not a fan of cilantro, but it worked beautifully here. I made it as described and added a few cucumbers. Looking forward to making it again.
quick and tasty. a keeper!

Delicious - didn't use the feta because my 13 year old doesn't like it and he was the one making it (rule in our house is if you want to modify a recipe, you have to be the cook). Best a few hours after making. The next day it was still good, but we had to put it in the fridge and the tomatoes just weren't as tasty.
I made this and it came out out very good! I added orecchiette to make it a bit heartier and it was delicious.  If you do that- make sure to increase the vinaigrette to account for the added pasta!
My boyfriend and I loved this recipe, he kept going back for more helpings. The only change I made was no olives and parsley, I don’t think they’re required for this to be yummy!

title: Sesame-Coconut Shortbreads Recipe
author: David Tanis

stats.Total Time: 45 minutes, plus chilling and cooling time
stats.Cook Time: 35 minutes, plus chilling and cooling time

comments.commentBody: I wanted a dairy free cookie so I replace butter with canola oil. You need a little extra oil or a little less flour to make this work. I dusted the cookies with combination of powdered sugar and cocoa powder. They were a big success.
Not sure what happened, but my cookies grew, flattened out completely, and formed a single cookie that filled the pan and that is the thickness of a cracker.
Can you substitute sweetened coconut if you cut back on the sugar?
I used the same amount of sweetened coconut and thought they were very tasty.
Skip the fridge and pat dough directly into a 9 x 13 pan. Bake for 20 minutes. Score bars. Cool completely before removing. Five times to potlucks and seven requests for the recipe. A winner!

I subbed King Arthur cup for cup gluten free flour (by weight), and everyone really loves them. They're not light or delicate, and not sure they would be with glutenous flour. My guess is they wouldn't be due to the tahini and mixing procedure. Will be curious to read "legit" reviews.

Wow. Sesame and shortbread are 2 of my favorite things, and together they are fantastic. Coconut is also a great flavor although I only had flaked unsweetened, which I put in the chopper in an attempt to get it finely grated, which worked somewhat. This recipe is a total keeper.  Make sure that you refrigerate the logrolls for a few hours, as the recipe specifies.

Hi NYT cooks -Any suggestions on how to keep the cookies round-shaped given the softness of the dough? Mine flattened out in the fridge and I'd love them to look like the picture that accompanies the recipe. Thanks in advance!

@Sharon T hi! When you refrigerate the cookie logs, set them in empty paper towel cardboard cores (aka paper tubes). Cut the tubes open lengthwise to create cradles for the logs. Works well! Or try making flat edges with the dough for square cookies.

Really delicious!  Not too sweet and the tahini sesame flavor is really good.  I reduced sugar to a 1/2 cup, and substituted flour + cornstarch (1T per cup) for cake flour, and they turned out lovely, lots of positive feedback.  Brought them as gifts as they are easy to make and transport.  Thank you!!!
These were very good.  Reduced sugar by 1/3 and after leaving dough overnight in fridge texture did not seem impacted.  Will make again.
So I'm assuming are these to be cold when they go into the oven? Straight from the fridge (sliced) to the oven?
Seems like a lot of sweetener for something labeled as not-too-sweet. May try it with less. I like baked goods with tahini.

These didn’t work at all for me, and I followed the recipe as written. Since some folks had success and others didn’t, I wonder if the tahini makes a difference? Mine were quite oily and spread a lot (the flavor was nice, though).

Made the cookie dough, put in basement fridge, got a cold and wasn’t up and around for 3 days—during which forgot all about cookie dough—noticed it while packing fridge with Passover supplies, sliced and baked. Perfect! They look just like the photo. Strong tahini taste not for everyone, but they are a true shortbread texture and delicious.
Can I substitute peanut butter for tahini? It will change the taste but tahini is not  available at my local grocer.
Has anyone tried these using coconut flour? I have a lot to use up!

Coconut flour absorbs much, much more liquid than cake or all-purpose flour, so you can't replace one with the other--but I think that King Arthur Baking has a good recipe for coconut-flour shortbread that you might enjoy!

Another winner from the legendary David Tanis. Cooked as per the recipe with the exception of adding chopped Bronte pistachios that I has left. Went the full 13 minutes in my oven. If you are not a seasoned baker, handle carefully when moving from the sheet pan to the cooking rack. Not to worry, the cookies will come together when cooled.
Great cookies, to make them vegan, I subbed with Miyoko's butter and maple syrup (a little less than the amount of honey the recipe called for) and these were perfect!
I didnt have tahini so I used cashew butter - delicious!
replaced the melted butter with browned butter that I infused with coconut flakes while browning, 1000/10!!!
I can't get tahini where I live - has anyone tried this with peanut butter?
Order it online! Worth it if you are a sesame fan- you can use for easy salad dressings or dips...or another batch of these cookies (store tahini in the fridge once opened).
Yup.  Big winner.  Everyone loves them even my non-dessert people
I loved these. I added zest of one lime to give cookies a bit of brightness. I’m keeping an extra roll in the freezer to use when I need a last minute batch of cookies

Yum! These cookies had all of my favorite flavors except for almonds.  Next time I'll add some almond flour.  The dough was quite soft so I sprinkled coconut flour on it in order to work it and roll it into a log.  Also I poured off the jar's sesame oil and spooned in the thicker/dryer tahini so the dough didn't get too wet. I wonder how these would work if spread in a pan and cut after baking...
Yummy but so so so sweet. Next time I would reduce the sugar and add more coconut.
I wonder about replacing some of the butter (2-4 TBS) with unrefined coconut oil.  I’m always looking for ways to enhance the coconut flavor. Any tho’ts?

Mine didn’t look like the picture (they spread out to thin little cookies) but were still delightful. 
Next time I’ll probably try adding some cardamom and pepper.
Can you use desiccated coconut?  Thanks!
Is the tahini supposed to be grounded or creamy pureé?

Tasty unique flavor combination. I will make again and bake longer at a lower temperature, my cookies were chewier in the center than a true shortbread. And maybe a tad less honey and sugar.
Love the sesame and coconut flavor combination.
Scrumptious. Unusual. We served them at a party and they were a big hit.

title: Lamb Shoulder With Greens and Beans Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: I have an alternative method for cooking down greens. I prep them per the recipe wash & drain well, then zap them in the microwave, 2-3 minutes based on volume.  I drain them well, squeezing out excess liquid if called for, then sauté them briefly with aromatics per the recipe. This way not only can partial prep be done in advance, but the aromatics are not diluted & thrown out with excess liquid in the greens.
At what point do we put the beans in? We are told in step five to return the greens and beans to the skillet, but we have not been told when to put the beans in there in the first place.
It seems fava beans are already precooked as the ingredients list calls for already blanched and shelled fava. And you would add them in step 5.

The ingredients list calls for “shucked, blanched and peeled” fava beans, so they’ve been delt with.  Perhaps step five could have said “return greens and add beans”, but it’s clear this is what to do with the beans.

Used this to riff a little bit. Swapped in great white beans for the favas. Made a hash from the stems of the chard, a little bit of fresh fennel, and some brown onion. Put a scoop of it under the lamb shoulder when plating and garnished with fresh fennel fronds. It was a HUGE hit!

Delicious.  Had butcher remove as much fat as possible. I used lacinato kale, which did not leave any liquid to be drained.  I used more than a pound because it cooks down so much.  Also used mixture of peas and edamame instead of fava, which I couldn't find.  That worked well too.  I put them in when reheating the greens before plating.  Served with mashed sweet potatoes and garlic parmesan roasted asparagus.  Good combo.  Went well with a nice Barolo.
Great idea about sweet potato puree instead of the  usual mashed white potatoes
Tasty enough but underwhelming (to us)....I wonder if searing the lamb first would have helped?
I should say... Pan-seared the lamb before cooking and was glad I did.
Made this as directed.  It's fantastic.

Made this for Easter dinner with boneless leg of lamb because that's what my store had. I did sear the meat very briefly at the start because I just couldn't resist. I covered the meat closely with baking parchment (crumpled & smoothed) before putting the lid on. Baked at 325 for first hour+, then at 250 for the rest of the 2 hours. Everybody loved it and all our guests wanted leftovers to take home. Peeling the favas and rinsing the greens (chard & Tuscan kale) took the most time.

I'm surprised there are so few comments here. This is an amazing dish and easy, although time-consuming if you use fresh fava beans, which truly make this a spectacular meal. I used a combination of frozen and fresh, but I wouldn't substitute edamame or anything else. Fava beans are so special in spring.

This was really good. I can see having this again next Easter (or any time we eat lamb).

I thought the broth would be clear, but it's actually tomato-y (well, duh). We opted for a mix of spinach and chard with fava beans.

Really - I'm impressed with how well this came out. I don't tend to rate 5s (on anything), but this is clearly a five.
I also made this with kale, edamame, peas: so good! And served with a side of cheesy, scalloped potatoes. Will definitely make this again.

Extraordinary. Divine. Had to use shelling peas because my usual fava stockist was out. Will make again when my own favas come in. Served with purple sweet potatoes and asparagus. Most beautiful plate I've ever made and absolutely scrumptious.

title: Lemon Cake With Strawberries and Cream Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: @Janet and @ Confused, sponge cakes do not incorporate butter or oil into the recipe.  The fat comes from the eggs, the lightness of the cake comes from beating air into the batter.

The directions are indeed confusing. If the tablespoon of butter is only for greasing the pan, that should be noted. That said, fatless sponge cakes are a thing, and it would appear that this is one of them.
It IS noted. It's the very first thing in the instructions. I'm not sure why everyone is so confused??
Never see butter for pan listed as an ingredient hence confused.
1:1 gluten-free flour worked perfectly. Delicious.
Thanks for letting us GF bakers know this was a success!
1 T. of butter? That's the only fat in the cake?
It's a sponge cake.  They do not contain fat other than what is in the egg yolks (and angel food cake does not contain even that).  There is cream in the glaze though!
Can I use all purpose flour if I don't have cake flour?
They're not the same, but you can add a little cornstarch to the A-P flour as a substitute. Search internet for correct ratios.
Is this recipe accurate? There is no fat in the batter? And no indication of what to do with the 1tablespoon of butter. Something seems off
@Jennifer Caravaggio in step 1 it says to butter and flour the cake pan.

I recommend folding the flour into the egg mixture rather than the other way around. I poured the egg mixture into the flour and it immediately formed lumps. Is there any reason not to fold the flour into the egg mixture instead?
My cake rose to the rim of the pan. However, it fell when cooling. What happened?

made this as I’m always on a hunt for a quick dessert for a potluck. Quite good. I think the next time, will do 1/4 t lemon extract and 1/4 t vanilla to up the lemon flavor. Over-folded when adding the flour which made the sponge a little tough. needed more than 8 oz of strawberries! 

It’s a good, easy recipe which can be pulled together, start to serving, in a little over 3 hrs. Perfect for those “can you bring a dessert?” invites!

This was heavenly, both times I made it! My chef daughter couldn’t believe I made it until she saw the whipped cream on the side. I folded the flour into the egg mixture and I used Grande Marnier instead of limoncello, cuz that’s what I have! Again, delicioso!

Huge disappointment! Followed the instructions to a T but while the final cake looked like the photo it had a rubbery consistency and absolutely no lemon flavor even though I added extra lemon zest and topped with the glaze. Wonder if the egg whites and sugar were beaten separately then add the yolks would make for a better texture? Agree with other comments about folding flour into the liquid instead of the other way around.
@Janet The fat for the cake is in the egg yolks. Plus more fat in the whipped cream topping.

I suspect this recipe is meant to list egg whites instead of eggs or add the yolks later, but there may be some other issues too… I’m a proficient baker and cook and I followed the recipe exactly. The cake had a huge puff in the center and then fell to about 1in thick all around as it cooled. I would not make this again or recommend it unless the recipe is revised.
not a good recipe. avoid.
This regular sponge cake recipe gets better reviews: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1022270-sponge-cake I wonder if it'd work to add some lemon flavor to it.
Is the tablespoon of butter to be used for coating the pan? It is not mentioned in the step by step instructions. So there is no fat in this cake?
It's the very first thing in the step by step instructions.
Are the eggs supposed to be separated?  They are not thickening

I made this cake on a whim, as another lemon cake recipe failed completely. So all of a sudden I quickly needed a substitute recipe, one that was easy and could be done in less than an hour. I found this cake, made it and it was a huge success. The trick is in the preparation of the dough, I beat the eggs and sugar until it was light and ribbon like then folded in the flour. Easy and done in a few minutes. The result was a a light, fluffy cake that was delicious.

Ugh - baked this twice and both cratered - maybe my oven setting? I set my Wolf Oven for Angel Food cake - first attempt was in Wolf Convection Steam oven and that cratered far worse! Should I start with cold eggs rather than room temperature eggs?
Has anyone made this with a substitute for limoncello? We are reticent to buy a whole bottle.
I followed directions exactly and it fell when I removed it. I returned to oven and it rose back up but after another 10 minutes, it fell again. I baked it 30 minutes total. A complete fail. Bummer!
Still the best!!

Light, fresh, and stress free.
This was my first baking experience, and it was hassle-free. I cannot stress how light this cake felt. I cut it into 8 generous helpings, and it felt weightless - I feel like this is hard to do with a cake/dessert.

It's on the list of repeat making!
@JR cake was undercooked in the middle. Eggs are temperamental - see cheesecake cracks, and they don't like going from a super hot oven to a cold room!
Disaster!  Cake rose and overflowed spring form pan in the oven.  Came out cooked/browned on the sides and raw/soupy in the middle.  Total disaster.

I just relegated this cake to the garbage.  Thank goodness that I made it a day ahead.  Looked a bit counterintuitive but seemed quick and easy.  First, after 15 minutes whisking the mixture was thick but not holding peaks.  I forged  on nonetheless.  The next counterintuitive part: the recipe says to put the sifted flour in a large bowl and fold the liquid mixture into the dry ingredient. Despite my misgivings I went ahead.  The result was an unservable cake with a solid layer in the bottom.

"15 minutes whisking"

Did you try and whisk this by hand for 15 solid minutes?? Because the directions clearly say to use a stand mixer.  Regardless, you then forged ahead, even though the wet ingredients were not mixed correctly and got an "unservable cake." That sounds like a user error, not a flaw in the recipe.

Same here. Among the worst failures in 20+ years of baking. The eggs never formed peaks (using a stand mixer). A traditional sponge cake whips just the egg whites and folds them into an egg yolk/sugar/flour mixture. And, folding the liquid into the flour--the flour was not absorbed fully, leaving pockets of flour in the final product, which as Julie said above, was mostly a solid rubbery layer. 
I weighed dry ingredients and sifted the flour.

To SL: I used a stand mixer.  Since I hadn't made the cake before, I ignored my instincts,  followed directions and didn't have a good result.  It happens.  However, I thought that others should know.
A hit! Lots of compliments from the family. I used AP flour which worked fine.  I had to make more whipped cream, the recipe amount felt un-generous.

There was too much batter for the 9" springform pan. The batter rose up over the top and cascaded down to the cake strips, which stopped the flow. Next time (and there will be a next time), I'll use the 10". Very light and airy. Almost too light. I'm thinking I took the "almost to peaks' approach too far, as the batter definitely held its shape. There was no "slowly dissolving ribbon", to quote Julia Child. It baked to a clean skewer in 20 minutes.

Has anyone made it without the frosting?
I’m thinking of just making the lemon cake with the strawberries.

It was a good cake-sponge cake-  It’s not exciting considering you have lemon juice and zest and strawberries . If you’re looking for something simple and sugary- this recipe will meet that. If you’re looking for delicious, owey gooey this isn’t going to meet those needs.

yikes, followed recipe to the letter,  toothpick came out clean , but cooled cake was a wet mess in the center.... flavor fine.... IF I EVER tried this recipe again I'd make it in a bunt pant, but doubt I'll try it ever again
Mine turned out dry.  Not happy.  I love this idea but I think there must be a better recipe out there

I would recommend not making this recipe. I followed this recipe to a T and it didn't turn out. The cake was underbaked (left it in the 25 mins), sticky and weird texture on the bits that were fully baked, not good. Huge disappointment after buying a specialty ingredient (cake flour) and spending an hour or so on it. Should've just done the shot of limoncello instead...

Made this recipe for a group of friends - the red, white and blue colors were perfect for a July 4 celebration. That said, I found the instructions to be perfectly clear - no butter in the recipe! I do think I’m not a big fan of sponge cake as I found it a bit dry. Having the whipped cream and berries helped but probably wouldn’t make again.
Disappointing. Flat as a pancake using Bob’s 1:1 GF. Rubbery
Do you mean 1 single Tbsp butter for the cake???
This did not turn out. I did it twice. It definitely needs some improved instructions. I was really disappointed. It looked so good.
Always love a sponge cake but this one was too dry!
Agree with other comments a out this being a disaster.  Made it for mother’s day. Went in the garbage.  Baked as directed. A gummy, tasteless glob that someone bas masqueraded as a cake.
Similar to the other comments, this was a terrible cake that went in the garbage.  Experienced baker, followed recipe. The texture was gummy, no flavor.  Why this has 4* I do not know.
It is in the oven, so fingers are crossed. But this method is called a Génoise sponge, for those like me who are curious about finding more tips on this (at least in the US) uncommon technique.

title: Herby Asparagus Salad With Beets and Prosciutto Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: Kaedub, juicy raw beets will rock your world! Try coarsely shredding peeled raw beets on a box grater (doing this in the sink reduces staining, an apron or an old shirt is wise), sprinkle with salt and sugar, squeeze over some citrus juice (or vinegar) and finish with olive oil and pepper. Same goes for other root vegs like carrots. Then riff with toppings like toasted nuts, herbs, runny tahini, julienned apples, garlicky croutons...Wakes up the palate and plate.
Pardon my ignorance, but I've never heard of using raw beets for anything! Aren't beets always cooked before you use them?

Raw beets?! oh go go go for them - superb in finely grated carrot and red beet salad, add sultanas, mixed seeds, parsley, and squish of oil and lots of lemon juice! yum! it keeps well and is divine.  if you fancy adding finely grated cheddar as an optional extra, believe me, it's one of the most moreish things for lunch, for snacking, for whenever you feel the munchies coming along

@Kaedub

Classic French beet salad is raw beets, tossed with vinaigrette.  Nothing new about it :))
@jiffyy but these are julienned. Why not just grate them and for ease use a food processor grating blade

A great refreshing salad the celebrates Spring.  I used roasted red beets and like the way the vinaigrette used on the beets turned it a pretty pink. Good visual presentation when further dressed with the base vinaigrette. The herb medley really makes it shine.

This is my first time reviewing a recipe after years and many wonderful dishes with NYT.  THIS IS THE BEST!   Please try the raw beets!  I didn’t have dill or mint but I did have chives so just did a mix of the herbs I had.  It was the perfect light dinner.

My significant other considered this one worth six stars; but, perhaps, that's because she 
loves asparagus & beets.
Yes, beets can indeed be eaten raw, but are best prepared with very thin slices

Made this for dinner last night ... delish!  I loved how all of the flavours worked so well together.  However, I think my preference would be to use roasted beets in the future.  I think the dressing would work better with roasted beets.  This is now in seasonal rotation.

My beets were roasted. I substituted ham from a famous national store. I did not have any of the fresh herbs to sprinkle on top.   And yet this was absolutely delicious and so fresh tasting.  Definitely a must to make again.
Never had raw beets before.  I shredded them on a box cutter - easy and absolutely delicious dish!  The herbs take it to another level, will be in my spring weekday rotation.

I have always considered the Nicoise to be the perfect salad, but this one truly gives it a run for its money, especially here in Wa state, where we have divine asparagus in the spring. I'd never heard of using raw beets, but they're just amazing...I may never cook a beet again. Thank you chefs and commenters; I used to subscribe to Gourmet, Bon Appetit, Saveur, Food & Wine, and Cook's Illustrated, but now about 90% of what I prepare comes from this website!
Even better the second day! First time using raw beets and this did not disappoint. Herb medley really makes it feel bright and fresh.

This was a joy to eat!
I used roasted red and golden beets. The sweetness of the golden was so complementary. Will definitely make again.
My family doesn't care for beets so I use an avocado instead. Very good and easy.
I used mango powder (amchor) instead of mustard which also added a nice tang. I blanched the beets; warm they take up the vinagrette.

I wanted to make this as a side rather than as a main and so I left off the prosciutto - it was still excellent. Super flavorful, fresh, and satisfying, and absolutely gorgeous on the plate. I'm sure with the prosciutto it's even better, but this makes a beautiful side dish as well. Other than the one big subtraction of the prosciutto, I made it as is and wouldn't change a thing. Guests raved.

BIG win! Guests and husband loved it. I used my OXO mandolin to julienne the beets, so easy! Served it with  NYT Mustard-Glazed Pork Tenderloin. I was surprised but no one missed a carb-based side dish. I'll definitely do this again.
The really is the perfect dinner party salad: delicious, beautiful and different. Everyone loved it.

This is a nice salad even though I made a rather significant change. I made pickled beets and eggs a couple weeks ago and wanted to use the last of them, so I used them in this salad. It was good, but different than intended. I will make again as written, but won’t rule out using pickled beets and egg again either. We liked it.

title: Cucumber-Cabbage Salad With Sesame Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: Christa, “lightly peeled” means, simply, that you don’t dig into the flesh of the cucumber when peeling. Just skim — “lightly” — the cuke with your peeler, leaving some strips of peel on the fruit. A hothouse cucumber has a tender skin that is usually eaten, while a field grown cucumber has something more akin to a soft rind, which is usually removed. In the photo, you can see that the cuke slices in the salad retain some rind. It adds to the bright color of the dish.

Made it exactly as written (except I didn't use dill - didn't think it would go with the flavor profile) and it was perfect. Served with peanut-satay style chicken on the side. Will definitely make again!
What in the world does "lightly peeled* mean? Are you kidding me?
No, I dont think anyone is kidding you. I am am sure what is meant is alternatively peeled stripes as can be seen from the picture. It's for looks, not necessary w/hothouse cucumbers.
@Gale you can also run the tines of a fork down the cukes!
Wonderful dish!  Followed the recipe to a T, and wouldn’t change a thing.

This was excellent. Fresh and crunchy - used regular green cabbage, finely shredded. I think it would work very well with any combination of crunchy veggies, such as radishes, purple cabbage, kohlrabi, jicama, daikon, even parsnips. Will definitely make again!
Great salad, but with Napa cabbage it became wilted very quickly and not very appetizing for leftovers.  Next time I will try with regular cabbage to hopefully keep the crunch a bit longer.
I just made this and it is delicious.  No changes to the recipe and no notes.  Five stars.

Delicious and oh, so easy. A bright and lovely salad to whip up (even you've forgotten the cilantro, which I did, but would be certain to include next time). Napa cabbage, without a doubt, for this salad!
This salad is crazy delicious. I love everything single thing about it and the dressing is drinkable. Doubling the dressing next time to keep it handy. A big, BIG home run!
Allergic to fish sauce - what can I use instead?
There are vegan fish sauces made with liquid aminos or seaweed or you could consider soy sauce.

@leigh
Worcestershire sauce (just a teaspoon or less) or anchovy paste mixed in a little white wine will sub reasonably well. My husband dislikes fish sauce, so I used these substitutions.
SO GOOD. I added Thai basil and Fresno chilis for color and it was 🔥
This is a godly salad. The crunch, the vibrancy, it is perfection. I would eat it every single day if I could figure out a way to make it in bulk and preserve the crunch.
@Bianca maybe keep the dressing on the side and add it when you’re ready to eat? That should help. The cucumbers would still get soggy though, you could leave them out.
2 tablespoons seems like a lot of sugar—is this dressing as sweet as it sounds?
2tbsp sugar to 5tbsp liquids is a reasonable ratio IMHO
@Sissy  It doesn't have to be SO sweet. Trade brown sugar for a splash of My Keto or pomegranate molasses. The traditional profile needs some sweet to balance the heat & tang of other ingred's.

I see that this recipe has five stars, so I guess I’m an outlier.  My husband and I didn’t love this salad - it was OK, but the association of the herbs and flavors in the dressing created a somewhat bitter taste with a distinct anis-like flavor (not one we like). I won’t bother experimenting with the ingredients because it requires too many ingredients to buy/have on hand for the result.
Delicious! I added Sambal and crushed pistachios. Paired with thai marinated pork. Perfect :)
I made this as written with pre-shredded cabbage  and pre-shredded carrots. Then used mini cucumbers. It’s such a great salad that comes together easily. Really delish!
What can I substitute for fish sauce?
Some have suggested worchester sauce. It replaces the umami of the fish sauce. Thankfully.

@Carol Weinstein
Try soy sauce
FYI--most Worcestershire sauces have anchovies (including Lea & Perrins), so if you are trying to make it vegan, choose your brand carefully or perhaps try soy sauce or tamari.

@TimsS-
When making a salad like this, I cut up all of the greens/ veggies, but only dress the amount needed for the meal. The remaining cut up veggies usually store well in a container or plastic bag and can be dressed just prior to serving.
Delicious and will definitely make again. Omitted the dill.
You can use Persian cucumbers and skip peeling them.

Very good and easy to prepare salad. I added leftover grilled chicken and some bell peppers. I think it would be good with the addition or scallions. The dressing will be great on so many other things!
@Elizajane1234 ...BTW a Tbl/tablespoon is the equivalent of 3 tsp/teaspoons; so you used 2 tsp of fish sauce. Now you know.

Double recipe loved by the 10 folks for bottling, but not enough.  Need a full large head of green cabbage and a small red with 2.5 times the dressing next time.  The ginger + pepper + garlic + dill gave it just the right kick to go with the brown sugar.  Will make again.
I didn’t have lime juice or fish sauce on hand, and we live fairly rural, so I subbed rice vinegar and tamari. Those seem to be pretty acceptable alternatives (for us, anyway). Also added basil.

This was fantastic! It went perfectly with grilled meat and even people unfamiliar with fish sauce loved it.  I typically am one who reduces sugar in recipes, but I didn't this time - I felt like it was necessary to balance out the fish sauce and the lime juice. The dressing had enough tang to make it pleasant, but not so much it tasted sour or fermented.  Next time round, I'll add more heat to the dressing as that suits our tastes. Great recipe - thank you!
For either kind of cabbage always rinse in colander then sprinkle with salt and let sit for an hour or so on a towel or paper towels. Rinse. It will be crispy and stay crispy longer that way.
this was excellent. wouldn't change a thing. I added shrimp on the side. *chef's kiss so good!
Wow, but how does it taste.

Very nice salad. Would be good with shredded chicken or other protein added. I put half of a Serrano pepper in the dressing and it’s a little hotter than I like. I also used about 2/3 T of fish sauce instead of a full tablespoon.
Made it according to the recipe and it was perfect! Regular cabbage was fine.

This salad is a nice template.  Delicious as written, or sub walnut oil for the sesame, walnuts for the sesame seeds, skip the ginger and fish sauce, use Tuscan or Provencal herb combinations, and voila.  Whole new lovely ballgame.

We live in a town that has a plethora of Asian grocery stores, so I was able to procure everything for this recipe but cilantro and dill, which I picked up at the regular supermarket. I have never used Napa cabbage and I am so glad I did.  It's so much more delicate than green cabbage. 
Followed recipe exactly and it was outstanding. I took it to a dinner at friends' and it was the tastiest thing on the table. 
Glad we loved it as I will be making it again this week to finish off the vegetables and herbs! Will follow others' advice to double the dressing and keep it on hand. It will be a great side salad for salmon or grilled chicken.
Delicious served alongside a salmon filet marinated in similar flavors. Had no cucumbers so subbed shredded kale, and also lacked cilantro. Still 5 stars, absolutely!

The dressing is delicious! I omitted the dill and added spring onions instead as I thought if fits the flavour profile better. Love the mint leaves in this. So fresh! Did not have sesame seeds, so added roasted peanuts for the crunch. My whole family loved it!

This is very delicious and I will make it again for sure.. I made it with napa cabbage and next time I will change up how I put it together. The instructions have you add the cabbage, carrots and cukes to the dressing and just before serving adding the herbs, but the dressing wilts the napa cabbage almost instantly. I don’t know if regular cabbage wilts as readily. So next time I will put it together just before serving. I will also add an additional cup of cabbage if using a napa head and I’ll play it be ear if using green cabbage.
Excellent!!

The components and dressing are very similar to the Vietnamese cabbage salad I've been making for years from Mai Pham's Southeast Asian cookbook.  Will be trying this version tomorrow with hoisin-marinated salmon for dinner.  In lieu of Napa cabbage, I like to use Trader Joe's Cruciferous Crunch and shredded carrots, and a tsp or so of Thai chili garlic sauce for the heat.  I always have a bottle of organic lime juice and Red Boat fish sauce in the fridge - looking forward to this.
I dislike this intensely.  A lot of prep and to end up with this soggy mess with an unpleasant taste just depressed me.

@Sook I ended up with a soggy salad too. I wondered about adding salt to the veggies, which draws water out of them, yet there was no mention of pouring off the water or straining them!? I’ll try it again but will salt only the cucumbers, then rinse and drain them before adding to the other vegetables and dressing the salad.

Didn’t add fish sauce or jalapeño - for picky eaters at my table. Missed the umami, but the flavors mixed just fine without it and the brightness of lime softened after a little time melding with the veg. The herbs are fantastic!
Made this with Persian cukes, no carrots and Meyer lemon juice instead of lime juice.  Served with Kua Kling.
Delicious! I made it to eat with the other ny times recipe: Ground Turkey, Shiitake and Cashew Lettuce  a great pairing!
Was out of sesame seeds so I used chopped peanuts - they were perfect!

title: Spiced Chicken Breasts in Pastry Recipe
author: David Tanis

comments.commentBody: Sounds wonderful! But how can you be sure the chicken is done? Recipe says till "chicken is firm" -- but can you tell if it is as it's wrapped in pastry? Maybe insert an instant read from one end to be sure. s/b 165
@Paulette You pound the chicken out to a decent thickness. Baked in a 400 degree oven I have never had a problem. They need to rest for a good five minutes as well.
The diagonal slits in the pastry allow you to check if the chicken is firm.
How thick should the pastry be when it’s rolled out?
Says 1/2 inch
Since the recipe specifies that you cut the pastry into four 7x7 inch squares, you'd want to roll it out enough to form a rough square that's at least 14x14.
Thick enough to get four 7x7 squares

The idea: moist chicken surrounded by pastry.  Years ago, I created  an analogue with far less work. Slice microwaved  seasoned breasts  into 1/4 - 1/3 slices.  Cut an oblong sourdough loaf x-wise alternately into 80% & nearly complete 1/4-1/3" thick slices. Slather insides of 80% cuts w. pesto, put chick slice in. Dribble a little melted butter over the loaf. Bake at 350 for 30 minutes. Guests pull apart ready made moist pesto chicken sandwiches! Now, sub pesto w Dijon, onion, cream, 5-spice...
